metadata.historical_context: John is serving in Korea during the Korean War (1950-1953), stationed with Co. C, 439th Engineer Combat Battalion, APO 301. Bob references a newspaper report of Communist guerrillas ambushing 5 or 6 GIs outside Pusan and killing 2 or 3 of them. Bob also expresses frustration about the draft board deferring married 21-year-olds while John remains overseas. The letter is dated May 1952, during the armistice negotiations period of the Korean War. Reference to the Indianapolis 500 on Decoration Day (Memorial Day) 1952, with Chet Miller having the fastest trial time of 139.00 mph.

pages.transcription:
May 27, 1952

Dear John

Not been doing to much Lately. I wen fishing at the Local lake here in Dallas and got a 6 ounce perch. Not like the time you and me + Dad went to the Crow Wings in Minnesota. The race at Indianapolis should be real good this year. the fasest time trial was 139.00 by Chet Miller I would sure like to see them some time. I got a Card from Dad and he is in Canada trying to get a bigger fish to hang on the wall at the shop.

Say the paper say the Communist gurella ambushed about 5 or 6 G.I. just outside of Pusan and killed two or three of them. So I think you had better the gun and keep a sharp lookout for those rotten bastard. I think I know why you have to stay over there so long. These chicken shit draft board have deferred every body with a pimple on their butt. If I was you I would tell that draft board in Des Moines to kiss your ass. Every time I see these guys

in the reserve or National, on there 21 years old that are married and deferred I get pissed off. Jimmy says he has a nice 51 Ford for you to drive when you get home. You two are lucky for that. This old 46 Chrysler I got isn't worth a good shit. I hope you and Jim come down to see me when you get home. I will be home Christmas I hope. So you keep up the good work and try and beat the old man rank of Sergeant. Well John good by for now and I will send you the result of the big race
over

on Decoration day at Indianapolis

Love Your Brother

Bob

P.S. Keep your eyes open for those sneaking red bastards.

3614 Noble St
Dallas, Texas

letters.summary: This is a May 1952 letter from Bob (Robert Hanifen) in Dallas, Texas to his brother John, an Army corporal serving in Korea with the 439th Engineer Combat Battalion. Bob shares lighthearted family news — a meager fishing catch, their father's trip to Canada to fish, and excitement about the upcoming Indianapolis 500 — before turning serious about a newspaper report of Communist guerrillas ambushing GIs near Pusan and urging John to stay alert. Bob vents anger at the draft board system for deferring stateside civilians while John remains overseas, and closes with encouragement for John to achieve the rank of Sergeant, promising to send the race results from Indianapolis on Decoration Day.

metadata.historical_context: Written in February 1946, shortly after WWII ended. Reference to 'camp' suggests Jim Jr. may still be in military service or stationed somewhere. The date places this in the immediate post-war period.

pages.transcription:
5 Feb. 1946
Bethany, Mo.,
8:15 PM

Dear Pop,

Got to write, I don't know quite why but I know I have really screwed the works up.

It wouldn't be so bad, but I wanted to carry through a deal like this just to show you I could, but I didn't.

I'm a big kid now, but right now I feel like crying.

I drove [dove?] the damn thing right on 50, can't understand it.

If you don't do anything else desk [check?] that oil from maybe

they unscrewed the drain plug at Roy Gordon's.

I'll get back to camp on time but it won't mean a damn thing now.

Well good-bye for now Dad

I really very sorry

Jim

letters.summary: Written on February 5, 1946 from Bethany, Missouri, this is a heartfelt and remorseful letter from Jim Hanifen Jr. to his father James (Pop/Dad). Jim confesses he has 'screwed the works up' — apparently related to a car incident on Route 50, possibly losing oil after the drain plug was left loose at a place called Gordon's. Despite being grown, Jim admits he feels like crying, having failed to prove himself capable of handling something independently. He notes he will make it back to camp on time but feels it no longer matters, and closes with a sincere apology.

metadata.historical_context: Written March 22, 1946, shortly after the end of WWII. Bob appears to be part of the post-war occupation forces in Italy. He references the emerging 'Russian situation,' an early reference to Cold War tensions. The mention of inspections, jeeps, shooting practice, and a lieutenant suggest active military duty in a peacetime occupation role.

pages.transcription:
22 March, 1946

Dear Dad

    Well I am down at work and nobody is here yet. We finished our last inspection Tues. and so we are done for the month. The Lt. let me do the inspection without his help so I feel pretty good about it. I might go to Rome this week with a convoy just to see Rome, Leghorn [Livorno?] Pesa [Pisa?] and the big cities of North Italy. The jeep is still running good. Had to have a new starter motor but now it is okay. Was out shooting 45s and carbines last week just for the hell of it. Had a good old time. The Lt. is going to Switzerland some and coming back April 6, he wants me to do 3 inspections starting the first. You know maybe I will get a rank out of this office. I am sending home all my money it should be about $57 some dollars every month. As yet I have not received a letter addressed to my new address. The letter I got yesterday was dated Feb. 22. Pretty

long time. How about sending some 620 films. My buddie got a camera so I can take some pictures. Don't send Johnny [a?] camera as most of the stuff is not getting thru. I will get myself a camera thru Jack Buckley, Col. [?] boy friend pretty soon, so don't worry about me. How about sending me that picture of my buddie and me I had taken in New York. Have Pauline and them see if they can find those pictures of mine I left at home. This Russian situation don't look to good to me, as we are in the hot spot right here. I was playing catch with the Lt. yesterday, boy he sure is a swell guy. I will send home some souvenirs one of these days. But I got to get ahead on my money. I am learning Dego pretty good. At least I know what the score is.

Well Dad goodbye for now.

    Lots of Love Your Son
                Bobby

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS:
Robert E. Hanifen 12170600
3020 Ord & Mt. AF
APO 528
Postmaster New York, N.Y.

ADDRESSED TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

POSTMARK: [Circular military postmark, partially legible: 'POSTAL PAY... A.P.O. 51[?]']
STAMP: Free military frank / postal meter marks visible

4 March, 1946

Dear Dad

     Just got back from the Show. State Fair it sorta made me a little homesick to see the old fairgrounds again. We inspected the Battalion Motor Pool today it took about 1 hr. I drove about 6 different vehicles. a jeep, a 3/4 ton station wagon, a 2½ ton 6x6, a 4½ ton auto car, and a 10 ton prime mover, boy was it ever huge. Almost need a step ladder to get in it. I wrote up the report in about a hour, getting so I can do it pretty

fast now. We 20 more things to inspection this month. The Lt. is going on his vacation the last week in March. So we'll have to do it all 3 weeks.
We have two inspection in Bari one in Macfarlane [Macfarlane?]. The Lt. says we will probably fly down. There it is about 200 miles away. And the roads are pretty rough to drive. Should be a good trip. Sure hope I can get a rating of this job.
I met a pretty nice girl at the Red Cross in Caserta. Her mother was with her a pretty good sign in this country. So they tell.

II

Her name is Anna, she is really nice compared to this other woman mainly because she is clean, well clean anyway. All the boys think she is alright. I am not going to get married so don't worry. Just somebody to dance, remember the song.
Naples is off limits the 18 of March, to much V.D. says the Army. This country is all closed up. Don't work at all in the afternoon, just sit around and talk all day. Not a bad life at all. Sorta of wish there was more work

to do. All the boys are sitting here bulling about the girls back home. That all we do at night it seems, besides go to show. Well Dad goodbye for now. Lots of love

                              for now

                                        Bobby

[remainder of page is bleed-through from reverse side, illegible]

letters.summary: Written on March 4, 1946 from Italy, Bob (Bobby) Hanifen writes to his father about his military duties inspecting the Battalion Motor Pool, driving six different vehicles including a massive 10-ton prime mover. He mentions upcoming inspections in Bari and possibly Macfarlane (about 200 miles away) that may require flying. He casually mentions meeting a girl named Anna at the Red Cross in Caserta, reassuring his father he has no marriage plans, and notes that Naples has been placed off-limits due to high VD rates. The letter paints a picture of postwar occupation duty in Italy — relatively light work, lots of idle time, and soldiers longing for home.

pages.transcription:
[Return address, upper left:]
Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17170600
Sqd. T – B55-249
Chanute Fld. Ill.

[Postmark:] Rantoul, Ill. / JUL 14 / 11-PM / 1945

[Upper right:] Free

[Recipient address, center:]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
984 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

[Back of envelope contains penciled arithmetic calculations arranged in columns — appears to be numerical tallies or accounts, possibly done by the recipient (father). Numbers are partially legible and rotated; exact values unclear due to orientation and staining. Sample figures visible include: 5, 6, 8, 4, 3 / 3, 8, 8 / n/2- / 8/3- and lower section: 6, 2, 10, 6, 3, 16, 3, 13 / 2, 9, 0, 8, 5 / etc. Not fully transcribable due to rotation and damage.]

Thur. 9[:]00

Dear Dad
    I am in school right now and not doing much so I thought I would write a line or two. My teeth have been hurting like hell but I suppose I will get over it pretty soon I hope. They have cut our classes here again about a week. We starting work now in the shop learning how to solder, rivet, shape metal. 15 days of it. They cut it down 3 days. 4 weeks after that on aircraft repair the real McCay [McCoy?] and 4 weeks of structural repairs and that all. We are on a rush order they say. They need 5000 guys by the Jan. I guess. Hope we still get a 15 day delay en route. Probably won't though. I am going to Chicago this weekend if everything goes alright.

say I never got any letter from Aunt Violet yet. Wonder what happen to it. I might come home in about 2 weeks or so. It all depends on how things work out. I am starving to death can't eat very much. But I sure do try. Those B-29s we are big we start working on them in about 2 weeks. Boy that will be a lot of fun. Might see Jimmy one of these days on a Island. Never can tell. Hope business is still good. By the way how is the oil wells coming along. Good I hope. Well time to start working again..

Goodby for now.
            Love
                Bobby

letters.summary: Written in July 1945 from Chanute Field, Illinois, this letter from Bob (Bobby) Hanifen to his father describes his military technical training in aircraft repair — including soldering, riveting, and sheet metal work — and the compressed training schedule driven by wartime demand for 5,000 trained men. Bob mentions personal discomforts (a bad toothache, not eating well), plans for a Chicago weekend trip, the possibility of a 15-day leave en route home, and curiosity about the family business and an oil well. He also muses that he might see his brother Jimmy on a Pacific island, reflecting the wartime dispersal of the family.

pages.transcription:
27, May 1946

Dear Jimmy just got your letter today. Was very glad to hear from you. I am almost a Sgt. if I work it right. Having a pretty good time in Italy. These god damn M.P.'s are what. Causing us all the trouble overseas. Brig up last night for having a button no bottom. What a lot of shit. I am suppose to fly to Foggia tomorrow for an inspection. Good deal. Hope Dad get that car. I am sending home all the money they will let me. Good thing they got that money control. or I would sell my shoes to these Genie[?] bastards. I am having quite a time with the air [MJs?/MPs?] of them are

broad. But a lot of them won't even speak to an American. I found two babe who are pretty nice. They are Red Cross girls. Most of them are pretty nice. I am speaking of Italian girls in the Red Cross. These American Red Cross women give me a pain in the ass. Hope Dad get me a good car. Either a Mercury or Ford. So that Chev. is quite the car but you feel damn good driving it. Can't even get a jeep for recreational anymore. These god damn officers are living like kings over here. PW's and Italians do all the work.

Say hello to Annie [Connie?] for me.

Lots of love, Bob

Dear Jimmy just got your letter today. Was very glad to hear from you. I am almost a Sgt. if I work it right. Having a pretty good time in Italy. These god damn M.P.'s are what. letters.summary: Written on May 27, 1946, from Italy, Bob writes to his brother Jimmy with a casual, irreverent account of post-WWII military life. He mentions nearly making Sergeant, being put in the brig over a missing uniform button (courtesy of the MPs), and flying to Foggia for an inspection. He complains about money controls (while acknowledging they prevent him from spending foolishly), discusses his interest in Italian Red Cross girls versus American ones, and asks his father to buy a good car (Mercury or Ford rather than a Chevrolet). The letter closes with a note to say hello to 'Annie' (or possibly 'Connie') and is signed 'Lots of love, Bob,' giving a vivid glimpse into the frustrations and small pleasures of occupation-era military service in Italy.

metadata.historical_context: Letter references a ration book, suggesting WWII-era rationing. Military training context (MP Barracks, inspections, P.T., Military hour) indicates Bob is in a military training program, likely ASTP or similar college-based military program at University of Wyoming during WWII.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is written on University of Wyoming 'Cowboy Joe' letterhead from Laramie. References a ration book (WWII rationing), military training schedule (P.T., Military hour, inspections), and a girlfriend 20 miles out with chicken dinner. Letter 16 (dated July 13, 1944) from Dad to Jim Jr. explicitly mentions Bob's ASTP address at University of Wyoming, Laramie — 'He starts off at 6 AM + goes right thru until 10:30 PM.' This matches Bob's description in Letter 7 exactly. Letter 18a (also Wyoming, multi-day letter starting Monday) references Frontier Days in Cheyenne (held annually in late July) and a September 30–October 10 leave, also consistent with 1944. Letter 7 mentions 'tomorrow we have English first hour, Math 2 hours, 1 hr. of P.T., 3 hr of Physics Lab, 1 hr. of Military' — matches ASTP curriculum. Dated July 10, which in 1944 falls on a Monday; the letter reads as written mid-week given the next-day schedule described. Estimated July 1944.

pages.transcription:
July, 10

Dear Dad

     I have wrote a couple times telling you the clothes fit & the shoes. I haven't sold them as yet because the guy I was going to sell them to broke a bone in his foot & can't play any more. I will send them back in about a week if can't sell them. Say you can my shoes stunk in my [ration?] book. If they haven't turned them in, I wait till I hear from you before I do anything. Sorry about the cars, I say they are really getting rough. If you don't pass at the [first?] 12 week term you get sent home. But they say it isn't much of a change though. I wrote Edith & John and forgot to put a stamp on the letter, I was in a pretty big hurry and I forgot the stamp. I wish I knew sooner about Russ Wellman because I though[t] was

there last week in fact we slept there at the M.P. Barracks. I write more often but I don't get a lot of time, always having inspection, room & personal, you have to do it even if you have [the?] fuss. They really are strict. Slight delay. It is now 10:15 almost time for bed. We to eat chow had ear corn, dressing, potato, milk, cookies and roast beef it was really good. We had a basketball game at 7:00 it was a pretty good game I played about 2 quarters and boy I sure am tired. Thanks for buck and the 2 buck bill. I am going out to one of my girl friend house Sun. She lives about 20 miles out. She is going to have chicken for dinner. God help her if she don't. I have had chicken only 3 times since I left home than it would make a [person?] a good brother, it was little and it sure cost me. I got the D.D. [or?] Drizzly [gg?] from eating cantaloupe and I fell sort of low, but I am getting over it now. Well tomorrow we have English first hour, Math 2 hours, 1 hr. of P.T., 3 hr of Physics Lab, 1 hr. of Military and a rigid personal inspection so I had better get to bed. This high altitude makes you sleepy all the time.

Love Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from the University of Wyoming in Laramie, where he is in a military training program. He updates his dad on his attempts to sell clothes and shoes, mentions forgetting to stamp a letter to Edith and John, and references knowing Russ Wellman and staying at the M.P. Barracks. He describes a busy military-academic schedule including English, Math, Physics Lab, P.T., and Military classes, notes he got a stomach illness from cantaloupe, played basketball, and is planning a Sunday visit to a girlfriend who lives 20 miles out and promises chicken dinner.

Lighthearted observations about German POW workers, Italian wages, and bartering with cigarettes. Slight wonder at the post-war world around him.
metadata.legibility_notes: Typed letter, generally very legible. A few words are struck through or overtyped (e.g., 'Film' with correction). Handwritten closing note at bottom left is slightly harder to read. The word 'Miro' before 'Film' may be 'Miro' or another name — likely a film plant name. '275 lire' is split across a line as '2751ire' (typewriter artifact). 'Dego' appears to be slang for Italian language/person.
metadata.historical_context: Post-WWII occupation of Italy, February 1946. Bob is stationed with an ordnance inspection unit overseeing motor pools including a German Prison Camp. German POWs are being used as labor to maintain US military equipment. References to displaced persons (Austrian woman and her sister fleeing Yugoslavia after her brother was killed by Germans). Mentions of Italian economy — low wages (275 lire/day ≈ $1.25), barter economy with cigarettes. Plans to travel to Foggia and Bari suggest ongoing inspection duties across southern Italy.

pages.transcription:
Feb. 28, 46

Dear Dad

    Just got to work and since I dont have much to do I am going to write you a letter.  First I must tell you my job was changed from driving a wecker to the tune of an office worker in the ordinance Inspector office not a bad deal.  My boss is Lt. Davidson a former piolt a swell guy.  Our job is to pull routine inspection at this depot about the same as Uncle Warren job.  Yesterday we inspected a German Prison Camp No. 2  At this camp which is a large Mortor Pool we look at 10% of them they have about 226 trucks there God knows how many Jeeps .  This Gernan there drive and repairs this equipment if it wasn't for them I dont know what this Depot would  they do all the work there and in fact all over the place.  They are smart as hell.  This job will take over most of Italy the Lt. says we are suppose to go to Foggia and Bari in a couple of weeks  Probably will fly over he he says.  Went to Caserta last night and had a good timem  Met an Austrian woman at the Red Cross Club she couldnt speak a word of English this guy with me can understand a little Dego so we got a long all right.  She can speak five language but no English.  Her brother was killed by the german and she and her sister made a run for it  they used to live in Youslavia before the war  They have a cousin who lives in the states who sends them clothing .  Last week the Ginny stole all there stuff while they were at work  they work at the Miro Film [Film?] plant a USA project  34 bucks a month.  My Office girl get 2751ire a day about $1.25 in American money  You can anything done here for a cigarretes.  I picked a M3 knife at the arsenal the other day it has a blade of about 6iches .  I got a Hinie to sharpen it up sand the handle and polish it .  they will do it for nothing but the next you willfind them to busy  but give him a smoke and he will be your man for anything  you they salute us all the time  what a life.  But this talking both German and Italyian is quite a job , but Iam learning pretty fast though .  I will write again soon  we have to inspect another motor pool in a couple minutes  so goodby for now.

                                    Lots of Love
                                    Bobby

Jam no Bonds
from you,
Total $600 on 6.50

letters.summary: Written February 28, 1946, from Bob Hanifen to his father in Des Moines, this letter describes Bob's new role as an office worker in the Ordnance Inspector's office in Italy, a change from driving a wrecker. He recounts inspecting German Prison Camp No. 2's motor pool, where German POWs perform most of the maintenance work, and notes upcoming inspection trips to Foggia and Bari. Bob also shares colorful observations about post-war Italian life: meeting a multilingual Austrian refugee woman at the Red Cross Club in Caserta, the low wages of Italian workers, the barter economy fueled by cigarettes, and his acquisition of an M3 knife polished by a German prisoner. The letter closes with a handwritten financial note about bonds, addressed to 'Jim' (his father, James Sr.).

pages.transcription:
May 25 - 76

Dear Bob - Found these few treasures thought you mite like them - I don't think I have anything else - Ray - Peggy husband sald [sold?] my car ned [net?] $275.00 but I'm bewildered where its gone to already I haven't forgotten I made Long d- Calls while there but Ill send one these pay day - Ive run into a little trouble the medicine to help my bones from degenerating has caused me to lose over ½ of my hair its very thin & have had to go to a couple new drs & each one wants this & that test -

This week you will again see one of your lovely children rec a Diploma - my you have done so good did the twins send you a graduation card If so I did not get one - I hope they didn't goof off & ruin themselves but I'm a little worried as yg [young?] Jimmy hinted something when I was there - They been their own boss too many yrs - Hows the girls doing? Good I hope

J & Helen called me Easter & yg Jimmy called when Edith died - said Doris still acted like nut I say like always - saffy [sappy?] -

Mat had a birthday both he & Bob this month - The 2 Barquists Tom was 16 & Bobby - [partment?] he must be 24 about now - Ann has not had her second baby yet it must be almost a month late - H & chan sent me a small Calculator - I don't drone [know?] any $$ to add only subtract & I can use my fingers with my big quantity - Marcus has had tough luck xx winds have ripped 4 sets of nets already this yr & its 1st yr that fish has had a good mkt price - He is sure health wise bad his blood pressure is very low but still able to gad of desires - meow -

All my love -

                    Aunt P F Hanifen
thought I'd better add the last name as maybe P Miller mite would but guess her scribble much better than mine —

letters.summary: Written May 25, 1976, this letter from Aunt P.F. Hanifen to her nephew Bob shares a collection of family keepsakes and updates on various relatives. She describes her own health troubles — medication for bone degeneration causing significant hair loss — and mentions attending multiple new doctors. She celebrates one of Bob's children receiving a diploma, worries about 'the twins' possibly goofing off, notes birthdays for Mat, Bob, and the Barquist boys, reports that Ann's second baby is overdue, and shares that Marcus has had a rough fishing year with winds destroying four sets of nets. The letter closes warmly with a humorous self-deprecating note about her handwriting compared to Pearl Miller's.

metadata.legibility_notes: Document is typed and generally legible. Some pages appear to be duplicates or carbon copies. Minor wear and foxing on paper. A few words slightly obscured by folds or age. Pages 2 and 3 appear to be duplicates (carbon copies of the same content), as do pages 1 and 4, and pages 5 and 6.
metadata.historical_context: This is a WWII demobilization-era document from November 1945, shortly after the end of World War II. It is an extract of Special Orders Number 331 from Headquarters AAF Overseas Replacement Depot and AAF Redistribution Station Number 5 in Greensboro, North Carolina. The orders extend furloughs for various enlisted men (including Pvt Robert E. Hanifen) who were granted 30-day reenlistment furloughs effective November 4, 1945. The extensions are granted 'for convn of the govt' (convenience of the government), pushing return dates to January 7-8, 1946. This reflects the massive demobilization effort following V-J Day in August 1945.

letters.summary: This is an official U.S. Army War Department document — a restricted extract of Special Orders Number 331, dated 27 November 1945, from Headquarters AAF Overseas Replacement Depot and AAF Redistribution Station Number 5 in Greensboro, North Carolina. The document extends the reenlistment furloughs of numerous enlisted men, including Pvt Robert E. Hanifen (ASN 17170600) of 424 Virginia Avenue, Des Moines, Iowa, by 34 days for the convenience of the government, pushing his required return date to midnight 7 January 1946. The document was mailed from Fort Des Moines and postmarked December 15, 1945, and arrived at Bob Hanifen's home address. Three carbon copies of the two-page order are included in the envelope, all signed by Captain William F. Shafer, Adjutant, by order of Colonel Younts. This document is significant as it captures Bob Hanifen's status in the immediate post-WWII demobilization period, showing him on extended furlough at his Des Moines home address.

metadata.from: Bob (Robert) Hanifen — signed 'Bobby,' writing to his father
metadata.to: Dad (James Edward Hanifen Sr.)
metadata.location_written_from: Unknown military/overseas posting (post-WWII occupation era, likely Japan or occupied territory based on context)
metadata.envelope_notes: No envelope visible in provided images
topics: working with buddy at General Supply
sending package home with gifts for family
buddy Lou transferring and becoming an interpreter
inquiry about brother Jimmy leaving the Army
people_mentioned: Lou (buddy, transferring to be an interpreter)
Jimmy (Jim Hanifen Jr., Bob's brother — asked if he's out of the Army yet)
metadata.mood_tone: Casual and conversational; somewhat lighthearted but includes a vivid and unsettling account of a nearby accidental shooting; generally optimistic about coming home soon
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is cursive and somewhat difficult in places; several words required guessing. 'General Supply' is inferred from 'Genered Supply' or similar. The word 'rating' may refer to a discharge rating or points. 'safe place' has a crossed-out word before it. Page 2 has some faded/bleed-through areas.
metadata.historical_context: Written in June 1946, post-WWII. Bob appears to be still serving overseas during the occupation period, waiting for his discharge rating to come through. He mentions locating graves of unknown soldiers, reflecting post-war graves registration work. The accidental shooting incident involving a .45 pistol reflects the dangers of peacetime military life with weapons still present.

pages.transcription:
7 June 1946

Dear Dad
    I received your package today and everything was in good shape. I am not doing much now. The office I used to work in closed down, so now I am working with my buddie at Genered Supply. Just killing time for two week till my rating come through. If it does, I am going to send a package in a couple of weeks with presents for different people. You open it alone. Inside will be a 45, you put it up for me in a [safe?] safe place. Wed. afternoon a kid two bags from me about 36 feet was shot by another kid messing around with a 45. The slug entered his right

side and came out the left side hitting his arm at the elbow and then glance off two walls. The bullet just missed his heart by 1/4 of inch. He is okay now, but I seen him a couple seconds after it happen and it wasn't very pretty. The Blood was on his legs all over the floor. My buddie Lou is transferring to [illegible] ome. He is going to be an interpreter, locating graves of unknown soldiers. Pretty good deal. Hope everything is okay at the now. It is getting mighty hot around here, believe me. Well that is about all the news.
        So long for now
            Lots of love Your Son
                Bobby

P.S. Is Jimmy out of the Army yet?

letters.full_transcription:
7 June 1946

Dear Dad
    I received your package today and everything was in good shape. I am not doing much now. The office I used to work in closed down, so now I am working with my buddie at Genered Supply. Just killing time for two week till my rating come through. If it does, I am going to send a package in a couple of weeks with presents for different people. You open it alone. Inside will be a 45, you put it up for me in a [safe?] safe place. Wed. afternoon a kid two bags from me about 36 feet was shot by another kid messing around with a 45. The slug entered his right side and came out the left side hitting his arm at the elbow and then glance off two walls. The bullet just missed his heart by 1/4 of inch. He is okay now, but I seen him a couple seconds after it happen and it wasn't very pretty. The Blood was on his legs all over the floor. My buddie Lou is transferring to [illegible] ome. He is going to be an interpreter, locating graves of unknown soldiers. Pretty good deal. Hope everything is okay at the now. It is getting mighty hot around here, believe me. Well that is about all the news.
        So long for now
            Lots of love Your Son
                Bobby

P.S. Is Jimmy out of the Army yet?
letters.summary: Written on June 7, 1946, Bob writes to his father from an overseas posting during the post-WWII occupation period. He thanks his dad for the package, mentions his office closed and he is now working with a buddy at General Supply, killing time waiting for his discharge rating. He plans to send a package home including a .45 pistol for safekeeping. He recounts a frightening accidental shooting incident in which a nearby soldier was shot through the chest with a .45, narrowly missing his heart. He closes by noting his buddy Lou is transferring to work as an interpreter locating graves of unknown soldiers, and adds a postscript asking if his brother Jimmy is out of the Army yet.

metadata.from: Pvt. Robert Hanifen (Bob) — signed 'Pvt. Robert Hanifen' on the interior of the card
metadata.to: Unknown — no envelope address visible
metadata.envelope_notes: This is a WWII-era patriotic Christmas card, not a traditional envelope. The cover features a silver bell with holly, red and white ribbons with blue stripes, and blue stars. Text on the cover reads 'A MERRY CHRISTMAS / From One in the SERVICE.' No postmark, stamp, or address is visible.
metadata.mood_tone: Patriotic, warm, and bittersweet — acknowledging the separation caused by wartime service while maintaining holiday cheer and hope for reunion.
metadata.legibility_notes: The interior text is printed/pre-printed verse in a cursive font, with the signature 'Pvt. Robert Hanifen' handwritten at the bottom. All text is clearly legible.
metadata.historical_context: This is a WWII-era military Christmas card sent 'From One in the SERVICE.' The verse references postponed joys and 'freedom's light,' clearly alluding to wartime separation. The red, white, and blue patriotic motifs (torch of liberty, ribbons, stars) are consistent with WWII home-front and military iconography. Bob Hanifen is listed as a Private (Pvt.), indicating he was enlisted in the U.S. Army.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: WWII patriotic Christmas card from 'Pvt. Robert Hanifen.' Bob held the rank of Private during 1944–early 1945 (he is PFC by February 1946 per Letters 8, 15, 25). The card's verse about postponed joys 'until we meet again 'neath freedom's light' is consistent with active wartime service. Bob was at Sheppard Field in spring/summer 1944 (Letter 71 postmarked May 25, likely 1944) and at University of Wyoming in summer 1944. He may have been home or on leave at Christmas 1944 before shipping to Greensboro in January 1945. Most likely Christmas 1944, possibly 1943 if he was already in some form of service, but 1944 is most probable given his Pvt. rank and active training timeline.

letters.summary: This is a WWII-era patriotic Christmas card sent by Pvt. Robert (Bob) Hanifen 'From One in the SERVICE.' The card features patriotic red, white, and blue imagery including a silver Liberty Bell on the cover and a torch of freedom on the interior. The pre-printed verse acknowledges the wartime separation — 'Some joys must be postponed until / We meet again 'neath freedom's light' — while affirming the spirit of Christmas endures through friendship. Bob signed his name and rank (Pvt. Robert Hanifen) at the bottom, making this a significant document of his WWII military service.

The back of the envelope annotation is in a different hand and partially illegible. The date year is not written in the letter itself but the postmark year appears to be 1945 based on context (WWII, shipping to Germany).
metadata.historical_context: Written during WWII. Bob is a PFC at a separation/replacement station in Greensboro, NC, and has been told at orientation that his unit is likely heading to Germany. He references a POE (Port of Embarkation) and APO number, consistent with late-war deployment processing. Possible POE cities listed: New Orleans, Newport News, or New York.

pages.transcription:
[Return address, handwritten:]
P.F.C. Robert E. Hanifen [serial number]
Sqdn Branch 2
[Printed:] AAF ORD AND AAF RS NO. 5
Greensboro, North Carolina

[Postmark:] GREENSBORO, N.C. / JAN 21 / [194?] / [time stamp]

[Upper right:] Free

[Addressed to:]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
90 [?] Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

[Written sideways/rotated, appears to be a later annotation or forwarding address in a different hand, green ink:]
Bob Hanifen
Co A - 35 Sig[nal?] Trng Bn
Camp Crowder, [Neosho?] Mo.
Spr. told mo.
149 Mormon Motors

[Number along side edge:] 37169243

Jan. 21.

Service Club
AAF ORD AND AAF RS NO. 5
Greensboro, N.C.

Dear Dad

Got one letter from you the other day. Have you got the 80 bucks yet. I moved to Sqd[ron?] [Bar?]. At Orientation they said we were going to Germany & maybe we will. We should ship out of here in less than 2 weeks. We made out changes of address to be sent to a P.O.E. for our A.P.O. number. You probably will [be] knowing where I am going before I do. Our P.O.E. will either be New Orleans, Newport News, Virginia,

or New York. Hope it is New York. Maybe we can get a couple of passes out of there. We are doing nothing to speak of in terms of work. Work at the Mail room, one day for 6 hrs. That's all. I have gained a lot weight here all my clothes on I weigh 160 not bad only been to town once. Been to shows on camp almost every night though in bed by 9 or 10 almost every night.

Well Dad goodbye for now.
Love Your Son

Bob

letters.summary: PFC Bob Hanifen writes to his father from the Service Club at the AAF Replacement Station in Greensboro, NC, dated January 21 (likely 1945). He reports that at Orientation they were told they are likely heading to Germany, and that they expect to ship out in under two weeks via a Port of Embarkation — either New Orleans, Newport News, or New York (he hopes New York for the chance at passes). Daily life at camp is very light — just one shift of mail room work — and he notes he has gained weight, now at 160 lbs, going to camp shows most nights and in bed by 9 or 10. The letter reflects the quiet pre-deployment period, with Bob in good spirits but aware of the imminent overseas assignment.

metadata.from: Bob (Robert Hanifen) — signed 'Love, Bob' at the end
metadata.to: Family (likely parents/family at home in Des Moines)
metadata.location_written_from: Ft. Warren (likely Fort Warren, Massachusetts or Wyoming)
metadata.envelope_notes: Only page 2 of the letter is shown; no envelope visible
topics: airport visit to arrange ride home
flights to Des Moines and Omaha
metadata.mood_tone: Casual and upbeat; Bob is describing weekend activities, planning for leave, and reporting on his physical fitness progress with a sense of humor
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but some words require interpretation; 'Black Maria' (patrol vehicle) transcribed as best guess; some words partially obscured by folds or ink smearing
metadata.historical_context: References to military police (MP) detachment, C-47 paratrooper planes, and physical fitness training suggest this is a WWII-era military letter, likely from Bob while stationed at a military base
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter mentions leave September 30 to October 10 and C-47 planes to Des Moines and Omaha from the airport at 'Ft. Warren.' References physical fitness scores and visiting MP quarters. Letter 18a (Wyoming, 1944) also mentions a leave 'the last of the 30th of Sept. to Oct. 12' and states 'If you want to come up then would be a time.' The dates are nearly identical, strongly suggesting this is the same leave period. Fort Warren (Cheyenne, Wyoming) is near Laramie, consistent with Bob being at University of Wyoming in 1944. This is likely page 2 of a letter written in September 1944 from Laramie/Cheyenne area. Estimated September 1944.

pages.transcription:
sleeping at Ft. Warren. He sent us to the head M.P. quarters and we talked to a Capt. from Des Moines for a while, we got out and got in the Black Maria [Black Maria?] and started for camp. On the way we picked up a couple of men. We left them off at the Guard House, and went on to the M.P. Detachment B.C.K. Check out a couple of blankets and went to bed. Got up at 8:00 & eat breakfast & went back to bed till about 10:00 we than went down town to church. After church we went out to the airport to see about a ride home. We get off the 30th of Sept. to the 10 Oct. They said sure and if we had wanted to go to Omaha we could have went right then on a C-47 paratrooper plane. He says the C.O. of the airport a plane leave for Des Moines every afternoon of the week, and there is almost one every day to Omaha. I am leading my class in Physical fitness so far. 50 sit ups. 45 push ups. 13 pull ups and 30 squat jumps. It is a pretty rugged course. Well so long for now & take it easy.

Love Bob.

letters.summary: This is page 2 of a letter from Bob Hanifen, written while stationed at Fort Warren. Bob describes a weekend outing involving the MP (Military Police) quarters, riding in a patrol vehicle ('Black Maria'), attending church downtown, and visiting the airport to arrange a ride home during his upcoming leave (September 30 to October 10). He mentions the availability of C-47 paratrooper planes flying to Des Moines and Omaha, and closes by proudly noting he is leading his physical fitness class with impressive scores in sit-ups, push-ups, pull-ups, and squat jumps.

metadata.from: Bob (Robert E. Hanifen) — confirmed by return address on envelope: PFC Robert E. Hanifen, and signed 'Bobby' at end
metadata.to: James C. Hanifen (father, James Edward Hanifen Sr.) — addressed 'Dear Dad'
metadata.location_written_from: Naples, Italy — arrived there the 20th, writing from Red Cross building
metadata.location_sent_to: 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.postmark: U.S. Army Postal Service, FEB 23, 37 [?] — likely FEB 23 1946
metadata.envelope_notes: Return address: PFC Robert E. Hanifen - 17170600, Sqd. A3 - APO 11898-A, Postmaster New York, N.Y. Marked 'Free' (military free postage). Addressed to Mr. James C. Hanifen, 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa.
metadata.mood_tone: Energetic, observational, candid, and at times crude — Bob is vividly describing his first impressions of Naples and North Africa, with frank and unfiltered commentary on the local population, war damage, and daily life as a soldier
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is fast and informal with many ligatures and abbreviations. Some words are difficult to parse — especially proper nouns and slang. The letter is written on multiple pages of American Red Cross stationery. Several passages with profanity or crude remarks are legible in context. The diagram on page 2 (a small sketch of barracks layout) is noted.
metadata.historical_context: WWII aftermath — Bob has arrived in Naples, Italy, likely as part of post-war occupation or transition forces. The city shows heavy bomb damage from wartime Allied operations. He references the Air Corps and Infantry having 'worked this place over.' He passed through Casablanca (Morocco) en route. References to 'Degas' (likely Italians, slang) and wartime conditions. The letter is dated February 1946, just months after the end of WWII.

pages.transcription:
Feb. 21, 46

Dear Dad,

Arrived in Naples the 20th at 1200 eat chow on ship and left at 1300 the 7 Service Depot. We came up in trucks 50 to a trucks. Big open air job. Right through the heart of the city. Those Air Corps and Infantry men really worked this place over. Blew hell out of it. Most of it is cleaned up now. It wasn't hurt as bad as the papers in the states said so. The Degas [Dagos?] took and thumbed their noses [at the] Dolan [?] sign and smiled all at the same time. What filthy bastards. One girl sounded off and said look at the American son-of-a-bitch. Swear[s]

a hard time. We pulled into a big area filled up with big buildings, palm trees, swimming pools, tennis courts, roller skating rinks, stadium and really a beautiful place. Statues all over. What was funny tho was all the holes in the buildings. It was formerly a German Headquarters. It was Mussolini's Naples Expedition [Expeditionary?] way — round, supposed to represent Africa. It is really nice. We live in small wooded barracks with a big building in the Patio [sketch of barracks layout] as nice barracks deal. Good food. PWs do all the work on the field. All we do is pulled guard. Pretty rough for a lot of Italians has been going in camp by 19 and 20 [hundred hours]. All I think [know?] about to tell they say. Sound like

war at night around here. Am in the Red Cross building now. Beautiful. Wish I had a camera to show it to you in picture. It took us 10 days to get here. We stopped a day at Casablanca. What a town. Filthy Arabs. Beg for shit damn near. We food away from the sea hull [?] if they can. I at least [?] in better shape than Naples but it really took a pounding. 3 mph we are lying in shallow water holes in them like your [?]. Big liners ran [?] Mercury [?] and big lined [?] aground our to the left of them. One boat is sunk so only the mast is still showing. Those Arabs are really the deal

The native cops beat hell out of them with the back [of] jack [?]. All the time for getting near the boat. They say in Naples here, they will sell you for anything. Had a [robbery?] last month. Beat hell and [someone?] and stole everything but his shorts. If you get a pass to town you are allowed to carry your own choice of weapon. .45 Carbine, M1 or whatever you get your hands on. This American Dego [Dago] are in like Flynn over here. All of them no Dego. The women are slut[s] all of them. As one time for two cigarettes. Cigarettes all for 2325 lire a carton [equals] $2 dollars in American money to anyone. Down town 36 or 40

bucks. But that is black market. I can send home all my money, which is mid [?] over the Army pay table. Not will be with my insurance and one band [?] out about 46 bucks. You can live damn good on your cigarettes ration. Just got back from the show. Doll Face and the Spanish Main. Beautiful theater, about the size of the Shrine [theater] here. Huge place. I will enclose this map of our voyage till we probably ship in 48 hrs. from now. A lot left of barracks already. Well Dad goodbye for now.

Your loving son Bobby

letters.summary: Written February 21, 1946, PFC Robert 'Bobby' Hanifen writes to his father from Naples, Italy, describing his arrival and first impressions of the war-damaged city. He vividly recounts the journey through a bombed-out Naples, his barracks (a former German/Mussolini headquarters), a stopover in Casablanca, and observations of the local Italian and Arab populations — often in crude and unfiltered terms typical of soldier correspondence. He also discusses the black market cigarette trade, military pay, and having just seen two movies at a beautiful local theater, with a promise to enclose a map of his sea voyage.

metadata.from: James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad) — signed 'Dad' at the end, writing to his son Jimmie/Jim Jr.
metadata.to: Jimmie (James Edward Hanifen Jr., Bob's brother)
metadata.location_written_from: Likely Des Moines, Iowa (D.M. referenced in letter)
metadata.envelope_notes: No envelope image provided.
topics: Bob's military/ASTP address at University of Wyoming
sending money and supplies to Bob
soldiers going home sick from Dakota University
metadata.mood_tone: Conversational, somewhat frustrated and world-weary, paternal concern mixed with dry humor; Dad is venting about the complexity of Army life and giving practical advice.
metadata.legibility_notes: Cursive handwriting is somewhat difficult to parse in places; some words required guessing. Abbreviations and phonetic spellings used throughout (e.g., 'no' for 'know', 'dam' for 'damn', 'nuf' for 'enough', 'rite' for 'right'). The address block '17470600' and 'ASTP' are partially legible.
metadata.historical_context: Letter references ASTP (Army Specialized Training Program), a WWII-era program that sent soldiers to universities for accelerated academic training. Bob appears to be stationed at the University of Wyoming in Laramie as part of this program. The letter also references soldiers being sent back home from Dakota University due to homesickness and then being told to wait for the draft, reflecting wartime mobilization anxieties.

pages.transcription:
July 13 - 44

Dear Jimmie

     Well I dont know how long this letter laid out here, so Ill try + give you Bob's add, Just thought, I cant give it all to you B.A. —: 17470600.
A.S.T.R.P. + there is a Unit No Laramie Wyo, also University of Wyo. + that is not all, He has to get Stamps on his letters, Isnt that Something. From the way it looks, you dont no more get out of one thing, until you get into another. That is a sure way to go Nuts, or get into dam good Trouble. You just as well no it. One Camp is just as bad as the other. Bob starts off at 6 AM + goes right thru untill 10:30 PM. In about a 3 weeks, He will no he is in Hell too. Ill bet a Buck - You when you get this, at lease rite. + Say I got it - 2 words arent a hell

of a lot to say. To me yet means, $500 saved. as it is Gamble to send it this way Bay. Bob dont get a dam penny while in school, even how to Send him Towels, + I suppose I will have to send him Soap + Toilet paper. 2 guys that went to Dakota University, Got Home Sick, so they let them Come Back to D.M. the next day, a letter came + Told them to Stay there, and Wait for the draft. Well the Kids Wouldnt have left, if they had Knowen that, But they Say it is realy Tough in those Schools - Well this is a nuf of this B. at this time, all I got to do now, is find Somthing to put this in 50 - 50 Long for this time

Dad -

letters.summary: In this July 1944 letter, James Hanifen Sr. (Dad) writes to his son Jimmie (Jim Jr.) to pass along Bob's military address at the ASTP program at the University of Wyoming in Laramie. Dad vents about the grueling nature of Bob's training schedule (6 AM to 10:30 PM) and the financial strain of supporting Bob, who receives no pay while in school. He also recounts a cautionary tale about two soldiers who left Dakota University due to homesickness only to be told to wait for the draft, warning that leaving these programs has serious consequences. The letter is signed simply 'Dad' and reflects a tone of paternal concern, dry humor, and working-class pragmatism.

metadata.from: Melio A. Tonini, Attorney and Counselor at Law
metadata.to: The Heirs of Frank J. Warnock, Deceased
metadata.location_written_from: Des Moines 9, Iowa (1000 Fleming Building)
people_mentioned: Ralph W. Hochhaiser (or similar — partially obscured)
metadata.mood_tone: Formal and businesslike, with some urgency regarding deadlines and decisions about the sale of property; mildly advisory in tone regarding the heirs' options.
metadata.legibility_notes: Page 2 has significant bleed-through from the reverse side making some text difficult to read, particularly the top portion. One name in page 1 (the purchaser of the 80-acre tract) is partially obscured by correction fluid/strikethrough. Page 4 has a large stain obscuring parts of the right column.
metadata.historical_context: Post-WWII Iowa legal/estate matter from 1954. The Hanifen family members (Robert Hanifen, John R. Hanifen, James Ed. Hanifen) appear in the Table of Heirship as heirs of Ollie Miller, connecting this letter to the Hanifen family correspondence collection.

pages.transcription:
MELIO A. TONINI
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW
1000 FLEMING BUILDING
DES MOINES 9, IOWA
TELEPHONE 4-2215

December 21, 1954

TO: THE HEIRS OF FRANK J. WARNOCK, DECEASED.

This letter is written to you as a supplement to my letter of December 17, 1954. You are advised that since that letter, the 80 acre tract of land now owned by Thompson [and Evans has been purchased by one Ralph W. Hochhaiser? — partially obscured by strikethrough]. He has also made an offer on the 40 acre tract of land on which the house is situated. For this property, he originally made an offer of $125.00 per acre, but I have now succeeded in getting him up to $150.00 per acre. The offer has been submitted through the Larson Real Estate Agency of Madrid, Iowa, real estate brokers. Consequently a commission of 5% will have to be paid to the Larson Agency.

Also as stated in my previous letter, there would have to be deducted costs of special administration, fees of special administration and attorney fees allowed me as attorney for the contestents, plus abstracting expenses and any other expenses incident to the sale, including taxes.

Because the purchaser of the 80 acre tract of land has already consumated the purchase of that part, it is very possible I will be able to get him to raise his offer on our tract. Personally, I feel that this is a good time to make the sale, inasmuch as we cannot get insurance on the buildings or the house, and they could be destroyed by fire. Eventho the sale should be consumated we would have to wait until March 1st or a short time thereafter before we could collect all of the money because we would not be in a position where we could give good title until the estate has been closed fully.

I don't know what your personal view-points may be but from my knowledge of the property, I think we are getting to the point where we cannot expect much more.

I have heard rumors that some of the heirs might want to renounce their share in favor of other heirs. Of course I want you to know that unless I hear from you I will make out the checks at the conclusion of everything just as the law provides.

Please advise me of your intentions in this regard. Also advise me if I have your address incorrectly set out. I must hear from you on or before December 30, 1954.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
MELIO A. TONINI

MAT/jg

pages.page_label: page 2 — Letter dated December 17, 1954 (front, with bleed-through from reverse)
pages.transcription:
[Note: The top portion of this page shows heavy bleed-through text from the reverse (page 3), making that section largely illegible as a distinct layer. The primary text of the December 17, 1954 letter begins below the letterhead.]

MELIO A. TONINI
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW
1000 FLEMING BUILDING
DES MOINES 9, IOWA
TELEPHONE 4-2215

December 17, 1954

TO THE HEIRS OF FRANK J. WARNOCK, DECEASED:

This is to advise you of the status of the Estate of Frank J. Warnock, deceased. As you all know, Mr. Warnock executed a will on May 20, 1953 in which he gave all of his property, both real and personal to Mr. Hershel Evans, now of near Winterset, Iowa, and Mr. Pete Thompson of Woodward, Iowa. Objections were filed to the probate of that will on behalf of those heirs who engaged me as attorney and also on behalf of all other heirs, inasmuch as the Court appointed me as attorney for all heirs, who had no representation.

The objections to the will of Frank J. Warnock were based on the ground that he was of unsound mind at the time he executed the will and also on the ground that undo influence had been used by the legatees named in the will. The case came on for trial on November 29, 1954, at the Boone County Court House. It continued through Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday. During the afternoon of Wednesday, an offer was made by the other side, offering to settle on the basis of the heirs taking 40 acres on which the house was situated, subject to the payment of the costs of special administration from that 40 acres. At that point we had to consider whether there was any opportunity for us to win the case. Based on the testimony which we had introduced there was absolutely no reason for believing that we could ever get to a jury. A Stipulation was therefore entered into on the basis of settlement for that 40 acres, and I was made trustee for the heirs. I accordingly have a Quit Claim Deed from both Thompson and Evans and their respective wives to that 40 acres. Of course you realize that the 40 acres which we received in settlement is not as good land as the other land across the road. After the sale of this 40 acres, I don't believe that any of us will be enriched very much, due to the fact that expenses of special administration are deducted therefrom and also attorney fees.

My last advice has been that Mr. Thompson and Mr. Evans were selling their 80 acres, at least I have understood that from a realtor in Madrid. If this is so it might be convenient to sell ours at the same time, in the hopes that the purchaser would want a total of 120 acres with improvements thereon. Although the improvements on our 40 acres are nothing to shout about, they can be made useable.

To those of you who have not been advised heretofore, I want you to know of the members of your family who have been especially helpful in this matter. It was through Agnes Warnock Graham that I first leaned of the death of this man, and consequently ascertained that there was a will leaving out all of the heirs. Pauline Miller was then appointed special administratrix. Since we had a relative as special administratrix, it gave us access to certain evidence

pages.page_label: page 3 — Letter dated December 17, 1954 (continuation, page 2 of letter)
pages.transcription:
-2-

that was discovered in the house that we would not otherwise have had. Mr. & Mrs. Robert Warnock, Mrs. Harriett Martin and Pauline Miller and Phil Miller were in constant attendance at the trial in Boone. Many of you took no part in this proceeding actively and yet you will participate in the results on an equal basis with those who assisted materially.

In order that you will understand the proportion to which you will be an heir, I am attaching a seperate sheet. If you have any questions regarding this record of inheritance, please advise me. On the other hand, if I have made an error in any way, I must also be advised. You understand of course that inasmuch as Elizabeth Brookhart died leaving no children and her husband is deceased, that none of her heirs receive anything. Harriett Martin as a surviving sister receives 1/4th and then the heirs of Albert Warnock, Evelyn Reed and Ollie Miller each participate in the 1/4th that would have gone to their respective father or mother. By this I mean that the heirs of Albert Warnock will divide a fourth, the heirs of Evelyn Reed will divide a fourth, and the heirs of Ollie Miller will divide a fourth. Horace Donald Warnock, being dead, his share will be divided between his children. Likewise the children of Earl Miller and the children of Peal Miller will divide the share that would have gone to their deceased parent.

If any of you have any ideas on how this property should be sold, please advise me immediately. I can tell you that it can be sold at private sale or at public sale. I am against, at this time, selling the same at public sale because I am certain that we would receive less. I am also personally convinced that if Thompson and Evans sell their 80 acres, we could probably get more out of our 40, selling it at the same time, but I would have to investigate this very thoroughly. I might tell you that the inheritance tax appraisers for the State of Iowa, have appraised our 40 at $75.00 per acre. This of course seems a ridiculously low figure and I feel that we should get around $150.00 per acre. Of course you understand that the appraisal of the inheritance tax appraisers is only for inheritance tax purposes.

Again I want to say that if you have any questions, please direct them to the undersigned as soon as possible. I am hoping that each of you get a copy of this as I am sending them to the last known address that I have.

Heirs of Evelyn Reed.......... Very truly yours, ..........1/4th
Glen Reed
Alvin Reed
Harriett Kiehm

[signature]
MELIO A. TONINI

MAT/jg

letters.full_transcription:
MELIO A. TONINI
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW
1000 FLEMING BUILDING
DES MOINES 9, IOWA
TELEPHONE 4-2215

December 21, 1954

TO: THE HEIRS OF FRANK J. WARNOCK, DECEASED.

This letter is written to you as a supplement to my letter of December 17, 1954. You are advised that since that letter, the 80 acre tract of land now owned by Thompson [and Evans has been purchased by one Ralph W. Hochhaiser? — partially obscured]. He has also made an offer on the 40 acre tract of land on which the house is situated. For this property, he originally made an offer of $125.00 per acre, but I have now succeeded in getting him up to $150.00 per acre. The offer has been submitted through the Larson Real Estate Agency of Madrid, Iowa, real estate brokers. Consequently a commission of 5% will have to be paid to the Larson Agency.

Also as stated in my previous letter, there would have to be deducted costs of special administration, fees of special administration and attorney fees allowed me as attorney for the contestents, plus abstracting expenses and any other expenses incident to the sale, including taxes.

Because the purchaser of the 80 acre tract of land has already consumated the purchase of that part, it is very possible I will be able to get him to raise his offer on our tract. Personally, I feel that this is a good time to make the sale, inasmuch as we cannot get insurance on the buildings or the house, and they could be destroyed by fire. Eventho the sale should be consumated we would have to wait until March 1st or a short time thereafter before we could collect all of the money because we would not be in a position where we could give good title until the estate has been closed fully.

I don't know what your personal view-points may be but from my knowledge of the property, I think we are getting to the point where we cannot expect much more.

I have heard rumors that some of the heirs might want to renounce their share in favor of other heirs. Of course I want you to know that unless I hear from you I will make out the checks at the conclusion of everything just as the law provides.

Please advise me of your intentions in this regard. Also advise me if I have your address incorrectly set out. I must hear from you on or before December 30, 1954.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
MELIO A. TONINI

MAT/jg

---

MELIO A. TONINI
ATTORNEY AND COUNSELOR AT LAW
1000 FLEMING BUILDING
DES MOINES 9, IOWA
TELEPHONE 4-2215

December 17, 1954

TO THE HEIRS OF FRANK J. WARNOCK, DECEASED:

This is to advise you of the status of the Estate of Frank J. Warnock, deceased. As you all know, Mr. Warnock executed a will on May 20, 1953 in which he gave all of his property, both real and personal to Mr. Hershel Evans, now of near Winterset, Iowa, and Mr. Pete Thompson of Woodward, Iowa. Objections were filed to the probate of that will on behalf of those heirs who engaged me as attorney and also on behalf of all other heirs, inasmuch as the Court appointed me as attorney for all heirs, who had no representation.

The objections to the will of Frank J. Warnock were based on the ground that he was of unsound mind at the time he executed the will and also on the ground that undo influence had been used by the legatees named in the will. The case came on for trial on November 29, 1954, at the Boone County Court House. It continued through Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday. During the afternoon of Wednesday, an offer was made by the other side, offering to settle on the basis of the heirs taking 40 acres on which the house was situated, subject to the payment of the costs of special administration from that 40 acres. At that point we had to consider whether there was any opportunity for us to win the case. Based on the testimony which we had introduced there was absolutely no reason for believing that we could ever get to a jury. A Stipulation was therefore entered into on the basis of settlement for that 40 acres, and I was made trustee for the heirs. I accordingly have a Quit Claim Deed from both Thompson and Evans and their respective wives to that 40 acres. Of course you realize that the 40 acres which we received in settlement is not as good land as the other land across the road. After the sale of this 40 acres, I don't believe that any of us will be enriched very much, due to the fact that expenses of special administration are deducted therefrom and also attorney fees.

My last advice has been that Mr. Thompson and Mr. Evans were selling their 80 acres, at least I have understood that from a realtor in Madrid. If this is so it might be convenient to sell ours at the same time, in the hopes that the purchaser would want a total of 120 acres with improvements thereon. Although the improvements on our 40 acres are nothing to shout about, they can be made useable.

To those of you who have not been advised heretofore, I want you to know of the members of your family who have been especially helpful in this matter. It was through Agnes Warnock Graham that I first leaned of the death of this man, and consequently ascertained that there was a will leaving out all of the heirs. Pauline Miller was then appointed special administratrix. Since we had a relative as special administratrix, it gave us access to certain evidence

-2-

that was discovered in the house that we would not otherwise have had. Mr. & Mrs. Robert Warnock, Mrs. Harriett Martin and Pauline Miller and Phil Miller were in constant attendance at the trial in Boone. Many of you took no part in this proceeding actively and yet you will participate in the results on an equal basis with those who assisted materially.

In order that you will understand the proportion to which you will be an heir, I am attaching a seperate sheet. If you have any questions regarding this record of inheritance, please advise me. On the other hand, if I have made an error in any way, I must also be advised. You understand of course that inasmuch as Elizabeth Brookhart died leaving no children and her husband is deceased, that none of her heirs receive anything. Harriett Martin as a surviving sister receives 1/4th and then the heirs of Albert Warnock, Evelyn Reed and Ollie Miller each participate in the 1/4th that would have gone to their respective father or mother. By this I mean that the heirs of Albert Warnock will divide a fourth, the heirs of Evelyn Reed will divide a fourth, and the heirs of Ollie Miller will divide a fourth. Horace Donald Warnock, being dead, his share will be divided between his children. Likewise the children of Earl Miller and the children of Peal Miller will divide the share that would have gone to their deceased parent.

If any of you have any ideas on how this property should be sold, please advise me immediately. I can tell you that it can be sold at private sale or at public sale. I am against, at this time, selling the same at public sale because I am certain that we would receive less. I am also personally convinced that if Thompson and Evans sell their 80 acres, we could probably get more out of our 40, selling it at the same time, but I would have to investigate this very thoroughly. I might tell you that the inheritance tax appraisers for the State of Iowa, have appraised our 40 at $75.00 per acre. This of course seems a ridiculously low figure and I feel that we should get around $150.00 per acre. Of course you understand that the appraisal of the inheritance tax appraisers is only for inheritance tax purposes.

Again I want to say that if you have any questions, please direct them to the undersigned as soon as possible. I am hoping that each of you get a copy of this as I am sending them to the last known address that I have.

Heirs of Evelyn Reed.......... Very truly yours, ..........1/4th
Glen Reed
Alvin Reed
Harriett Kiehm

[signature]
MELIO A. TONINI

MAT/jg

letters.summary: This document consists of two letters (dated December 17 and December 21, 1954) and an attached Table of Heirship from Des Moines attorney Melio A. Tonini to the heirs of Frank J. Warnock, deceased. The December 17 letter explains the outcome of a will contest trial at Boone County Court House, in which the heirs settled for 40 acres of land (rather than the full estate) after Warnock's will left everything to Hershel Evans and Pete Thompson; Tonini was made trustee for the heirs. The December 21 supplemental letter reports that a buyer (possibly Ralph Hochhaiser) purchased the adjacent 80-acre tract and has offered $150/acre for the heirs' 40 acres through the Larson Real Estate Agency in Madrid, Iowa, with a response deadline of December 30, 1954. Significantly for the Hanifen family collection, the Table of Heirship identifies Robert Hanifen, John R. Hanifen, and James Ed. Hanifen as heirs of Ollie Miller, each receiving 1/21st of 1/4th of the estate.

New York, N.Y. Recipient address in cursive. Note: the year in the letter date appears to read '1946' though the postmark year is partially illegible.
topics: inspection tour to Bari and Manfredonia
German soldiers described as swell guys
brother stationed in Los Angeles / transferred to San Luis Obispo
metadata.mood_tone: Lively, humorous, and brotherly — Bob writes with a casual and joking tone, sharing entertaining anecdotes about near-marriage, jeep driving, Italian women, and army life. There is also an undercurrent of wariness regarding theft and safety.
metadata.legibility_notes: Most of the letter is readable with careful attention. Some words are difficult due to cursive flourishes and faded ink, particularly on pages 3 and 4. The word 'fratello' (Italian for brother) is used as a greeting and closing, which is distinctive. Some words required best-guess interpretation. The date year on page 1 could be read as '1946' or conceivably '1940' but context (WWII over, stationed in Caserta, APO) confirms 1946.
metadata.historical_context: Written in the post-WWII occupation period. Bob is stationed at Caserta, Italy, a major Allied headquarters during and after WWII. References to Cassino and Salerno reflect major WWII battle sites in Italy. Mentions of German soldiers as 'swell guys' suggests fraternization during occupation. References to P-38 and Luger German pistols, and guarded barracks/parking lots indicate ongoing security concerns in occupied Italy.

pages.transcription:
April 2, 1946

Dear Fratello Jim

In Italian it means brother. Well Jim I receive your letter and I am very glad to hear of your good fortune. Stationed in L.A. right near home is a pretty nice if you ask me. Larry about your being transferred to San Louis Obispo. Was sorry to hear that the motor fell out of the Chev. rolled — hell did you do. Tels of about 80 miles a hour. I drive the living hell out of my jeep "Mile Butch" is its name. That has a gun really move. It should though it had everything put on it except a new motor. I would really like to see Betty + Morton faces when Jim my boy hulled in. Bet it really floored them.

Bet it really floored them. I told you I am working in the Inspector office here at Caserta. I should be a Corporal before long I hope. Everybody is getting ratings. I am doing the work by myself since the Lt. went to Switzerchland on his vacation. I should get something at least by god I know the reasons why. The girls are very nice here, that is the nice ones. I also had myself married about 2 weeks ago. This girl was mad for my body + so was her mother. The nice girls go everywhere with their mothers. I got out of it just in time. She was a cute bit though, stacked my god was she. I seen Jack Buckley in Foggia a couple weeks ago when we to Bari + Manfredonia

for an Inspection tour. Look on the map it is on the Adriatic sea. These towns were shot to hell. I haven't been to Salerno or Cassino yet. But the boys says they are nothing but rumble as far as that. This town is full of holes. Jack Buckley is getting me a camera. Sweet guy.

My buddies here is getting me to carry a pistol at night by yourself you need something to protect yourself with. Our barracks area and parking lots are always under guards. These Guinies will steal you blind if they get a chance. I am trying to get hold of a P-38 or Luger, German guns. These Lineys over here all have them. They are really a swell gun. a 45 is pretty big to lug around all the time. Send me some pictures of yourself and I will do the

same. Write and tell if your girl would like a few cameo rings or necklaces or what have you. They cheap as hell here. Send me a few cartons of cigarettes and I can get anything. They sell here for 2700 lire a carton. That is about 130 cent a pack in American dough. Haven't gained any weight yet. But I hope I do. Could stand about 30 lbs. I recone you could to. The weather is very nice now. Hot in day time, cold as hell at night. They say it really gets hot here in the summer. These Germans are swell guys. Do anything you ask. They will sell anything if they can possibly do it. These Dieseboys though can [otta?] go to hell. Well.

Jimmy old man goodby for now.

Lots of Love Yours Fratello

Bobby

P.S. Picture of me + my buddie
San Venora. Put it in your album.

letters.summary: In this April 1946 letter, PFC Bob Hanifen writes from Caserta, Italy to his brother Jim (Cpl. James E. Hanifen Jr.) at Camp San Luis Obispo, California. Bob shares colorful anecdotes from occupied Italy: working in the Inspector's office while his lieutenant vacations in Switzerland, nearly getting entangled in marriage with an Italian girl and her mother, encountering Jack Buckley in Foggia during an inspection tour to Bari and Manfredonia, and navigating post-war conditions including theft risks and seeking a German P-38 or Luger pistol for personal protection. He also makes practical requests — cartons of cigarettes (valuable currency in Italy at 2700 lire per carton) and photos — and offers to send cameo jewelry in return. The letter is a vivid snapshot of American GI life during the post-WWII Italian occupation, written with characteristic Hanifen humor and brotherly affection.

metadata.from: Bob (Robert) Hanifen — signed 'Love, Bob' on final page, writing from University of Wyoming, Laramie
metadata.to: Dad (James Edward Hanifen Sr.)
metadata.location_written_from: University of Wyoming, Laramie
metadata.envelope_notes: No envelope image provided. Letter written on University of Wyoming 'Cowboy Joe' letterhead.
metadata.mood_tone: Cheerful, upbeat, and detail-oriented; Bob is eager to reassure his father about finances and enjoys recounting social activities and physical training experiences.
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but cursive is rushed in places; some words require guessing (e.g., 'toilet article' area, some proper names). Several words are crossed out or overwritten. 'Jesse Cook' is a best guess for one girl's name.
metadata.historical_context: Letter appears to be from a military training program context (possibly ROTC or early WWII-era military training), referencing physical training, obstacle courses, typhoid shots, room inspections, passes, and confinement to campus. References to Fort Leavenworth suggest prior military posting. Frontier Days in Cheyenne is an annual rodeo event.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Multi-day letter (Monday to Friday) from University of Wyoming, Laramie on 'Cowboy Joe' letterhead. References Frontier Days in Cheyenne (annual rodeo, last week of July), a September 30–October 12 leave, fishing with Alvin (who appears in Letter 31a from June 1945 in Laramie), physical training, and Pauline/John/Fran. The Frontier Days reference places the start of the letter around late July 1944. The mention of snow starting around September 16 and invitation to come before August for fishing aligns with summer 1944. The leave dates of Sept. 30–Oct. 12 are almost identical to Letter 14's leave of Sept. 30–Oct. 10, confirming the same period. Estimated August 1944.

pages.transcription:
Mon.

Dear Dad, We have a free period so I thought I would write you a letter. The expense account is coming along all right. I have 16 dollars left & I loaned Jim 6 dollars in Fort Leavenworth. I had to get my blouse tailored and it took 2.25 for that, 1.43 for towels & wash rags, a couple bucks for [that?] toilet articles. I have only spent a 1.60 on girls, I let them take me out, or just go for a walk. I do my own laundry like my socks & under wear, I have to send my other clothes to the laundry. It takes about 30¢ a week for it. I spent about 3.00 in Leavenworth and about 2.22 in Denver. Now that we have got settled I only spend about 1.00 a week. We get a leave the last of [the?] 30th of Sept. to Oct. 12. If you want to come up than would be a time; come up about a couple days before my leave and I could ride home with you. That other money went to

something, but I don't for what. I am taking it pretty easy now and I am going to make up for the loss. Every Sunday they want to go on a trip to the mountain. It costs about $2.00. I haven't yet, because this Sunday we are going down to some kid's [or?] uncle's house for the weekend. Next week we are going to hitchhike to Cheyenne to see the Frontier Days. 1 dollar a week would get me along fine. Everything thing in this town is pretty reasonable except haircuts they cost 75¢. I don't know where I could get you a fish pole to use. Some kid here got to go fishing with some men Sunday and they caught about 6 trout apiece. I have gained 4 pounds now. The P.T. is really making me rugged, a 35 min. of exercises, 1 hr. 30 of football, the obstacle courses, and a half mile. All the other guys have spent a lot more money than I have & I move so fast. They want about a buck for a good meal in town. I haven't started way of it yet, but I come awful close to it. I am going to try [to?] to find Alvin again, maybe I can get a couple of meals.

Friday       Dear Dad

I will start writing again, it is the first opportunity I have had since Mon. It is

2.

about 20:06 now. We just got another typhoid shot, what a blast, I can hardly write, got a bump as big as an egg. A lot of things have happened since Mon. I met Alvin and am going fishing with him Sun. He says for you to come up he has a couple extra poles. The fishing is really good he say. It starts snowing about Sept. 16 so he say. If you want to fish or even get here you had better come around in August. I spent the buck Wed. night. My Bud's boy friend who is a acting second [lieutenant?] came up and got a 3 hr. pass till 10:30. Had a pretty good [time?]. The girls I have been going is a thing of the past. I dropped her & found two others. One's name is Marian Devant she lives in Laramie, & the other Jesse Cook lives about 22 miles out of town. She drives in and we have a pretty good time. Got a letter from Jim & John,

They are really bearing down on us now. If the rooms don't pass inspection & you with, it no pass for the week — you are confined to the campus. I done my school work & [also?] made my bed and feel pretty good but not too good. I now weigh a pound & a half more since Mon. Tell Fran & Pauline hello & John too. Have really been busy. Always on the ball. Got a bugler now who tries to beat the gong every morn. There are looking for him & when we do, [waiting?] for him. Tell Kay hello and thanks for letter. Tell the boys hello too. Tell Les his crate would blow up in this altitude. We went on a picnic in the mountains Sun. up to the summit, the highest [point?] on the Lincoln Highway, 8360. The 38 Chev. darn near blew up. Every mile or so it ran out water. We had to walk the last 6 miles to the top. We could see 6 states from a lookout station. Some ladies took my picture up there & am sending them as soon as I can.

Well good by for now
Love, Bob
