something, but I don't for what. I am taking it pretty easy now and I am going to make up for the loss. Every Sunday they want to go on a trip to the mountain. It costs about $2.00. I haven't yet, because this Sunday we are going down to some kid's [or?] uncle's house for the weekend. Next week we are going to hitchhike to Cheyenne to see the Frontier Days. 1 dollar a week would get me along fine. Everything thing in this town is pretty reasonable except haircuts they cost 75¢. I don't know where I could get you a fish pole to use. Some kid here got to go fishing with some men Sunday and they caught about 6 trout apiece. I have gained 4 pounds now. The P.T. is really making me rugged, a 35 min. of exercises, 1 hr. 30 of football, the obstacle courses, and a half mile. All the other guys have spent a lot more money than I have & I move so fast. They want about a buck for a good meal in town. I haven't started way of it yet, but I come awful close to it. I am going to try [to?] to find Alvin again, maybe I can get a couple of meals.

Friday       Dear Dad

I will start writing again, it is the first opportunity I have had since Mon. It is

about 20:06 now. We just got another typhoid shot, what a blast, I can hardly write, got a bump as big as an egg. A lot of things have happened since Mon. I met Alvin and am going fishing with him Sun. He says for you to come up he has a couple extra poles. The fishing is really good he say. It starts snowing about Sept. 16 so he say. If you want to fish or even get here you had better come around in August. I spent the buck Wed. night. My Bud's boy friend who is a acting second [lieutenant?] came up and got a 3 hr. pass till 10:30. Had a pretty good [time?]. The girls I have been going is a thing of the past. I dropped her & found two others. One's name is Marian Devant she lives in Laramie, & the other Jesse Cook lives about 22 miles out of town. She drives in and we have a pretty good time. Got a letter from Jim & John,

They are really bearing down on us now. If the rooms don't pass inspection & you with, it no pass for the week — you are confined to the campus. I done my school work & [also?] made my bed and feel pretty good but not too good. I now weigh a pound & a half more since Mon. Tell Fran & Pauline hello & John too. Have really been busy. Always on the ball. Got a bugler now who tries to beat the gong every morn. There are looking for him & when we do, [waiting?] for him. Tell Kay hello and thanks for letter. Tell the boys hello too. Tell Les his crate would blow up in this altitude. We went on a picnic in the mountains Sun. up to the summit, the highest [point?] on the Lincoln Highway, 8360. The 38 Chev. darn near blew up. Every mile or so it ran out water. We had to walk the last 6 miles to the top. We could see 6 states from a lookout station. Some ladies took my picture up there & am sending them as soon as I can.

Well good by for now
Love, Bob
letters.summary: This is a multi-day letter (started Monday, continued Friday) from Bob Hanifen to his father, written on University of Wyoming letterhead while Bob is undergoing military training in Laramie. Bob provides a detailed account of his expenses, reassuring his father he is managing money carefully, doing his own laundry, and avoiding unnecessary spending. He describes vigorous physical training (PT, obstacle courses, football), socializing with girls, plans to see Frontier Days in Cheyenne, a painful typhoid shot, and an exciting mountain hike up to the summit of the Lincoln Highway at 8,360 feet where they could see 6 states. He also mentions meeting Alvin (who has fishing poles and invites Dad to visit), receiving a letter from Jim and John, and the strict military discipline including campus confinement for failed room inspections.

pages.transcription:
Dear Jim,

    I want to thank you very much for my graduation gift. Everybody liked it very much & so do I. I got a small briefcase, a new ink pen, 2 dollar [worth of?] billfold, a chain & medal and a toilet set. I am going to [go?] to college in July or the Navy Air Corp. so don't be surprised when you hear it. I heard from Don P[illegible] and he has a pretty good deal he says.

Pauline made some cookies for you and she won't let us have any of them — they'll be there some day soon. We had another bad storm last night and did the river ever rise — they think it might flood again.

                    Your Brother,
                        Bob

P.S. John says he is going to throw you in the sewer when you come home again.

Pauline made some cookies for you and she won't let us have any of them — they'll be there some day soon. We had another bad storm last night and did the river ever rise — they think it might flood again.

letters.summary: Bob Hanifen writes to his brother Jim (Pvt. James Hanifen Jr.) at Fort Riley, Kansas, thanking him for a graduation gift and listing other gifts he received. Bob mentions he plans to attend college in July or join the Navy Air Corps, and shares family news: Pauline has baked cookies reserved for Jim, a bad storm caused the river to rise with possible flooding, and younger brother John humorously threatens to throw Jim in the sewer when he comes home. The letter is postmarked June 11, 1944.

Robert E. Hanifen'), and a letter from Jimmy about possible discharge in three weeks. Also references Drake Relay sports pages — the Drake Relays occur annually in late April in Des Moines. Letter mentions being in Europe/Italy still. The reference to Jimmy getting out 'in three weeks' aligns with Letters 11 (June 7, 1946) asking 'Is Jimmy out of the Army yet?' suggesting this letter predates Letter 11. Estimated April 1946, consistent with the Drake Relays reference and Bob still being a PFC about to be promoted to Corporal.

pages.transcription:
[Page marked '4' in upper left — likely continuation/page 4 of a longer letter, or the numbering refers to a different sequence]

learning to handle a auto car —
it is a tractor, made by the
White Co. & [it's a?] darn good tractor.
and the trick is the 90 foot flat
[diagram of tractor/truck labeled '90']
which is quite a lot of
truck for one man to handle
especially to back into a space
like this [diagram with measurements]
                50"      only 50 feet to
                          operate —
                          it really gets
                          you to investing

[Page is written upside down — back of the sheet from page 1, continuing from the bottom of page 1]

...a lot. and Maybe I
can a job on that convoy to
Germany if I work it right.
Everything is fine here only
the food ration has been cut
pretty bad guess the States are
not getting enough beef. I was
put in for a Corporal last
week. In a week now I
will be Corporal. So that's [Hanifen?]
[illegible] but $[illegible] a month. Keep a eye
out for a good car for me
to buy when I get home.

[American Red Cross letterhead]

42 mercury or some other
good make, one like that 42 [Chev?]
I am saving every bit of
my pay check. We can live
here a month on our PX ration.
3600 lire for our tobacco ration
alone which is $13.40 in American
money not a bad deal at all.
Hope everything is going good
with you. Got a letter from
Jimmy he says in three weeks
maybe he will be out sure

[Written upside down at top — back of page 3 sheet]

hope for his sake. Well Dad
goodbye for now. Say hello to
everyone for me.

                    Lots of love your son
                              Bobby

P.S. Dad please me the sport
pages for Friday and Saturday
on the Drake Relay. We
can't ever get to hear anything
up to date.

letters.summary: Bob (Bobby) Hanifen writes to his father from what appears to be a post-WWII occupation posting in Europe, describing his training on a White Company tractor-truck (90 feet long) and the challenge of maneuvering it in tight spaces, complete with hand-drawn diagrams. He mentions the possibility of getting a convoy job to Germany, notes that food rations have been cut, and reports that he has been put in for a Corporal promotion worth additional monthly pay. He is saving all his pay, notes that PX tobacco rations in lire represent good value in American money, mentions a letter from his brother Jimmy who hopes to be discharged in three weeks, and closes by asking his dad to send the sports pages covering the Drake Relays, as they can't get up-to-date news overseas.

metadata.historical_context: Written in April 1945, near the end of WWII in Europe (Germany surrendered May 8, 1945). Bob is training at Sheppard Field as part of the AAF (Army Air Forces) pipeline, apparently destined to be a B-29 gunner — the B-29 Superfortress was the primary bomber used in the Pacific theater. The training schedule described (basic training, gunnery school, furlough, combat crew assignment, overseas) was the standard AAF training pipeline in 1945.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad,

Not much doing around here tonight being as we are through for the day. Drilled a lot and took a few tests. Had a barracks inspection today as it was Sat. so we got the night off but can't leave the field. Not much to do in town anyway from the way the guys talk. Every body is going to be a B-29 gunner it seems judging from what the officers say. They need plenty of them. 71 days of basic training a week of gunnery school 18 day furlough 12 weeks with a combat crew, a small furlough and then overseas.

I figured if it goes according to schedule we should be over by Christmas. It is not so hot here yet as it has been cloudy about every day. The food is still pretty good 3 times a few [?]. We go to sleep at 6:30 in the

morning pretty good deal I should say. To Bag it is really a hard bed not having anyplace to put anything in. Everything you have you keep in your barracks bag. Well I got to go to shower now so goodbye for now.

Your loving son,

Bob

[remainder of page heavily obscured by bleed-through from reverse side — illegible]

letters.summary: Bob Hanifen writes from Sheppard Field, Texas on April 23, 1945, giving his father a casual update on his Army Air Forces training. He describes their routine of drilling, tests, and barracks inspections, and relays what the officers are saying about the training pipeline for B-29 gunners: 71 days of basic training, gunnery school, an 18-day furlough, 12 weeks with a combat crew, another small furlough, and then overseas — estimating he might be overseas by Christmas. He briefly notes the weather, food, and living conditions before signing off to shower.

metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob describes arriving at a new military base after traveling from Leavenworth through Tulsa and Dallas/Ft. Worth. Letter 46 (postmarked April 15, 1945) was written from Fort Leavenworth and mentions shipping to Texas on Monday. Letter 21 (April 23, 1945 postmark) is from Sheppard Field, Texas. This letter describes arriving at a base after the Leavenworth-to-Texas journey, placing it between those two letters. The flooding referenced ('Flood water held us up all day') matches spring 1945 conditions. The barracks 'made for 48 men which has now a total of 94 men' is consistent with overcrowded wartime training bases. Estimated approximately April 18–21, 1945.

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father describing his arrival at a new military base after a train journey from Leavenworth through Tulsa (detoured due to flooding) and Dallas/Ft. Worth. He describes his crowded barracks (designed for 48 men but housing 94), the lack of foot lockers, the early morning schedule (up at 5:15, off at 6:00), and a good steak dinner at the mess hall. Page 2 is heavily obscured by ink bleed-through, making the latter portion of the letter largely illegible, though references to Des Moines and general camp life are faintly visible.

pages.transcription:
Return address (upper left):
Cpl. Robert E. Hanifen 12170600
20 12th Ord. & Mnt. Co. BE
APO 528
c/o P.M. New York, N.Y.

Addressed to:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Postmark: U.S. Army Postal [Service], May 10, 194[6], APO 612
Top right: FORM 539 (in red)

American Red Cross [letterhead with red cross symbol]
Caserta  Italy

[Partial bleed-through or writing on envelope flap — mostly illegible; fragments visible include: 'prob m W[?]', 'and d[?]', 'by [partly?]', 'Some of th[e?]']

[top center, written as arithmetic/calculation:]
1875
D
37.50 [?] 1

9 May 1946

Dear Dad

    How my dad today in good health I hope. I have been having a little trouble lately with my head. But the Doc said I had a head cold which was causing it to ache. So everything is okay. I have been having a fine time in Italy lately. Went Dancing last night at the Red Cross and dance with a sweet girl I know. Some of these don't look no more funnie than I. This women work at the Officer Club might get a few connections through that Department. And over here that help a hell of a lot. I made 16 the 3 of may. Hope I can get by the time

I come home. f. I stay with St. David [St. David's?] I am sure of it. They are sending a convoy to Germany the 26 of May. It will take @ 2 months for it to get there and back. Hope I can on it. I am inclosing a 41 dollar money order. I still have $14 to send home to be up with my pay back. Did you ever get the 39 dollar one I sent. If so so. I never have got to 5 one get. Can't use the pip for recreational any more. Well Dad I will write again soon I have been goofing off lately but I will get on the ball soon.

Lots of Love Your Son
                    Bobby

This women work at the Officer Club might get a few connections through that Department. And over here that help a hell of a lot. I made 16 the 3 of may. Hope I can get by the time

[Page 2]
I come home. f. I stay with St. David I am sure of it. They are sending a convoy to Germany the 26 of May. It will take @ 2 months for it to get there and back. Hope I can on it. I am inclosing a 41 dollar money order. I still have $14 to send home to be up with my pay back. Did you ever get the 39 dollar one I sent. If so so. I never have got to 5 one get. Can't use the pip for recreational any more. Well Dad I will write again soon I have been goofing off lately but I will get on the ball soon.

Lots of Love Your Son
                    Bobby
letters.summary: Written on May 9, 1946, from Caserta, Italy, this letter from Bob (Bobby) Hanifen to his father updates him on his health (a head cold causing headaches, now resolved), his social life at Red Cross dances, and his financial situation — he is enclosing a $41 money order and inquires whether a previous $39 money order was received. He mentions a convoy being sent to Germany on May 26th that he hopes to join, and expresses optimism about coming home soon. The tone is cheerful and casual, with Bob acknowledging he has been 'goofing off' on letter writing but promising to do better.

Jan. 29

Dear Dad

We are finally leaving Greensboro. We leave at 8:30 PM the 29 for Camp Kilmer N.J. ersey, it is just 30 min. out of New York City. Hope we get a pass we are supposed to be there 48 hours. 23 hundred of us on the boat. We are going Naples, Italy. Not too bad. Don Pilant is going Germany probably Hamburg. Wish I was going there. We will probably go into Germany somewhere

after a while. Naples is for redistribution to different areas. I received the 26 Saturday they had everything mixed up. I will [write?] you from Camp Kilmer if I can. Well I got to run now. So goodby for now

Lots of Love
Bob

letters.summary: Written January 29, 1946, Bob informs his father that he is finally leaving Greensboro, North Carolina, heading to Camp Kilmer, New Jersey — a major embarkation point just outside New York City — before shipping out overseas with 2,300 fellow soldiers. His destination is Naples, Italy, which he describes as a redistribution point before likely being sent into Germany. He mentions that his comrade Don Pilant is heading directly to Germany, possibly Hamburg, and Bob wishes he were going there instead. The letter is a brief, rushed farewell written just before departure.

red tape for sending things home
metadata.mood_tone: Casual, lively, and slightly adventurous; Bob describes exciting and sometimes tense military experiences with a matter-of-fact tone, mixed with family concern
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is cursive and moderately difficult; some words are ambiguous (e.g., 'Carbine & 30 Thompson' on page 1, 'bubbie' or similar nickname unclear). Several words required close interpretation. The sketch of a guard tower is included on page 1.
metadata.historical_context: Post-WWII occupation period in Italy, June 1946. Bob is stationed near Naples with an Ordnance and Maintenance Company. German POWs were still being used as labor and staged a work strike, requiring US soldiers to stand armed guard. A serious riot occurred in Naples that same night, with 14 people and 8 police reportedly killed — likely referencing post-war civil unrest in Naples. The letter reflects the chaotic conditions of post-war occupied Italy.

pages.transcription:
19 June 1946

Dear Dad

   Down to work already, have been working rather hard the last week. Have been taking trucks to the Docks at Naples, a 90 foot float. Takes all day for one trip down & back. Went dancing at the Off[icers] Bros[?] last night, the girls are very nice there. It is raining like hell here, hasn't been very hot here yet. They say it can get pretty hot, but as yet it hasn't. We brought in to our Armament shop yesterday 400 Carbine & 30 Thompson Sub Machine guns. My [bubbie?] & I now have about 2000 rounds of 45 ammo. We are going to shoot it up [&?] [illegible] with a Sub Machine gun. It is a lot of fun. The German PW went on strike Wed. morning for more food, so we had to stand guard over them. I was in a tower about 60 feet high with a machine gun. [None/One] of them boys to go away. They went back to work that afternoon.

[Sketch of guard tower with labels showing dimensions: 3" and 10"]

I was coming back through Naples at about 7:00 with my float and the street was filled completely with people, about 2000 of them. I didn't know what to do exactly, so I asked the German. He handed me my 45 pistol. I said not yet, and put my tractor in 3rd and started plowing through. Them ginnies were pumping for their lives. At 10:00 that same night they had a riot there — 14 people were killed and 8 cops. I told them Dan would go if he could walk. I think Violet was against it. It would be [good] for him to go to school. At least now he will have to use his own mind now and not his mother.

Hope Johnny is doing alright. It takes so much red tape to send anything home, is why I haven't sent anything. I am enclosing a $59 dollars money order. You know what to do with it.

Lots of Love, Your Son
                Bobby

letters.summary: Written on June 19, 1946, from post-war Naples, Italy, Bob (Bobby) Hanifen writes to his father describing his military duties hauling equipment to the Naples docks, socializing at the Officers Club, and a tense episode guarding German POWs who went on strike for more food. He recounts a harrowing drive through a crowd of ~2,000 people in Naples and a deadly riot later that night in which 14 civilians and 8 police were killed. He closes with brief family remarks — mentioning Violet and Johnny — and encloses a $59 money order.

metadata.historical_context: WWII — Bob is stationed in Caserta, Italy, with APO 528, serving in an ordnance/motor transport company. References to Military Police units, fire fighters inspections, jeep security orders, and selling cigarettes to locals for lire all reflect the WWII occupation context in Italy circa early 1945.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is explicitly dated 'March 2' and the content — PFC Bob at Caserta, Italy; first letter received (from Jimmy); Red Cross dance in Caserta; Ike jacket just issued; inspecting 643 MP and 3090 fire fighters; Lt. going on vacation last week of March — is entirely consistent with Bob's Italy posting from February 1946 onward. Letter 3 (dated March 22, 1946) mentions the same inspection duties and Lt.'s Switzerland vacation. Letter 8 (February 28, 1946) mentions upcoming trips to Foggia and Bari and the Red Cross Club in Caserta. 'March 2' falls between these two confirmed dates. Year is 1946 with high confidence.

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS:
P.F.C. Robert E. Hanifen
T170600 2312 Ord [Ordnance]
MT Co. APO 528
Postmaster New York N.Y.

ADDRESSED TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Heo Way [Leo Way?]
Des Moines, Iowa

STAMP/MARKINGS: U.S. Army Postal Service A.P.O. circular stamp (partially legible). 'FORM 539' printed in red upper right. Cancellation bars visible at right.

March 2.

Dear Dad
     I got my first letter today. It was from Jimmy. A very nice letter too. I am at the Red Cross in Caserta tonight with 3 buddies of mine. They are going to have a dance here. They are pretty nice considering the place where we are at. We got our Ike jacket today and they are very nice too. I got it pressed and had my stripes put on for 30 cent not bad. Everything is dirt cheap around here. I really

like the idea. You can your cigarettes to anybody for 250 lire that is about 1.25 in American money. Give a guy spending money anyway. Today was a half day. Wed & Sat. both are. We inspected the 643 M.P. and 3090 fire fighters today. They are little places so it only took about 1 hr. I wrote up the report. It took about 45 min. The Lt. check it and said I did a pretty fair job. He is going on his vacation the last week in March. So that mean I will

probably do all the inspection, if we haven't got them done by then. The Capt. had another jeep stole last night. It was lock, all jeep must have a padlock in fact all vehicles do. And it was in a guarded parking lot. These ginnie [guinea?] are slick. I savt [save?] the food is swell but a lot of shows at Camp and plenty of entertainment in town. In fact I am enjoying myself very well. I can understand a fair amount of Italian

but it will be sometime before I can understand it very well. I do hope my letters are getting to you. I have written about 2 times a week. The Lt. lets me drive the jeep all the time. He is nice a swell guy.

     Well Dad goodbye
     Lots of Love [Your son?]
          Bobby

letters.summary: Written on March 2 (likely 1945) from Caserta, Italy, PFC Bob Hanifen writes to his father about life at his Army posting. He reports receiving his first letter (from his brother Jimmy), visiting the Red Cross, getting his Ike jacket pressed with stripes sewn on cheaply, and inspecting the 643 M.P. and fire fighter units — writing a report his Lieutenant praised. He notes the cheap prices in Italy, the ability to sell cigarettes for lire, entertainment at camp and in town, and his growing understanding of Italian, closing warmly as 'Bobby.'

References to secret current trip destination, prior trips to Australia and India, and naval censorship all reflect wartime conditions. Sender expresses desire to return to civilian life.

pages.transcription:
[Return address, upper left]
Limt. [Prowlite?] USNR
USS Gen. C.G. Morton AP-138
c/o Fleet Post Office
San Francisco, Calif.

[Postmark, center]
U.S.
JUN
4
1945

[Stamp, upper right]
6 CENTS AIR MAIL — UNITED STATES OF AMERICA [red airplane design stamp]

[Recipient address, lower center-right]
Mr. James Hanifen
904 Keo
Des Moines, Iowa

[Censor stamp, lower left]
PASSED BY NAVAL CENSOR [circular stamp with illegible central text]

Dear Jim:                                                    21 June 45
     Just stopped in at our little ship's store in picked up a box of cigars for you which I'll send on at the next port of call.
     We've had some trouble with packages not reaching their destination so will insure this one in see if it helps any. Sent you some cigars from New Caledonia around X mas time, but perhaps like alot of the things we send they never got there.
     Anyway the cigars are from Donnie & me in I hope you enjoy each & every one as much as we have appreciated the many favors you have extended us.
     Donnie is in Frisco where we are now living at 336 Serrans Drive, Park Merced. We have a very lovely apartment there & both like it very much. The only trouble is I don't get to see enough of it. Donnie is fine as always in always waiting my return from each trip.
     The last time in, the latter part of April, we had Helen Mulligan

- 2 -

out for dinner one evening 'n had a wonderful visit. Helen is with Fireman's Fund Ins. Co. in Frisco
     Our last trip, prior to this one as this is still secret, was to Australia & India a three and a half month jaunt, but interesting. We found Melbourne, Australia is the number one liberty port, and Calcutta, India the worst hole in the universe -- yet lots of sightseeing in spite of the filth.
     Now be sure & tell all "Hello" in I'm anxious to return to Des Moines and civilian life. Write when you have a chance in the best of everything to you & yours.

                                             Sincerely,
                                                 Jon

Limt. [Prowlite?] USNR

letters.summary: A U.S. Navy lieutenant (last name unclear, signing as 'Jon') aboard the USS Gen. C.G. Morton AP-138 writes to James Hanifen in Des Moines in June 1945, sending a box of cigars as a gift of gratitude for past favors. He shares that his wife Donnie is living in their apartment in Park Merced, San Francisco, and that their previous trip took them to Australia and India — praising Melbourne as the best liberty port and condemning Calcutta as the worst. He closes by expressing eagerness to return to Des Moines and civilian life, suggesting a pre-war connection to the Hanifen family.

metadata.historical_context: Letter written in July 1945 during WWII. Bob is stationed at Chanute Field, Illinois, a major U.S. Army Air Forces training base. He is in school for riveting, suggesting aircraft maintenance training. The war in the Pacific was still ongoing at this time (V-J Day would come in August 1945).

pages.transcription:
Return address (upper left):
B.R. Bobhanifen 1706[?]00
Sqd. T-B55-279
Chanute Fld. Ill.

Postmark: RANTOUL, ILL. / JUL 20 / 11-PM / 1945

Addressed to:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
964 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Fri. 20

[U.S. ARMY AIR FORCES letterhead with bomber illustration]

Dear Dad,

  I received your letter today. Hey I don't work in the garden anymore; that was just temporary work while we were waiting to get in school. We don't do nothing now but go to school. We started riveting last night and I hit my finger twice. Boy was I mad at that hammer. I am going to go up to Chic[ago?] again this week.

  I will come home Aug. 4 if I can make it. The Sat. after Pay day. I have a chance to go see Willmette & Mark so I think I will try & make it over one of these week. A kid drives to Toledo every week in and a guy can ride over

for a buck or two. When I come home I will ride to Iowa City with some guy & hitch hike from there. Or else a kid who lives in Olen [Olin?] you up by Fort Dodge drives home every once in a while & I can ride clear thru with him. I went swimming this morning and the water was swell. I have to go see the Dentist Mon. or Tues. & get that other wisdom tooth pulled, only hope it is well by the time I get ready to come home.

[one or two lines redacted/blacked out]

Well Dad, toodley for now. I am going to the show with a buddie. It is the only time we can during the week.

Your Loving Son,
  Bob

P.S. Have you receive my bonds yet. I should have three $18.75 just checking up.

letters.summary: Written on July 20, 1945, from Chanute Field, Illinois, Bob writes to his father updating him on his military training, noting he has moved on from garden work to school, where he has begun riveting classes (and already hit his finger with a hammer). He discusses plans to come home on August 4th — the Saturday after payday — and outlines various hitchhiking and ride-sharing options for the trip back to Des Moines. He also mentions a pending dentist appointment to have a wisdom tooth pulled, a trip to see friends Willmette and Mark, and closes with a postscript asking whether his father has received his bonds, noting he should have three at $18.75 each.

metadata.legibility_notes: Most text is readable but some words require inference. 'They told us' is partially unclear. 'New bomb' almost certainly refers to the atomic bomb given the August 1945 date and context of Japan surrendering.
metadata.historical_context: Written around August 9-10, 1945, directly referencing the atomic bombings of Japan and Japan's impending surrender. Bob mentions 'this new bomb might get me home a little quicker' — almost certainly a reference to the atomic bomb dropped on Hiroshima (Aug 6) and Nagasaki (Aug 9). Page 2 references hearing about 'the Japs wanting to surrender,' consistent with Japan's announcement around August 10, 1945.

pages.transcription:
Return address (upper left):
Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17110600
Sqd. I 1355-249
Chanute Fld, Ill.

Postmark: [city illegible], AUG [10 or 11], 11 PM, 45

Addressed to:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
914 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Thurs. 9

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

Dear Dad,

I received your letter and I might take you up on that deal about coming home. This new bomb might get me home a little quicker I hope. But I don't know.

We are not doing much in school no material to work with they told us.

Haven't got a letter from Jimmy in a long time. Has he shipped to another base.

Fri. 10

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

Just heard about the Japs wanting to surrender. Hope they do. I don't know what I will do this weekin [weekend?] yet. I will try to get home the week after next. Hope I can do it. Say hello to every-body.

Love Bob

letters.summary: Written across two days (Thursday August 9 and Friday August 10, 1945), this letter from Bob Hanifen at Chanute Field, Illinois to his father in Des Moines captures a pivotal moment in WWII history. Bob references 'this new bomb' (almost certainly the atomic bomb) as potentially speeding up his return home, and the next day excitedly notes hearing that Japan is wanting to surrender. He also mentions that school at the base is stalled due to lack of materials, and wonders why he hasn't heard from his brother Jimmy, speculating he may have been transferred to another base.

The garden detail may have been the 'temporary work while waiting to get in school' referenced in Letter 27 (July 20, 1945: 'I don't work in the garden anymore; that was just temporary work while we were waiting to get in school'). This places Letter 29 just before school started, approximately late June to early July 1945. Estimated July 1945, possibly late June.

pages.transcription:
Sat. 0800

Dear Dad

Well I am a gardener now. Permanent detail at the field garden. Not much work but I still don't like to weed & hoe. I imagine weeding carrots. All we do is sit around and work a couple hours at a time. I can't come this Sun. as we are restricted to our area. We are going to move to another Sqd. and so they say tough about you guys. Worked on a detail Sgt. for a week still couldn't get a pass out of him. Boy is he ever hard to convinced [sic]. It is raining now so that means no work today in the garden. I think we start to school Mon but I am not sure of it yet. We are suppose [sic] to get payed [sic] today but will

probably won't I think we will get payed [sic] around the tenth. I still have 4 bucks from last payday. and that 10 dollars money order Dad sent me. I am either going to buy some good Sun tan or a new pair of shoes. I can sell my old for four bucks 80 cents that [is half?] of what I'd pay for them. I need a shoe stamp though. See what you can do will you. When your [sic] downtown see if they have any of those flight shoes they are half cuts just a little bit higher than a pair of oxford. They have a slightly raised toe and some of them are sheep-line [sic] on the inside. If you can find a pair like that I wear a 10 ½ B and I will send the money to you. The End for now.

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father on a Saturday morning from a U.S. Army Air Forces base, reporting that he has been assigned to a permanent garden detail — light work he dislikes nonetheless. He mentions being restricted to the area so he cannot visit on Sunday, an upcoming move to another squadron, and difficulty getting a pass from a tough Sgt. He discusses his financial situation (waiting on payday, holding $4 plus a $10 money order from Dad) and asks his father to look for a pair of 'flight shoes' (half-cut, slightly raised toe, possibly sheepskin-lined) in size 10½ B downtown, noting he will need a shoe stamp — a wartime rationing requirement.

The mention of 12-week course and school starting Monday fits the Chanute Field timeline. Estimated June–July 1945, likely just before or at arrival at Chanute Field.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad

Well I finally got here. We arrived Thur. at 8:10. The field is pretty nice except it is a lot smaller than Sheppard and the buildings around here are black, sure needs a paint job bad. We have a swell barracks one of the few new ones. We start school on Mon. I can get home on a weekend pretty easy a few guys have already gone home. If I leave here Sat. Morn. I don't have to be back till Mon. at 6:00 not bad. I was thinking if I came home one of this weekend and had something to drive back I could go to see Aunt Bonnie the next weekend and bring the car back the next weekend. We are suppose to be here around 12 weeks but it is very indefinite. I was a Teletype

operator and now I am a sheet metal worker. All teletype operators go to Scott Field in about a week that how quick things change. Next week is payday and so I think I will come home if I can get away. Might catch a plane home a lot of them leave here pretty regular. only one of my buddies is left now, wonder if Pauline would care if I brought him home with me. He is a swell guy leaving in Hastings Nebr. Got to have a road buddie when a guy traveling. well Dad goodbye for now will write again soon

Love Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father shortly after arriving at a new, smaller military base (likely compared unfavorably to Sheppard Field in Texas). He describes the base conditions, notes that school starts Monday, and enthusiastically plans weekend trips home since he only needs to be back by Monday at 6:00. Bob mentions a significant and frustrating reassignment — he was a teletype operator but has now been made a sheet metal worker, as all teletype operators are being sent to Scott Field. He hopes to come home on payday the following week and mentions possibly bringing a buddy from Hastings, Nebraska along for the ride.

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS:
Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17170600
Apt T BKS-249
Chanute Fld., Ill.

ADDRESSED TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

POSTMARK: RANTOUL, ILL. JUL 30 4-PM 1945

NOTES IN UPPER RIGHT (partially torn): 'went ... [Miami?] ... fly ...'

Mon. 1800

Dear Dad
    Well here I am again, a couple of days have elapsed since I started this letter, a lot has happened since. Sat. I down & bought a new set of furniture 5 bucks. I am on the C shift now, from 1900 to 0100 that means no nights off except Sun. for 15 weeks. & no chance to come home very good. We were [crossed out] [crossed out] released Sun. at 0100 and so we decided to go to Chicago. We left at 1200 and got to Blue Island at 4:00 had a swell time at aunt Bonnie

We to Chicago with Kay next morning on the Burban[?] train. I was suppose to leave on the Ill. Central at 9:05 and after I left Kay I took out for the station and missed the thing by 5 min. Boy was I burned. I went back got the Blue Island train and went back and started to hitch hike back, got back too by 9osh in plenty of time. Well Dad Goodby for now & think. I will go back to Chicago again next week so goodby for now
    Love Bob

Here is one you missed
I don't no how
also one to join
[arrow pointing up]
[crossed out word/line — illegible]
what is that word.
I dont Know Myself

letters.summary: Written Monday evening (postmarked July 30, 1945) from Chanute Field, Illinois, Bob tells his father about being assigned to the C shift (1900–0100), which limits his chances of coming home. He recounts a weekend trip to Chicago with a friend named Kay, visiting Aunt Bonnie in Blue Island, but missing his return train by five minutes and having to hitchhike back to base. pages.transcription:
AFTER FIVE DAYS RETURN TO
Alvin Miller
808 South Second
Laramie Wyo.

[Postmark: LARAMIE WYO. JUN 5 1 30PM 1945]

[Addressee:]
James E. Hanifen
904 Keo Way
Des Moines Iowa

[Stamp: 3¢ United States Postage — 'Win the War' purple stamp]

808 So 2nd.
Laramie Wyo.
June 11, 45

Dear Jim,

   We heard from Jimmie today and he asked us to send these negatives to you so some could be enlarged. You can keep the negatives as we have some pictures finished. This was the first letter we had from Jimmie since he left the States. Heard from Bobbie about a month ago. How about coming out.

   Alvin went fishing last Sunday. Got his limit of some real nice ones. Better come out so as to go fishing with him

during his vacation. That is the latter part of July.

   How is Edith? Would so enjoy another good visit with both of you.

   Our girls are sure growing. Shirley Rae looks and even acts a lot like Alvin. She is almost four months old now. We have taken her on a couple fishing trips already. Of course Sharon Kay really enjoys her picnics this summer. It is time to put the baby to bed now so will stop. Hope you write to us soon.

                    Alvin, Carolyn &
                         Girls.

letters.summary: This June 1945 letter from Alvin and Carolyn Miller in Laramie, Wyoming to Jim Hanifen in Des Moines, Iowa relays a request from 'Jimmie' — who had recently left the States (likely on military deployment) — to send photo negatives to Jim so enlargements could be made. The letter also shares family news: Alvin's successful fishing trip, an invitation for Jim and Edith to visit in late July, and updates on their two daughters, Shirley Rae (almost four months old) and Sharon Kay. The tone is warm and sociable, reflecting a close friendship between the families during the final months of WWII.

pages.transcription:
Jan. 14

Army Air Forces

Dear Dad
  Sorry I haven't sooner but just haven't got around to it. I owe 16 dollars from my travel pay in Oct. I will be sending about 90 more in a couple of days. Just put it in Warbonds if you want for me. Haven't done much of anything yet except lay around. Met Don Clark last night had a swell time. Say have Johnny see if they still sell those fountain pen at school. Esterbrook is the name. Have him buy me one. If not write and tell me right away or have him go to P.X. and see if a Wac or somebody out there if Jimmy still there have him do it.

Be sure to answer if you do. And say send my coat hanger forget I buy some some place. Say I will call if I can when I get to my P.O.E. which will be pretty soon I imagine
Well Dad goodbye for now
Love your Son Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from Greensboro, N.C. on January 14, 1946, apologizing for not writing sooner. He mentions owing $16 from October travel pay and plans to send about $90 more, asking his father to put it in War Bonds. He requests that his brother Johnny find him an Esterbrook fountain pen at school or the P.X., and asks his father to send his coat hanger. He closes by noting he expects to reach his Port of Embarkation soon and will try to call.

[Upper right: Free]

[Recipient address]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
934 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

June 18, 45

Dear Dad

Well the shipping has been postponed again. It was definitely June. But bad condition of railroad travel forced a cancellation of orders. And on top of that they told us that all Teletype operators would go to Chanute as Sheet Metal workers. Now they say will go as Air Corps unassigned. All they are doing is playing with us. They don't need us any place in particular and they have to get us off the field somehow so that how they are doing it. I am discouraged about it all. No flight pay when you are a sheet metal or teletype worker. What a deal.

A few lucky guys are going to gunnery but they have been here since

January & February. God only knows who will ship. When we are on the train I will believe but not until then. We might go anywhere. So we're[?], Kessler, the floods are so bad they are routing trains going to Chanute thru New Orleans — it takes about 3 days to get there. But we are it easy though, don't have to do nothing but eat and sleep and attend roll call. We work harder trying to get out of work than if we were really working. Got caught day dreaming and had to clean latrines and stack[?] beds. Hope you had a nice Father's Day. I will send you something better when I get to a place where they have something.

Well goodbye for now

Love, Bob

letters.summary: Written on June 18, 1945 from Sheppard Field, Texas, Bob tells his father that his deployment has been postponed yet again — this time due to poor railroad conditions — and that his group has been reshuffled from teletype operators to sheet metal workers and now to 'Air Corps unassigned.' He expresses deep frustration at the Army's disorganized handling of personnel and the loss of flight pay that comes with these ground reassignments. He also mentions severe flooding rerouting trains through New Orleans, wishes his father a happy Father's Day, and promises to send a gift once he reaches a permanent posting.

metadata.to: Cpl. John R. Hanifen, 439th Engineer Construction Battalion

metadata.envelope_notes: No envelope visible. The letter is accompanied by a multi-page Narrative Summary document. Pages 1 and 13 appear to be duplicate copies of the same cover letter.
topics: appreciation letter to Cpl. John R. Hanifen
bridge construction over Han River and Som River
Narrative Summary of Exceptionally Meritorious Performance of Outstanding Service
metadata.mood_tone: Formal, proud, and deeply appreciative; the commanding officer expresses sincere gratitude and admiration for the battalion's extraordinary accomplishments under adverse conditions during the Korean War.
metadata.legibility_notes: Pages 1 and 13 are clear and legible. Pages 3-12 of the Narrative Summary vary: some paragraphs, particularly at tops of pages 3, 4, 5, and 6, are partially obscured by folds, shadows, or faded type, making some passages difficult to read fully. Page 12 top portion is faded. Overall the document is readable with some effort.
metadata.historical_context: This document relates to the Korean War (1950-1953). The 439th Engineer Construction Battalion served in Korea from 8 February to 31 December 1951. Key historical references include: the CCF (Chinese Communist Forces) spring offensives of 1951, the reconstruction of the KIRA-CHON railroad bridge (described as the highest railroad bridge repair in military engineering history at that time), opening of the railroad to WONJU, supporting X Corps and IX Corps operations, the collapse of the 6th ROKA Division in early May 1951, and the TANYANG Pass operations. The unit was originally an Organized Reserve Corps unit affiliated with the Kansas Contractors Association.

pages.transcription:
HEADQUARTERS
439TH ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION
APO 301

SUBJECT: Appreciation

TO : Cpl. John R. Hanifen
     439th Engineer Construction Battalion

     Attached for your "remembrances" is a copy of a "NARRATIVE SUMMARY OF EXCEPTIONALLY MERITORIOUS PERFORMANCE OF OUTSTANDING SERVICE" by our Battalion for our 1951 Korean tour. You may well be justly proud of your personal contribution to the great team that in fourteen short months has, without the benefit of any Unit Training whatsoever, accomplished many construction missions seldom if ever equalled in military annals.

     Not content to rest on the laurels of your 1951 construction feats, your constant devotion to duty, careful planning, and untiring manual efforts have carried you to even higher military construction heights. Your contribution to the brilliantly successful launching of the two 850 foot, 375 ton girders over the treacherous Han River and your professional manner of construction on Korea's longest highway bridge over the Som River has made the "439th" a name to be long remembered and discussed wherever Military Engineers might gather.

     Mere words will not even begin to express my sincere admiration and appreciation for your historic efforts, therefore, a very inadequate, but none-the-less heartfelt "THANK YOU" for your individual contribution to the important accomplishments of our Battalion will have to suffice.

                              [signature]
                         M. C. MILLER
                         Lt Colonel   CE
                         Commanding

NARRATIVE SUMMARY OF EXCEPTIONALLY MERITORIOUS

          PERFORMANCE OF OUTSTANDING SERVICE

                      - BY -

          439th ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION

          (For the Period 8 February-31 December 1951)

[Top portion partially illegible due to fading/fold]

Recognized as one of the most outstanding Active Reserve units in the Corps of Engineers in [Fifth?] Army area, the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion (VRC) was one of the first of such units to be called to active duty by the Department of Army for service in the Korean conflict. Affiliated with the Kansas Contractors Association, the unit was called to the colors on [14?] August 1950. With Headquarters at McPherson, A Company at Emporia, B Company at Hays, and C Company at Salina, Kansas, the unit had an initial strength of 28 officers and 215 enlisted men. Most of the key positions were held by World War II veterans, both officers and men, widely experienced in heavy construction through association with one of the larger contracting firms in Kansas.

     Arriving at Camp Carson, Colorado, on 26 August 1950, for assignment under the 32d Engineer Construction Group, this battalion and four others spent four tedious months trying to conduct unit training constantly thrown off schedule by irregular arrival of filler personnel, most of whom arrived only two weeks before the units departed for overseas, by an almost complete lack of T/O&E equipment, and by administrative and POM requirements.

     Upon its departure for FECOM on 8 January 1951, the battalion collected a large share of its construction equipment at the POE for the first time. After a three-week stop over in Japan during which time additional equipment was procured, the unit finally arrived in PUSAN, KOREA, on 8 February 1951.

     Finding itself in a bleak, desolate, and mountainous combat zone relatively untrained, untried, and yet to see most of its big equipment for the first time, the members of the battalion nevertheless approached their assigned tasks eagerly and aggressively and were destined to show that this battalion would live up to the expectations of the Department of Army that, through its Organized Reserve Corps program, an Active Reserve unit, particularly in the Corps of Engineers, could readily adapt itself to arduous field service in an acute emergency.

     Almost immediately after its arrival in PUSAN harbor, members of the unit worked assiduously day and night in adverse weather and under the trying dockside bedlam, to off-load its equipment. After a brief period of staging outside PUSAN, the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion became one of the first units to be operational under the 32d Engineer Construction Group with responsibility for reconstructing and improving, initially between ANDONG and SEOUL, the badly damaged railroad, and the completely inadequate MSR between ANDONG and TANYANG, both being vital supply routes directly supporting X Corps and attached units fighting then in the vicinity of WONJU.

     In spite of extremely hazardous roads, inclement weather, and constant threats from guerrilla attacks along the MSR, this unit disregarded the advice, subsequently proved erroneous, of personnel in higher headquarters in Japan and EUSAK that it was foolhardy and virtually impossible to move large engineer equipment forward from PUSAN on the MSR, and proceeded to move its equipment forward in one of the most outstand-

[Top portion partially illegible due to fading/fold]

ing achievements of the kind in Korea. The battalion at this time, February-March 1951, although battle lines were in turmoil, now found itself deep in X Corps area frequently ahead of elements of combat engineer units and in the close proximity of division CP's. Combat engineer commanders working slightly ahead on the MSR were astounded to see such quantities of large and vitally needed equipment in Corps but alone being so far forward in the Corps area.

     The extraordinary manner in which the Battalion organized itself after landing in Korea and the determination, aggressive spirit, and outstanding ability of its personnel quickly set it apart and above other units with similar missions.

     With the vital ANDONG Railroad line, the only railroad in eastern and central Korea connecting PUSAN with SEOUL through ULSAN, YONGCHON, CHECHON, and WONJU, in a serious state of repair, many of its destroyed bridges being expeditiously but unsafely repaired by Koreans, several tunnels either blocked or severely damaged, and major river crossings being made on temporary low-level structures threatened by floods at any time, rail traffic supporting the central front in March 1951 was very uncertain and could proceed with caution only as far north as CHECHON. With the front pushing ahead slowly approximately 30 to 40 miles to the north, opening of the WONJU railhead 20 miles to the north was of supreme importance to the tactical situation. In the meantime all supplies had to be moved forward by truck from CHECHON over an unimproved, rough and time-consuming road. The opening of the railroad to WONJU would mean the location and rapid expansion of supply dumps closer to the front, would permit evacuation of wounded by rail nearer to the front, and would eliminate truck transportation over 35 miles of narrow, rough road including a 7-mile one-way mountain pass. In effect supplies would be 4 hours closer to the front when the railroad could again operate into WONJU.

     Accordingly, the most vital of all Group missions was given to the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion to open the railroad to WONJU in three weeks. To do this, three major tasks presented themselves; first, the reconstruction of the KIRA-CHON Bridge, the highest railroad bridge of its kind in Korea spanning a deep valley to connect two tunnels on either side; second, the clearing of a tunnel blocked completely by the explosion of an ammunition train in the tunnel itself; and third, the rebuilding of the WONJU railyard lying in complete shambles just six miles north of the KIRA-CHON Bridge.

     With the CCF threatening attack in April, the key to the herculean task of rapidly opening the railroad to WONJU was obviously the rapid reconstruction of the KIRA-CHON Bridge. This structure consists of ten spans, total length of 800 feet, its steel girders being supported by steel towers near the center of the bridge and concrete piers near the abutments. During the retreat of UN forces earlier in the war, two 103-foot high steel towers at the center of the bridge were destroyed by demolitions removing a total of 270 feet of steel girders and track.

     The plan of attack in rebuilding this vital structure consisted of

[Top portion partially illegible due to fading/fold]

(a) remove debris of damaged towers, (b) erect new towers to facilitate erection of new towers by cranes still standing, (c) erect two new towers and transfer thereof portions of two other towers on still standing, (d) fabricate one continuous new girder 270 feet long, weighing 135 tons, from 40" I-beams, (e) launch the continuous beam on rollers across gaps between each tower, and (f) [jack?] the beam down into position.

     During the period 1-22 April 1951, utilizing two construction companies of the battalion supplemented by equipment and personnel from other units in the Corps, the unit rendered one of the most outstanding engineering achievements in Korea, officially recognized as being the highest railroad bridge repair in the history of military engineering, and became the first Military Engineers ever to launch a continuous beam of the record length of 270 feet weighing 135 tons.

     This unprecedented achievement was carried on under hazardous working conditions in cold, wet weather, subjecting the men working on steel over 100 feet in the air to the most severe tests. In spite of seemingly insurmountable problems, the members of the battalion by exemplary application of construction principles, through extraordinary devotion to duty working under severe pressure for long hours, completed this unparalleled engineering project in an exceptionally meritorious manner and in time to permit supplies to enter WONJU by rail during the crucial stages of front line action. It is significant that this work was completed without a fatality or serious injury in spite of the inexperience of the troops on high-steel. In recognition of this unsurpassed military engineering achievement, six Bronze Star Medals were awarded to individual members of the command.

     While the KIRA-CHON Bridge was being repaired, work was also being rushed on a tunnel which had been completely blocked and badly damaged by the explosion of an ammunition train within the tunnel just north of CHECHON. Exposed to the dangers of burned ammunition, severely cracked concrete tunnel lining and jagged debris of the train, members of the unit removed the wreckage and debris only after many hours of back-breaking and arduous work only to have the roof cave in completely at one point to such an extent that the ground above soared into the tunnel and blocked it again. Enlarging the hole which formed above ground was continued to prevent further sliding of material into the tunnel while emergency repairs were rapidly undertaken in the tunnel below. Although not as spectacular as the KIRA-CHON Bridge project, this work was equally as important and necessary in opening the railroad to WONJU as was the bridge repair; the temporary repairs were completed in an exceptionally meritorious manner under extremely hazardous conditions by the time the bridge was completed on 22 April 1951.

     The third important step in opening the railroad from CHECHON to WONJU involved the complete reconstruction of the WONJU railroad yards completely destroyed by bombing and the explosions of ammunition trains. From what had once been an original trackage of 10,000 feet, of which 9,000

[Top portion partially illegible due to fading/fold]

feet were destroyed, a new rail-head was constructed giving usable trackage of 22,000 feet. Storage and unloading areas were increased from 12 acres to 140 acres, and service roads were increased from 1300 feet to 26,000 feet. In addition loading ramps, electrical lighting systems and improved water-supply systems were installed. The bulk of this work was done between the latter part of April and June 1951. Because of being heavily committed on the KIRA-CHON Bridge and on tunnel repair, another unit completed preliminary work of clearing the yard of wreckage, initially begun by the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion, and the laying of track for partial operation of the yard. Immediately after rail traffic was reopened to WONJU the latter part of April 1951, the unit took over all work in the railhead and developed it to the dimensions enumerated above.

     As soon as the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion took over this work after it had so successfully completed its mission of reopening the railroad to WONJU, various service units immediately moved forward from CHECHON and clamored for depot areas and spur tracks for off-loading supplies. With the realhead and surrounding areas in the process of being graded, with streams of trucks arriving constantly for supplies and ammunition from the front, and with the rainy season approaching, it became essential that all-weather storage areas, spur tracks, and access roads be prepared all the more rapidly. Concentrating most of the battalion on this new crisis, members of the battalion worked unceasingly to keep up with the ever increasing demands. By their exceptional devotion to duty, tireless effort, and outstandingly efficient operations the members of the battalion constructed what is now recognized as a model railhead and certainly the most outstanding in south central Korea. As a result of this well-planned, completely gravelled and well-drained facility, the speed in unloading trains and handling of supplies was so increased over what had previously existed or was thought possible in other railheads that this effect alone on the logistical support for X Corps was of inestimable value in the combat efficiency of forward units.

     While concentrating on the urgent work in the WONJU railhead, orders were received by the battalion to expedite its efforts in the opening of the railroad between WONJU and SEOUL, a distance of 68 miles. This portion of the railroad had to be reopened without delay since it was parallel to the rear boundaries of both I and IX Corps along the HAN River and, in view of the poor road net in those areas, would increase immensely the delivery of supplies to units deployed immediately north of the railroad and assist in expediting a build-up for defense against an expected attack by the CCF down the PUKHAN River valley.

     Working from WONJU westwardly and from SEOUL eastwardly toward the PUKHAN River, the Battalion was faced with the formidable task of reconstructing 15 railroad bridges and relaying approximately 150,000 feet of rails and 70,000 ties destroyed by UN forces in previous withdrawals. Upon the collapse of the 6th ROKA Division in early May, the work was completely interrupted and units of the Battalion were forced to withdraw rapidly to WONJU. Immediately after the tactical situation was stabilized, the battalion resumed its work from WONJU toward the PUKHAN River,

the portion from SEOUL to the PUKHAN River being assigned to another Group in the meantime by the Army Engineer.

     In the space of thirty-five days, 15 April-20 May 1951, two major bridges west of WONJU were reconstructed and the track repaired opening the railroad 25 miles from WONJU to CHIPYONG-NI immediately behind the 7th US Division. By 2 June 1951, work was completed on three more bridges and the track was rebuilt to open the railroad to LANGTONG [YANGPYONG?], 35 miles out of WONJU, immediately behind the revitalized 6th ROKA Division. By 17 July 1951, the track and three bridges west of LANGTONG [YANGPYONG?] were reconstructed making the railroad operational to the PUKHAN River Bridge, 48 miles from WONJU. In this prodigious effort between WONJU and the PUKHAN River, the battalion had reconstructed 10 concrete piers, utilizing native contractors and had transported and launched a total of 14 girders in spans previously demolished in eight major bridges; nearly 48 miles of track had been completely rebuilt as well as rehabilitation of three small railyards at CHIPYONG-NI, YONGMUN, and YANGPYONG.

     The exceptionally meritorious accomplishment of opening the railroad first from CHECHON to WONJU, and then immediately beyond to the PUKHAN River, contributed immeasurably to the logistical support and combat efficiency of tactical units during the critical CCF spring offensives. The battalion completed this mission under the most adverse conditions of weather and enemy activity. Although not trained or even equipped for railroad construction work, the unit--through the diligence, devotion to duty, and exceptional efficiency of its members--rendered an outstanding measure of support to tactical units by expediting the flow of supplies forward by rail to supply points which could now be considerably closer to the front. The many problems engendered by inadequate supplies of construction materials and equipment, inaccessability of bridges and rail, inexperience of troops, working under pressure of deadlines, were faced with resourcefulness, skill and determination by the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion.

     The terrain in the zone of responsibility of the 32d Engineer Construction Group and assigned units in east central Korea was much more rugged than on the western side of the peninsula due to the SOEBEAK [SAEBEAK?] range and major spurs extending therefrom generally westwardly across routes of communication, chiefly the SOEBEAK [SAEBEAK?] range. As a result, the units had more mountain passes to contend with on the MSR and laterals than did units working along the western side of Korea.

     The first major pass encountered, as units progressed northerly in the early phases of operation in February and March 1951, was the incomparable TANYANG PASS, the severest bottleneck on the X Corps MSR between PUSAN and WONJU. Extending a distance of 16 miles between PUNGGI and TANYANG, were 12 miles of dangerous narrow, crooked, mountainous bench road the highest and longest one-way mountain pass in central Korea on an MSR. At the bottom of many ravines, and at the foot of the vertical cliffs and high banks around which the narrow and treacherous road was originally built by the Japanese, were many wrecked ve-

hicles of all types which had plunged from the over-dangerous road. With barely enough room for one vehicle, disaster had occurred repeatedly when vehicles tried to pass one another or when vehicles had skidded on the ice and snow during winter months. It was not unusual for convoys to spend from 2 to 4 hours to cross the pass including time waiting for traffic.

     The 439th Engineer Construction Battalion was the first unit of the Group to tackle TANYANG Pass. Due to the short time it remained in the Pass in March 1951, prior to proceeding north on aforementioned railroad work, the unit was engaged in 24-hour operations of keeping traffic open and blasting away some of the many critical points. Hampered by snow, ice, and mud this unit was solely responsible for moving the large volume of traffic over the pass during the critical fighting immediately to the north. Working without any let-up, in blizzards, driving sleet and extremely low temperatures, and displaying superior devotion to duty, vitally needed supply convoys proceeding to CHECHON and beyond were able to get through the pass in spite of the treacherous road and frequent dangerous land slides which blocked the route several times during the day and night. Elements of the 10th NKPA Division then recently cut-off in the TANYANG area frequently harassed troops working in the pass compounding the extremely dangerous and hazardous conditions already existing. The 439th Engineer Construction Battalion displayed such indomitable courage and aggressiveness in the face of seemingly insurmountable difficulties during this early period in its operation as to win the respect and admiration of all units it assisted.

     Being fully committed on critical railroad work during the next few months, the Battalion's next major project on the MSR did not occur until in June and July 1951. Between CHECHON and WONJU, approximately six miles south of WONJU, another relatively short but extremely treacherous defile existed being famous for its short-radius reverse curves through and around a rocky promontory and deep gorge. The battalion undertook the construction of a completely new section of road to eliminate this defile. This required a deep cut through rock of some 35 feet for a distance of nearly 1000 feet, followed immediately by a fill of 50,000 cubic yards at a height of 30 feet across the mouth of a narrow gorge. Completion of this project provided a two-way, class 50, roadway eliminating delays to traffic of from 1 to 4 hours which previously occurred on the X Corps MSR between CHECHON and WONJU. The notable feature of this project was the unprecedented and bold method employed in completely eliminating the defile rather than attempt to improve the existing road. Beyond the capabilities of many engineer units, the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion immediately recognized the challenge presented and with incomparable aggressiveness, exceptional efficiency, and remarkable resourcefulness, completed the project in an outstandingly superior manner between 1 June and 23 July 1951.

     In subsequent shifts of responsibility to the north of WONJU in late summer 1951, the Battalion quickly eliminated extreme curves in the five-mile pass south of HONGCHON and then devoted much of its effort in the pass just south of HANGYE on the road from HONGCHON toINJE.

This latter pass, known as HANGYE PASS, consisted of a one-way, four-mile, northbound route and a similar length two-way southbound route containing grades as high as 16 percent. Obviously, with cold weather approaching, a very serious interruption of traffic would occur if traffic were precluded from using the southbound route due to icing conditions, and were forced to use the one-way northbound route of lesser grades but subject to dangerous slides and complicated by many hair-pin turns.

     Accordingly, the battalion in the late fall undertook development of HANGYE PASS northbound route into a two-way, class 50 highway. Here again it was necessary to construct radical new alignments in several places to provide the desired results. A fill of a record height of over 135 feet was constructed in one location to eliminate a one-way horse-shoe curve benched around a steep cliff high above the HONGCHON River. Displaying the ultimate in aggressiveness, professional skill, and applying construction principles gained from many years of experience, the members of the battalion completed this urgently needed road reconstruction in time to eliminate the danger of complete traffic breakdown in freezing weather.

STATISTICAL SUMMARY OF MAJOR TASKS PERFORMED

     1. 19 railroad bridges were rebuilt in support of the Korean National Railway on the KYONGGYONG or ANDONG Line, which included reconstructing 23 concrete piers, launching and placing 19 steel girders at a total length of 1240 lineal feet.

     2. 16 highway bridges were constructed, reconstructed, or repaired. Total linear feet of bridging amounted to 1421 feet.

     3. 4 tunnels were repaired in support of the Korean National Railway.

     4. Since 8 February 1951, the Battalion has maintained and repaired 322 miles of MSR and lateral roads.

ADMINISTRATIVE DATA FOR PERIOD 8 FEBRUARY-31 DECEMBER 1951

     1. During above period, the unit had an average enlisted strength of 791, operating 10 percent understrength. Officer strength averaged 26, or 3 percent below strength. Warrant officer strength averaged 2 being 88 percent understrength.

     2. Record of absences without leave: Total of 4

     3. Convictions by Court-Martial: Total of 8

     4. Punishments under AW 104 (Art 15 UCMJ): Total of 79

     5. Venereal disease rate: Total number of cases 88

     6. Awards to personnel:

          Soldiers Medal          1
          Bronze Star             7
          Purple Heart            1
          Commendation Ribbon     1
          Good Conduct Medal     27

PROPOSED CITATION FOR MERITORIOUS UNIT COMMENDATION

     The 439th ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION is cited for exceptionally meritorious conduct in the performance of outstanding services in Korea in support of combat operations during the period 8 February to 31 December 1951. Responsible for reconstructing, improving, and maintaining the severely damaged routes of supply both railway and highway supporting X Corps and attached units, the 439th ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION accomplished its vital missions under the most adverse condition of weather and enemy activity and in spite of critical shortages of materials and supplies. Through the diligence, professional ability, and exceptional devotion to duty of its members, the battalion's unprecedented reconstruction of the highest steel railroad bridge in the history of military engineering at KIRA-CHON, Korea, was an extraordinary achievement permitting timely and greatly increased support to both IX and X Corps by opening the railroad to WONJU during the 1951 spring offensive of the enemy. In spite of seemingly insurmountable difficulties, the outstanding resourcefulness and exemplary performance of its members enabled the rebuilding of the railroad on beyond WONJU to the PUKHAN River. This successful and rapid completion of railroad reconstruction permitted supply points to be relocated many miles closer to the front producing timely and measurable effects on the combat efficiency of forward units. The unit's outstanding construction abilities and professional skills accomplished major improvements in the X Corps MSR in some of the most rugged terrain in Korea. The many problems engendered by inclement weather, moving of vitally needed construction equipment and ma-

terials over inferior roads, and harassment by guerrilla forces, were faced with resourcefulness, competence and determination by the members of the Battalion. The 439th ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION displayed such outstanding devotion to duty in the superior performance of exceptionally difficult tasks as to set it apart and above other units with similar missions. The determination, ability, aggressiveness, and esprit de corps of members of this Battalion reflect great credit on themselves, the Corps of Engineers, and the military service of the United States.

HEADQUARTERS
439TH ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION
APO 301

SUBJECT: Appreciation

TO : Cpl. John R. Hanifen
     439th Engineer Construction Battalion

     Attached for your "remembrances" is a copy of a "NARRATIVE SUMMARY OF EXCEPTIONALLY MERITORIOUS PERFORMANCE OF OUTSTANDING SERVICE" by our Battalion for our 1951 Korean tour. You may well be justly proud of your personal contribution to the great team that in fourteen short months has, without the benefit of any Unit Training whatsoever, accomplished many construction missions seldom if ever equalled in military annals.

     Not content to rest on the laurels of your 1951 construction feats, your constant devotion to duty, careful planning, and untiring manual efforts have carried you to even higher military construction heights. Your contribution to the brilliantly successful launching of the two 850 foot, 375 ton girders over the treacherous Han River and your professional manner of construction on Korea's longest highway bridge over the Som River has made the "439th" a name to be long remembered and discussed wherever Military Engineers might gather.

     Mere words will not even begin to express my sincere admiration and appreciation for your historic efforts, therefore, a very inadequate, but none-the-less heartfelt "THANK YOU" for your individual contribution to the important accomplishments of our Battalion will have to suffice.

                              [signature]
                         M. C. MILLER
                         Lt Colonel   CE
                         Commanding
