After a three-week stop over in Japan during which time additional equipment was procured, the unit finally arrived in PUSAN, KOREA, on 8 February 1951.

     Finding itself in a bleak, desolate, and mountainous combat zone relatively untrained, untried, and yet to see most of its big equipment for the first time, the members of the battalion nevertheless approached their assigned tasks eagerly and aggressively and were destined to show that this battalion would live up to the expectations of the Department of Army that, through its Organized Reserve Corps program, an Active Reserve unit, particularly in the Corps of Engineers, could readily adapt itself to arduous field service in an acute emergency.

     Almost immediately after its arrival in PUSAN harbor, members of the unit worked assiduously day and night in adverse weather and under the trying dockside bedlam, to off-load its equipment. After a brief period of staging outside PUSAN, the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion became one of the first units to be operational under the 32d Engineer Construction Group with responsibility for reconstructing and improving, initially between ANDONG and SEOUL, the badly damaged railroad, and the completely inadequate MSR between ANDONG and TANYANG, both being vital supply routes directly supporting X Corps and attached units fighting then in the vicinity of WONJU.

     In spite of extremely hazardous roads, inclement weather, and constant threats from guerrilla attacks along the MSR, this unit disregarded the advice, subsequently proved erroneous, of personnel in higher headquarters in Japan and EUSAK that it was foolhardy and virtually impossible to move large engineer equipment forward from PUSAN on the MSR, and proceeded to move its equipment forward in one of the most outstand-

[continued page 2]

ing achievements of the kind in Korea. The battalion at this time, February-March 1951, although battle lines were in turmoil, now found itself deep in X Corps area frequently ahead of elements of combat engineer units and in the close proximity of division CP's. Combat engineer commanders working slightly ahead on the MSR were astounded to see such quantities of large and vitally needed equipment in Corps but alone being so far forward in the Corps area.

     The extraordinary manner in which the Battalion organized itself after landing in Korea and the determination, aggressive spirit, and outstanding ability of its personnel quickly set it apart and above other units with similar missions.

     With the vital ANDONG Railroad line, the only railroad in eastern and central Korea connecting PUSAN with SEOUL through ULSAN, YONGCHON, CHECHON, and WONJU, in a serious state of repair, many of its destroyed bridges being expeditiously but unsafely repaired by Koreans, several tunnels either blocked or severely damaged, and major river crossings being made on temporary low-level structures threatened by floods at any time, rail traffic supporting the central front in March 1951 was very uncertain and could proceed with caution only as far north as CHECHON. With the front pushing ahead slowly approximately 30 to 40 miles to the north, opening of the WONJU railhead 20 miles to the north was of supreme importance to the tactical situation. In the meantime all supplies had to be moved forward by truck from CHECHON over an unimproved, rough and time-consuming road. The opening of the railroad to WONJU would mean the location and rapid expansion of supply dumps closer to the front, would permit evacuation of wounded by rail nearer to the front, and would eliminate truck transportation over 35 miles of narrow, rough road including a 7-mile one-way mountain pass. In effect supplies would be 4 hours closer to the front when the railroad could again operate into WONJU.

     Accordingly, the most vital of all Group missions was given to the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion to open the railroad to WONJU in three weeks. To do this, three major tasks presented themselves; first, the reconstruction of the KIRA-CHON Bridge, the highest railroad bridge of its kind in Korea spanning a deep valley to connect two tunnels on either side; second, the clearing of a tunnel blocked completely by the explosion of an ammunition train in the tunnel itself; and third, the rebuilding of the WONJU railyard lying in complete shambles just six miles north of the KIRA-CHON Bridge.

     With the CCF threatening attack in April, the key to the herculean task of rapidly opening the railroad to WONJU was obviously the rapid reconstruction of the KIRA-CHON Bridge. This structure consists of ten spans, total length of 800 feet, its steel girders being supported by steel towers near the center of the bridge and concrete piers near the abutments. During the retreat of UN forces earlier in the war, two 103-foot high steel towers at the center of the bridge were destroyed by demolitions removing a total of 270 feet of steel girders and track.

     The plan of attack in rebuilding this vital structure consisted of [continued]

(a) remove debris of damaged towers, (b) erect new towers to facilitate erection of new towers by cranes still standing, (c) erect two new towers and transfer thereof portions of two other towers on still standing, (d) fabricate one continuous new girder 270 feet long, weighing 135 tons, from 40" I-beams, (e) launch the continuous beam on rollers across gaps between each tower, and (f) [jack?] the beam down into position.

     During the period 1-22 April 1951, utilizing two construction companies of the battalion supplemented by equipment and personnel from other units in the Corps, the unit rendered one of the most outstanding engineering achievements in Korea, officially recognized as being the highest railroad bridge repair in the history of military engineering, and became the first Military Engineers ever to launch a continuous beam of the record length of 270 feet weighing 135 tons.

     This unprecedented achievement was carried on under hazardous working conditions in cold, wet weather, subjecting the men working on steel over 100 feet in the air to the most severe tests. In spite of seemingly insurmountable problems, the members of the battalion by exemplary application of construction principles, through extraordinary devotion to duty working under severe pressure for long hours, completed this unparalleled engineering project in an exceptionally meritorious manner and in time to permit supplies to enter WONJU by rail during the crucial stages of front line action. It is significant that this work was completed without a fatality or serious injury in spite of the inexperience of the troops on high-steel. In recognition of this unsurpassed military engineering achievement, six Bronze Star Medals were awarded to individual members of the command.

     While the KIRA-CHON Bridge was being repaired, work was also being rushed on a tunnel which had been completely blocked and badly damaged by the explosion of an ammunition train within the tunnel just north of CHECHON. Exposed to the dangers of burned ammunition, severely cracked concrete tunnel lining and jagged debris of the train, members of the unit removed the wreckage and debris only after many hours of back-breaking and arduous work only to have the roof cave in completely at one point to such an extent that the ground above soared into the tunnel and blocked it again. Enlarging the hole which formed above ground was continued to prevent further sliding of material into the tunnel while emergency repairs were rapidly undertaken in the tunnel below. Although not as spectacular as the KIRA-CHON Bridge project, this work was equally as important and necessary in opening the railroad to WONJU as was the bridge repair; the temporary repairs were completed in an exceptionally meritorious manner under extremely hazardous conditions by the time the bridge was completed on 22 April 1951.

     The third important step in opening the railroad from CHECHON to WONJU involved the complete reconstruction of the WONJU railroad yards completely destroyed by bombing and the explosions of ammunition trains. From what had once been an original trackage of 10,000 feet, of which 9,000 feet were destroyed, a new rail-head was constructed giving usable trackage of 22,000 feet. Storage and unloading areas were increased from 12 acres to 140 acres, and service roads were increased from 1300 feet to 26,000 feet. In addition loading ramps, electrical lighting systems and improved water-supply systems were installed. The bulk of this work was done between the latter part of April and June 1951. Because of being heavily committed on the KIRA-CHON Bridge and on tunnel repair, another unit completed preliminary work of clearing the yard of wreckage, initially begun by the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion, and the laying of track for partial operation of the yard. Immediately after rail traffic was reopened to WONJU the latter part of April 1951, the unit took over all work in the railhead and developed it to the dimensions enumerated above.

     As soon as the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion took over this work after it had so successfully completed its mission of reopening the railroad to WONJU, various service units immediately moved forward from CHECHON and clamored for depot areas and spur tracks for off-loading supplies. With the realhead and surrounding areas in the process of being graded, with streams of trucks arriving constantly for supplies and ammunition from the front, and with the rainy season approaching, it became essential that all-weather storage areas, spur tracks, and access roads be prepared all the more rapidly. Concentrating most of the battalion on this new crisis, members of the battalion worked unceasingly to keep up with the ever increasing demands. By their exceptional devotion to duty, tireless effort, and outstandingly efficient operations the members of the battalion constructed what is now recognized as a model railhead and certainly the most outstanding in south central Korea. As a result of this well-planned, completely gravelled and well-drained facility, the speed in unloading trains and handling of supplies was so increased over what had previously existed or was thought possible in other railheads that this effect alone on the logistical support for X Corps was of inestimable value in the combat efficiency of forward units.

     While concentrating on the urgent work in the WONJU railhead, orders were received by the battalion to expedite its efforts in the opening of the railroad between WONJU and SEOUL, a distance of 68 miles. This portion of the railroad had to be reopened without delay since it was parallel to the rear boundaries of both I and IX Corps along the HAN River and, in view of the poor road net in those areas, would increase immensely the delivery of supplies to units deployed immediately north of the railroad and assist in expediting a build-up for defense against an expected attack by the CCF down the PUKHAN River valley.

     Working from WONJU westwardly and from SEOUL eastwardly toward the PUKHAN River, the Battalion was faced with the formidable task of reconstructing 15 railroad bridges and relaying approximately 150,000 feet of rails and 70,000 ties destroyed by UN forces in previous withdrawals. Upon the collapse of the 6th ROKA Division in early May, the work was completely interrupted and units of the Battalion were forced to withdraw rapidly to WONJU. Immediately after the tactical situation was stabilized, the battalion resumed its work from WONJU toward the PUKHAN River,

the portion from SEOUL to the PUKHAN River being assigned to another Group in the meantime by the Army Engineer.

     In the space of thirty-five days, 15 April-20 May 1951, two major bridges west of WONJU were reconstructed and the track repaired opening the railroad 25 miles from WONJU to CHIPYONG-NI immediately behind the 7th US Division. By 2 June 1951, work was completed on three more bridges and the track was rebuilt to open the railroad to LANGTONG [YANGPYONG?], 35 miles out of WONJU, immediately behind the revitalized 6th ROKA Division. By 17 July 1951, the track and three bridges west of LANGTONG [YANGPYONG?] were reconstructed making the railroad operational to the PUKHAN River Bridge, 48 miles from WONJU. In this prodigious effort between WONJU and the PUKHAN River, the battalion had reconstructed 10 concrete piers, utilizing native contractors and had transported and launched a total of 14 girders in spans previously demolished in eight major bridges; nearly 48 miles of track had been completely rebuilt as well as rehabilitation of three small railyards at CHIPYONG-NI, YONGMUN, and YANGPYONG.

     The exceptionally meritorious accomplishment of opening the railroad first from CHECHON to WONJU, and then immediately beyond to the PUKHAN River, contributed immeasurably to the logistical support and combat efficiency of tactical units during the critical CCF spring offensives. The battalion completed this mission under the most adverse conditions of weather and enemy activity. Although not trained or even equipped for railroad construction work, the unit--through the diligence, devotion to duty, and exceptional efficiency of its members--rendered an outstanding measure of support to tactical units by expediting the flow of supplies forward by rail to supply points which could now be considerably closer to the front. The many problems engendered by inadequate supplies of construction materials and equipment, inaccessability of bridges and rail, inexperience of troops, working under pressure of deadlines, were faced with resourcefulness, skill and determination by the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion.

     The terrain in the zone of responsibility of the 32d Engineer Construction Group and assigned units in east central Korea was much more rugged than on the western side of the peninsula due to the SOEBEAK [SAEBEAK?] range and major spurs extending therefrom generally westwardly across routes of communication, chiefly the SOEBEAK [SAEBEAK?] range. As a result, the units had more mountain passes to contend with on the MSR and laterals than did units working along the western side of Korea.

     The first major pass encountered, as units progressed northerly in the early phases of operation in February and March 1951, was the incomparable TANYANG PASS, the severest bottleneck on the X Corps MSR between PUSAN and WONJU. Extending a distance of 16 miles between PUNGGI and TANYANG, were 12 miles of dangerous narrow, crooked, mountainous bench road the highest and longest one-way mountain pass in central Korea on an MSR. At the bottom of many ravines, and at the foot of the vertical cliffs and high banks around which the narrow and treacherous road was originally built by the Japanese, were many wrecked ve-

hicles of all types which had plunged from the over-dangerous road. With barely enough room for one vehicle, disaster had occurred repeatedly when vehicles tried to pass one another or when vehicles had skidded on the ice and snow during winter months. It was not unusual for convoys to spend from 2 to 4 hours to cross the pass including time waiting for traffic.

     The 439th Engineer Construction Battalion was the first unit of the Group to tackle TANYANG Pass. Due to the short time it remained in the Pass in March 1951, prior to proceeding north on aforementioned railroad work, the unit was engaged in 24-hour operations of keeping traffic open and blasting away some of the many critical points. Hampered by snow, ice, and mud this unit was solely responsible for moving the large volume of traffic over the pass during the critical fighting immediately to the north. Working without any let-up, in blizzards, driving sleet and extremely low temperatures, and displaying superior devotion to duty, vitally needed supply convoys proceeding to CHECHON and beyond were able to get through the pass in spite of the treacherous road and frequent dangerous land slides which blocked the route several times during the day and night. Elements of the 10th NKPA Division then recently cut-off in the TANYANG area frequently harassed troops working in the pass compounding the extremely dangerous and hazardous conditions already existing. The 439th Engineer Construction Battalion displayed such indomitable courage and aggressiveness in the face of seemingly insurmountable difficulties during this early period in its operation as to win the respect and admiration of all units it assisted.

     Being fully committed on critical railroad work during the next few months, the Battalion's next major project on the MSR did not occur until in June and July 1951. Between CHECHON and WONJU, approximately six miles south of WONJU, another relatively short but extremely treacherous defile existed being famous for its short-radius reverse curves through and around a rocky promontory and deep gorge. The battalion undertook the construction of a completely new section of road to eliminate this defile. This required a deep cut through rock of some 35 feet for a distance of nearly 1000 feet, followed immediately by a fill of 50,000 cubic yards at a height of 30 feet across the mouth of a narrow gorge. Completion of this project provided a two-way, class 50, roadway eliminating delays to traffic of from 1 to 4 hours which previously occurred on the X Corps MSR between CHECHON and WONJU. The notable feature of this project was the unprecedented and bold method employed in completely eliminating the defile rather than attempt to improve the existing road. Beyond the capabilities of many engineer units, the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion immediately recognized the challenge presented and with incomparable aggressiveness, exceptional efficiency, and remarkable resourcefulness, completed the project in an outstandingly superior manner between 1 June and 23 July 1951.

     In subsequent shifts of responsibility to the north of WONJU in late summer 1951, the Battalion quickly eliminated extreme curves in the five-mile pass south of HONGCHON and then devoted much of its effort in the pass just south of HANGYE on the road from HONGCHON toINJE.

     This latter pass, known as HANGYE PASS, consisted of a one-way, four-mile, northbound route and a similar length two-way southbound route containing grades as high as 16 percent. Obviously, with cold weather approaching, a very serious interruption of traffic would occur if traffic were precluded from using the southbound route due to icing conditions, and were forced to use the one-way northbound route of lesser grades but subject to dangerous slides and complicated by many hair-pin turns.

     Accordingly, the battalion in the late fall undertook development of HANGYE PASS northbound route into a two-way, class 50 highway. Here again it was necessary to construct radical new alignments in several places to provide the desired results. A fill of a record height of over 135 feet was constructed in one location to eliminate a one-way horse-shoe curve benched around a steep cliff high above the HONGCHON River. Displaying the ultimate in aggressiveness, professional skill, and applying construction principles gained from many years of experience, the members of the battalion completed this urgently needed road reconstruction in time to eliminate the danger of complete traffic breakdown in freezing weather.

STATISTICAL SUMMARY OF MAJOR TASKS PERFORMED

     1. 19 railroad bridges were rebuilt in support of the Korean National Railway on the KYONGGYONG or ANDONG Line, which included reconstructing 23 concrete piers, launching and placing 19 steel girders at a total length of 1240 lineal feet.

     2. 16 highway bridges were constructed, reconstructed, or repaired. Total linear feet of bridging amounted to 1421 feet.

     3. 4 tunnels were repaired in support of the Korean National Railway.

     4. Since 8 February 1951, the Battalion has maintained and repaired 322 miles of MSR and lateral roads.

---

ADMINISTRATIVE DATA FOR PERIOD 8 FEBRUARY-31 DECEMBER 1951

     1. During above period, the unit had an average enlisted strength of 791, operating 10 percent understrength. Officer strength averaged 26, or 3 percent below strength. Warrant officer strength averaged 2 being 88 percent understrength.

     2. Record of absences without leave: Total of 4

     3. Convictions by Court-Martial: Total of 8

     4. Punishments under AW 104 (Art 15 UCMJ): Total of 79

     5. Venereal disease rate: Total number of cases 88

     6. Awards to personnel:

          Soldiers Medal          1
          Bronze Star             7
          Purple Heart            1
          Commendation Ribbon     1
          Good Conduct Medal     27

---

PROPOSED CITATION FOR MERITORIOUS UNIT COMMENDATION

     The 439th ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION is cited for exceptionally meritorious conduct in the performance of outstanding services in Korea in support of combat operations during the period 8 February to 31 December 1951. Responsible for reconstructing, improving, and maintaining the severely damaged routes of supply both railway and highway supporting X Corps and attached units, the 439th ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION accomplished its vital missions under the most adverse condition of weather and enemy activity and in spite of critical shortages of materials and supplies. Through the diligence, professional ability, and exceptional devotion to duty of its members, the battalion's unprecedented reconstruction of the highest steel railroad bridge in the history of military engineering at KIRA-CHON, Korea, was an extraordinary achievement permitting timely and greatly increased support to both IX and X Corps by opening the railroad to WONJU during the 1951 spring offensive of the enemy. In spite of seemingly insurmountable difficulties, the outstanding resourcefulness and exemplary performance of its members enabled the rebuilding of the railroad on beyond WONJU to the PUKHAN River. This successful and rapid completion of railroad reconstruction permitted supply points to be relocated many miles closer to the front producing timely and measurable effects on the combat efficiency of forward units. The unit's outstanding construction abilities and professional skills accomplished major improvements in the X Corps MSR in some of the most rugged terrain in Korea. The many problems engendered by inclement weather, moving of vitally needed construction equipment and ma-

terials over inferior roads, and harassment by guerrilla forces, were faced with resourcefulness, competence and determination by the members of the Battalion. The 439th ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION BATTALION displayed such outstanding devotion to duty in the superior performance of exceptionally difficult tasks as to set it apart and above other units with similar missions. The determination, ability, aggressiveness, and esprit de corps of members of this Battalion reflect great credit on themselves, the Corps of Engineers, and the military service of the United States.
letters.summary: This document is a formal appreciation letter from Lt. Colonel M. C. Miller, Commanding Officer of the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion, addressed to Cpl. John R. Hanifen, acknowledging his personal contributions to the battalion's remarkable service during the 1951 Korean War tour. Accompanying the letter is a comprehensive 10-page Narrative Summary documenting the battalion's exceptionally meritorious performance from 8 February to 31 December 1951, including the historic reconstruction of the KIRA-CHON railroad bridge (the highest railroad bridge repair in military engineering history at that time), opening of the railroad to WONJU, TANYANG and HANGYE Pass road improvements, and extensive railroad and highway reconstruction supporting X Corps operations. The narrative concludes with statistical summaries showing 19 railroad bridges rebuilt, 16 highway bridges constructed or repaired, 4 tunnels repaired, and 322 miles of roads maintained, along with a Proposed Citation for Meritorious Unit Commendation. The letter specifically mentions Cpl. Hanifen's contribution to launching two 850-foot, 375-ton girders over the Han River and constructing Korea's longest highway bridge over the Som River, making the "439th" famous in military engineering circles.

metadata.from: Bob (Robert) Hanifen — signed 'Bobby,' return address shows 'Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17106[illegible]'
metadata.to: James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad) — addressed to Hanifen Body & Paint Co.

metadata.envelope_notes: Return address: Pvt. Bob Hanifen 1710600[?], Sqd. T-BKS-249, Chanute Fld. Ill. Marked 'Free' (military free postage). Envelope is worn and torn on one edge.
metadata.mood_tone: Casual and slightly dramatic; Bob recounts his dental experience with humor and some anxiety, then updates his father on his military training.
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but some words are difficult — 'Plepiclas' on page 3 is unclear (possibly 'Plexiglas'). Some staining on page 1 partially obscures text. The word 'Intra' (extra?) is ambiguous.
metadata.historical_context: Written July 1945 during WWII. Bob is stationed at Chanute Field, Illinois (a major Army Air Forces technical training base). He is learning aircraft repair skills including fixing bullet holes, consistent with wartime aviation mechanic training.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad

Well got another brainstorm today an decided to go to the Dentist. I went at 1300 and sat down in the waiting room. In about 6 min. Called me in and looked over my teeth. Said take an X-ray of his wisdom teeth and that Intra [extra?] tooth I got. The picture show their [illegible] the wisdom tooth was pushing in side way on the other tooth. In another had me in another room. The Doc

was an expert tooth puller. He numbed the gum got a knife, tooth pullers, chisels, everything you can think of. He really to went in about 20 min of cutting, chiseling, pulling he had it out and the Intra [extra?] one on top of that. You should see me all swelled up on one side. Come back tomorrow for a check and I will pull the other one in about 10 days, because the other wisdom tooth is doing the same thing. Boy I really stepped in it. It didn't hurt too bad, but it sure scared Hell out of me.

Have to go to school tonight at 7:00 so I hope it don't hurt too much. We are Plepiclas [Plexiglas?] now. Learning how to repair bullet holes and all that kind of stuff. Not too hard. Only hope now I can eat tonight. That what has been causing my head to ache lately.

Well goodbye for now.
Your Loving Son

Bobby

In another had me in another room. The Doc was an expert tooth puller. He numbed the gum got a knife, tooth pullers, chisels, everything you can think of. He really to went in about 20 min of cutting, chiseling, pulling he had it out and the Intra [extra?] one on top of that. You should see me all swelled up on one side. Come back tomorrow for a check and I will pull the other one in about 10 days, because the other wisdom tooth is doing the same thing. Boy I really stepped in it. It didn't hurt too bad, but it sure scared Hell out of me.

Have to go to school tonight at 7:00 so I hope it don't hurt too much. We are Plepiclas [Plexiglas?] now. Learning how to repair bullet holes and all that kind of stuff. Not too hard. Only hope now I can eat tonight. That what has been causing my head to ache lately.

letters.summary: Written from Chanute Field, Illinois in July 1945, Bob (Bobby) Hanifen writes to his father about an eventful trip to the dentist, where he had an impacted wisdom tooth and an extra tooth surgically removed. He describes the procedure with vivid detail — numbing, cutting, chiseling, and pulling — and notes he is left with one side of his face swollen, with another wisdom tooth to be pulled in about 10 days. He also briefly mentions his military technical training, noting they are now studying Plexiglas repair and learning how to fix bullet holes in aircraft.

topics: upcoming transfer to Keesler Field for tech school

metadata.mood_tone: Casual, upbeat, and conversational; Bob is keeping it brief due to lack of news but shares several interesting military experiences with good humor.
metadata.legibility_notes: Most text is legible but handwriting is moderately cursive and some words required guessing. 'Keesler Field' is somewhat unclear but deducible from context. 'Bks Bk02' unit designation on envelope partially obscured by postmark. 'Neo Way' address may be 'Leo Way' or similar — transcribed as written.
metadata.historical_context: WWII era; Bob is in Army Air Forces Basic Training at Sheppard Field, Texas. He mentions qualifying as a Marksman with a carbine and upcoming firing of the .45 cal pistol. He references an air show and a parade where officers received medals. His brother Jimmy is apparently stationed in Hawaii. Bob expects to transfer to Keesler Field for technical school around early June.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is from Sheppard Field, Texas (confirmed by return address: 'AAF - BTC - BKS - Bk02, Sqd I-3706-ABG, Sheppard Field'). Bob has qualified as Marksman with a carbine and will fire the .45 the next day, is going on bivouac 'next two' (weeks), mentions transferring to Keesler Field around 'the first of June,' and mentions a letter from Jimmy in Hawaii. Letter 71 (May 25, 1944, per the Friday May 25 date and context) also mentions qualifying with the .45 pistol and 31 days of basic training — 'my 31 day of basic and I only have 4 more days to go.' Letter 36's content (carbine Marksman, about to fire .45, bivouac upcoming) is earlier in basic training than Letter 71 (which describes just returning from bivouac and firing the .45 for score). Letter 36 is therefore from earlier in May 1944. The mention of sending Jimmy a letter to Hawaii and wanting to go to Keesler around June 1 is consistent. Estimated May 1944, approximately mid-May.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad
   Not doing much tonight because there is not much to do. There is a dance going on 1000 men & 30 women. I am a A.M. gunner, shall go to Keesler Field for tech school around the first of June. I am a Marksman with a Carbine, tomorrow we fire the 45 cal. pistol for score. Boy it is a nice gun. Just like the one you used to have. Go on bivouac next two. It almost snowed Mon. but today it is around 90° in the shade. What a difference.

Got a letter from Jimmy the other day. He says he really like Hawaii, wish I could say that. It's full of sand, boy does the wind ever blow around here. Got Wyoming beat a mile. Wish you would send that swimming suit. Hope Gram like the flowers. Well, that's all for now. Will write more later. Doing so much at once can remember all of it. Had a air show yesterday. Pretty pretty good. Was in a big parade today. A couple of officers got home medals. Boy the guys really kicked off in that sun. So long for now.

   Love Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes a short letter to his father from Sheppard Field, Texas, describing his military training activities. He has qualified as a Marksman with a carbine and will fire a .45 caliber pistol the next day, notes the wild weather swings in Texas (near snow one day, 90° the next), and mentions an air show and parade where officers received medals. He also reports that his brother Jimmy is in Hawaii and apparently enjoying it, asks his father to send his swimming suit, and mentions he expects to transfer to Keesler Field for technical school around the first of June.

metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is dated 'Sat. Morn. 9/10' with return address at Sheppard Field, Texas. Content describes post-WWII military life at Sheppard Field (June 1946 per metadata: Bob is still in the Army after the war's end). The discharge orders going to Denver are consistent with June 1946 demobilization. The 105° heat, 7½-mile hike, and dramatic storm match the metadata estimate of June 8, 1946. The '9/10' is likely the time (9:10 AM) not a date. Return address shows 'Pvt. Bob Hanifen, Sq. I-3716 ABU, Sheppard Field' — consistent with Bob back at Sheppard Field in 1946 after returning from Italy or being reassigned.

pages.transcription:
Sat. Morn. 9/10

Dear Dad

I am writing this on Sat morn. We were suppose to have a parade but they postpone it. It was really hot yesterday 105° in the shade we went on a 7½ mile [hike?] hike. Boy was it a long way. We came back and had a hour of drill, 3 hours of P.T. and Medical aid. We pract-ice artificial respiration. The ground was hot you could hardly lay on it. About 6:00 a storm started brewing and a few min. later it hit and I mean hit. The street was full to the curb with water and it was hailing. The wind was terrific it blew the water along in sheets. It really tore

up the camp. They a big outdoor theater and it turn it down a threw it clear across the drill field. It tore a lot roof off knock a lot of signs down. Went down town and there was one place where the bus went thru for about 50 yds. about 2 feet down. It really rained. We have another hike today 5 miles I guess. A lot more guys got orders today but no luck as yet. They are going to Denver for fire gunnery boy what a break for them guys. Well Dad goodby for now.

Your loving Son

Bob

letters.summary: Written on a Saturday morning from Sheppard Field, Texas, Bob describes an exhausting previous day that included a 7½-mile hike in 105° heat followed by drill, P.T., and first aid training including artificial respiration practice. He vividly recounts a powerful storm that struck around 6 PM, bringing hail, flooding, and violent winds that destroyed an outdoor theater and tore roofs off buildings. He notes that more fellow soldiers received discharge orders (headed to Denver for fire gunnery training), but he himself has not yet received orders, hinting at impatience to be released.

metadata.historical_context: Written during the Korean War era (October 1951). Milo's unit (443 Engineer Pipeline Company) was originally slated to deploy to France, likely as part of NATO buildup, but the deployment was delayed or redirected. Mentions of discharge and deployment reflect the active military mobilization of the early 1950s.

pages.transcription:
"Bob"
443 Engineer
Pipeline Company
                                        Oct. 30, 1951

Hi John!

    Long time no see. When you comeing [sic] home hot rod? if you stay over there much longer you will probable [sic] forget the old U.S.A. I was home on a delay in route and your dad gave me your address. I was suppose [sic] to go to France with this outfit but they couldn't get enough men to fill it up, so they sent another outfit, I guess we are leaveing [sic] sometime after New years. What are you doing now? still driving a truck. I sure will be glad the day we get out of this fucking ar[my], I get to hate it more ev[er]y day.

                                                                        over

Gene De young [sic] got his discharge a couple of weeks ago, he sure brought back a nice cunt from Long Beach. (lucky fucker) well John I'll close for now and write more later.

                        Your Buddie [sic]

                             Milo,

My address is:

    Pfc  Milo Olivier
    US  550 678 92
    443 Eng Pipeline Co
    Fort Leonard Wood
             Mo.

letters.summary: This is a letter dated October 30, 1951, written by Pfc Milo Olivier of the 443 Engineer Pipeline Company at Fort Leonard Wood, Missouri, to his friend John Hanifen. Milo reconnected with John after getting his address from John's father during a delay en route home, and writes to catch up, expressing frustration with army life and noting that his unit's planned deployment to France was postponed. He mentions that their mutual acquaintance Gene De Young recently received his discharge and returned home, and closes with his mailing address. The letter is notable for its candid, crude language reflecting the informal camaraderie of enlisted soldiers during the Korean War era.

metadata.historical_context: Written in May 1946 shortly after WWII ended; Bob appears to still be stationed or posted abroad (reference to Foggia, Italy, a major WWII air base); mentions war bonds and sending money orders home, consistent with wartime/post-war military pay practices.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is dated 'May 9, 1946' explicitly in the text ('Well this is the 1 of May' — likely a writing error for 'the 9th of May' given the date heading). Written on American Red Cross stationery, consistent with Bob's Italy posting. References Foggia (a major Italian air base Bob visited per Letters 18, 73, etc.), Jack Buckley, and the jeep — all consistent with Bob's Italy letters. The $59 money order referenced matches Letter 24 (June 19, 1946) which also mentions enclosing a $59 money order; however, the content differences suggest these are separate money orders at different times. The swimming trunks request and trip to Foggia with the Lt. are referenced in Letter 39 (May 9, 1946 per the date heading), while Letter 22 (also May 9, 1946) was sent separately. Both are from Caserta/Italy, May 1946. Confirmed as May 9, 1946.

pages.transcription:
May 9, 1946

AMERICAN RED CROSS

Dear Dad

     Well this is the 1 of May and everything is still okay. I got your card from Phena [?] to Kansas. What are you doing down there. By the way did you ever get my war bonds & my 59 dollars money order.

Another story how about sending me my swimming trunks, the blue ones with the Air Corps insignia on it. It takes about

a month for a package so send it right away. Please. We went to Foggia the other day the Lt. & my buddy in the office Jeko [?]. We drove my jeep. Had a nice time. Jack Buckley took us to the wrestling matches. It rain coming back and those black top Italian road are slick as hell enough so you can go into a slide pretty easy. Glad to get back. I am to Driving school this week

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from what appears to be a posting in Italy (referencing Foggia, a major WWII air base) in May 1946. He confirms everything is okay, asks whether his father received his war bonds and $59 money order, and requests his blue Air Corps swimming trunks be mailed promptly. He recounts a recent trip to Foggia with his lieutenant and office buddy, where Jack Buckley took them to wrestling matches, and notes the dangerous slickness of Italian roads in the rain on the drive back. The letter ends mentioning he is attending driving school that week.

metadata.from: Bob (Robert Hanifen) — signed 'Love Your Brother Bob'

metadata.envelope_notes: No envelope visible. Letter written on novelty stationery featuring a red martini glass graphic with the caption 'High Again Jan'
metadata.mood_tone: Casual, friendly, and mildly self-deprecating; upbeat despite financial constraints; humor about potentially changing his mind on sending the check
metadata.legibility_notes: Several words are difficult to read due to cursive style and ink flow; some amounts partially unclear (e.g., '$20.00' for tires, '$35.00' raise); the word 'lot' appears written as 'lot' with a strikethrough and rewrite; 'two' is written and then crossed out to correct; income tax amount reads as '137.49' with '.49' kept for necessity

pages.transcription:
March 26, 1952

Dear Jim & Helen & Jimmy III
& Rosemary

Well here I am again without too much news but with a lot of money for you. I am hopping [sic] to pay you all of the balance inside of two months. I got two tires last & tubes for $20.00 Goodyear. I know a guy who works for them. It is hell not being able to get everything wholesale like I used to. My Income Tax Ret. was 137.49. I am keeping the .49 for necessity. I haven't heard from John in some time. I sent some pictures of the new cars awhile ago. Haven't heard from anyone to far as that. I know the reason why only hoped you'd understand.

I am still with the The Texas Co. pencil pushing for a living haven't got a raise as yet. Jan got another job. It pays the same as mine with a $35.00 raise in 3 months. Well I guess I had better

send you the check before I change my mind.

Love Your Brother
Bob

letters.summary: In this March 1952 letter, Bob writes to his brother Jim and family promising to repay a debt, noting he hopes to pay the full balance within two months. He shares updates about purchasing Goodyear tires at a discount through a contact, receiving a $137.49 income tax refund (keeping only the $0.49 for himself), and continuing his job at The Texas Company. He humorously closes by saying he'd better send the check before he changes his mind.

Hanifen (US 55 046 284) to Private E-2 and forfeiting $65.00/month for 3 months. This is almost certainly Bob's brother John Hanifen, serving during the Korean War era. pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS (upper left):
B.A. Bob Hanifen 17170600
Sqd. T-BKS-249
Chanute Fld. Ill

POSTMARK: RANTOUL, ILL. / JUL 28 / 11 AM / 1945

UPPER RIGHT: free [military free frank]

ADDRESSED TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Fri.

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

Dear Dad

     Well I am thru with the dentist for awhile at last. Went down to see him Wed and he pulled the other tooth, today my jaw is swollen all over my face. Sorry you didn't come in last week. Why don't you drive over for Air Forces day. Aug. 30 it is going to be quite a show. I could ride back with you and have a pretty nice time at home. I was going to go to Bloomia [Bloomington?] this weekend but my face is all out of shape so I think I will stay in camp and take it easy. Was going to the show

today but it was closed when I got there. So I went twinning [sunning?] or I should say wading to get a better sun tan. Well that about all the news in my brain for now.

     Love Bobby

HEADQUARTERS
32D ENGINEER CONSTRUCTION GROUP
APO 301, c/o Postmaster
San Francisco, California

SPECIAL COURT MARTIAL                                    19 July 1952
ORDERS NUMBER     27

     Pursuant to the authority of paragraph 97, MCM, 1951, the unexecuted portion of the sentence to be reduced to the grade of Private E-2 and to forfeit $65.00 per month for 3 months in the case of Corporal John R. Hanifen, US 55 046 284, Company "C" [illegible] 9th Engineer Construction Battalion, APO 301, U.S. Army, as promulgated [in] Special Court Martial Order Number 26, this headquarters, 5 July 1952, is [omitted?].

BY ORDER OF COLONEL CRAMSIE:

OFFICIAL:                             DANIEL L SCHOENLY
                                      1st Lt         CE
[signature: Fred E. Harvey]           Adjutant
FRED E HARVEY
WOJG        USA
Asst Adjutant

DISTRIBUTION:
"A" Plus
  3 – TAG Washington 25, D.C.
  4 – CG EUSAK, KJA
 10 – Individual concerned
 10 – (Add) CO, 439th Engr Const Bn, APO 301

letters.summary: Written on a Friday in late July 1945, Bob writes to his father from Chanute Field, Illinois, reporting that he has finally finished with the dentist after having another tooth pulled, leaving his jaw swollen. He invites his dad to drive out for Air Forces Day on August 30, noting it will be a great show and Bob could ride home with him afterward. Because of his swollen face, Bob cancels a planned trip to Bloomington and spends the day in camp instead. A separate document stored with the letter is a 1952 Special Court Martial order concerning Corporal John R. Hanifen (almost certainly Bob's brother John), reducing him in grade and forfeiting pay while serving with the 32nd Engineer Construction Group in Korea.

[Return address, upper left]

[Postmark: RANTOUL, ILL. / JUL 3 / 11-PM / 1945]

[Recipient address, center]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
914 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Dear Dad

    Sorry I disappointed you but it's pretty far to Des Moines on the times we get off. This C shift don't give a fellow a lot of time over the weekend. I went to Chicago Sun and Mon. That's the reason why I never wrote. We got in no Dutch for going on the plane ride. Hell you can go to Calif. on a plane if you can get back in time. Just seen the Chanute Field baseball team tromp all Great Lakes. What a up set. By the way the word in that paragraph what does it mean. Well Dad took-

ing for now.

[illegible line — bleed-through text obscures content]

Love Bob

[decorative underline/flourish]

[Remainder of page is illegible due to heavy bleed-through from the other side of the sheet — ghost text from page 1 is visible but unreadable as distinct content on this side]
Would you guys mind if you put these pictures in my Service Record Book then I can save them O.K.?

letters.summary: Written in July 1945 from Chanute Field, Illinois, Bob writes to his father apologizing for not visiting Des Moines, explaining that his C shift schedule doesn't allow enough time off on weekends. He mentions a trip to Chicago and notes they got in no trouble for taking a plane ride. He excitedly reports that the Chanute Field baseball team beat Great Lakes in what he calls a big upset. A separate small note included with the letter asks his family to put some pictures in his Service Record Book for safekeeping.

metadata.mood_tone: Energetic and informative; mildly fatigued from testing but upbeat; hopeful about passing flight physical; shows awareness of impending Pacific deployment as Germany winds down
metadata.legibility_notes: Several lines partially obscured by staining, foxing, and fold damage; some words guessed from context. The middle section of page 1 is particularly difficult due to bleed-through and staining. Some words remain illegible.
metadata.historical_context: Written during WWII, likely spring 1945 given reference to Germany being 'almost over' and anticipation of Pacific deployment. References to Drake Relays (spring athletic event in Des Moines) and Mother's Day help narrow timing to May 1945. Gas mask training and military classification tests reflect wartime military training context.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob mentions mental classification tests, upcoming flight physical and psychomotor tests, gas mask day, Drake Relay results (held late April in Des Moines), Mother's Day flowers sent to grandmother, and word from Jimmy that 'San Francisco, Calif. That's the gateway to the Pacific. That is where we are going I guess. Seeing that Germany is almost over with.' V-E Day was May 8, 1945; 'Germany is almost over' suggests this was written in late April or very early May 1945, before the German surrender. The Drake Relays and Mother's Day both fall in May. This fits within Bob's Sheppard Field training period, consistent with Letters 21 (April 23, 1945, from Sheppard Field) and 52 (June 1, 1945, also Sheppard Field). Estimated May 1945, likely first or second week.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad,

Well the fire works really started yesterday. We started our Mental classification tests & solid hours on everything you hope to think of. I didn't know there was so many different kinds of tests. My head actually [ached?] after we got through.

[Today?] we take our flight physical and the psychomotor tests which takes about 1 or 2 hours. It is a [coordination?] all kind of different machines just like playing with a pin ball machine. Boy is it ever [hot?] down here.

I one of guys who never took the [test?] had to run the obstacle course [and was] after I [illegible] [sit out?] [illegible] from [illegible] listening to an instructor about the carbine rifle. We will have to do it too in a couple days from now. Even during a break they wouldn't let them [illegible] [illegible]. Those guys actually looked black when they got [through?]

If you could find Friday's paper or Sat. with the Drake [R]elay results I wish you would send it. Especially Friday's ones with the high school results. Today is Gas mask day — everybody has to wear them. Never know when they are going to throw a little gas. What a place. I sent some flowers to Drammaw for Mother's Day. Hope she likes them. I received Jimmy [news?] — [word?] yesterday. San Francisco, Calif. That the [gateway to the?] Pacific. That is where we are going I guess. [Seeing?] that Germany is almost over with. Say to [illegible] for me and [illegible]. I am thinking of her. Tell Lester to get on the ball too. Well goodbye for now. I will write again as soon as I find out if I passed my flight physical.

Love, Bob

Today is Gas mask day — everybody has to wear them. Never know when they are going to throw a little gas. What a place. I sent some flowers to Drammaw for Mother's Day. Hope she likes them. I received Jimmy [news?] — [word?] yesterday. San Francisco, Calif. That the [gateway to the?] Pacific. That is where we are going I guess. [Seeing?] that Germany is almost over with. Say to [illegible] for me and [illegible]. I am thinking of her. Tell Lester to get on the ball too. Well goodbye for now. I will write again as soon as I find out if I passed my flight physical.

Love, Bob
letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from a military training base, describing an intensive day of mental classification tests, upcoming flight physical and psychomotor evaluations, and carbine rifle instruction. He references Gas mask day, Drake Relay results he'd like clippings of, and flowers he sent to his grandmother for Mother's Day. Most significantly, Bob notes he has received word that San Francisco will be their gateway to the Pacific theater, anticipating deployment there as Germany is nearly defeated, and says he will write again once he learns whether he passed his flight physical.

pages.transcription:
April 21, 1952

Dear Jim & Helen

Not doing to much right now just had today off. Had the car worked on today and it is running pretty good now. The [illegible] valve needed grinding so I had it done. Say who do you have insurance with by the way. I am carrying mine with Hartford. I don't know if I am nuts or not. The damn [stuff?] raise $20.95 since last year. How about seeing how the stuff cost in Iowa. I must be living in a high price town. I am sending you the bal. of the car payment real soon. Write and tell me how much time I got left. Or has it expired already. I know you are holding the bag on it, and I sure appreciate your helping me. I hope I can do something for you someday. I am getting on my feet now and hope to pay off everybody I owe real soon. Write and tell me what kind of Ins. you have. Send some pictures of the [the?] kids one of these

days if you have anything. Well goodbye for now and write and write me up, okay

Love Your Brother
Bob

P.S. Here is my old policy and the new rates are alongside of it.

letters.summary: In this April 1952 letter, Bob writes to his brother Jim and Jim's wife Helen with a casual, financially-focused update. He had a day off and got his car repaired — the valves needed grinding. He inquires about Jim and Helen's insurance carrier, comparing his Hartford policy unfavorably after a $20.95 rate increase, and muses that he must be living in a high-cost town compared to Iowa. Bob acknowledges that Jim has been financially helping him with a car payment, thanks him warmly, and promises to send the balance soon and pay off all his debts as he gets back on his feet. He encloses his old insurance policy with new rates noted alongside, and asks for photos of the kids.

pages.transcription:
Mon, 13
time 6:30

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

Dear Dad
  Just got back from a wonderful weekend in Peoria, with my buddie Hank. We stayed at his aunt house over the weekend. We left camp at 2:00 and rode into Champaign in a trailer and it rained all the way. We started hitchhiking at 3:00 and at 8:00 we finally got a ride out of time. Arrived in Peoria around 11:00.

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

Hank cousin took us to church at 12:00, came back and went out to the golf courses to Melanie's [?] father, came back for dinner, had a swell feed, took a 2 hr. nap and Melanie [?] fixed us up with some girls. We all went on a picnic had a swell time, seen a lot of good golf at the golf courses, lot of Pros were there for a big tournament. Joe Kirkwood the trick shot artist was

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

there, He put on a [crossed out word] swell show. We then went to the show and got to bed around 1:00 never got up till 10:30 had a nice breakfast and left for the fld. got back at 4:30.
  I'm going to come home this week if everything goes right. How is the 37 Ply, did you ever put in that new motor. This guy is going to Ft. Daly [?] but he is not coming back. I come make expense and some profit by picking

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

up four guys in Iowa City, 2 bucks apiece. Write and tell me all about it will you. Well Dad goodbye for now. And I will write again real soon. I don't know what the war ending will affect me. Probably will get 2 years in the Islands as occupation army. Sure hope not. So Dad I see your
  Son Bobby

letters.summary: Written on Monday, August 13, 1945, from Chanute Field, Illinois, Bob Hanifen writes to his father at the family auto body business in Des Moines to describe a wonderful weekend pass to Peoria with his Army buddy Hank. The two hitchhiked to Peoria, stayed at Hank's aunt's house, attended church, visited a golf tournament featuring trick-shot artist Joe Kirkwood, went on a picnic with girls, and saw a show. Bob mentions plans to come home that week, asks about a '37 Plymouth and its new motor, and notes he can make some money picking up riders to Iowa City. The letter closes with Bob expressing uncertainty about his postwar fate, worrying he may be assigned two years of occupation duty 'in the Islands' following the war's end.

metadata.historical_context: Letter written April 15, 1945 — the day after President Franklin D. Roosevelt died (April 12, 1945). Bob mentions the president's death and a cancelled dance on account of it. He is at Fort Leavenworth preparing to ship out to Texas. World War II is still ongoing at this point.

pages.transcription:
Return address (upper left):
Pvt. B.B. Hanifen
Army Air Corps
Leavenworth, Kans.

Upper right: free

Postmark: FORT LEAVENWORTH / APR 15 / 11 AM / 1945 / KANS.

Addressed to:
James E. Hanifen
904 Leo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Dear Dad

Well the army life is just what they it is, just what they make it. We get up at 4:30 in morning, mess around a while then do a little detail. All we did today was to up a few cigarette butts and sleep. I am at the Service Club it is a pretty nice place. Been to the show it was pretty good.

We ship on Mon. noon at 12:30 for Texas. It sound pretty good. Haven't got K.P. yet sure am thankful. Had to mop out a big building the other day it took us 3 hours about 30 of us [and] judge for yourself

how big the place was.

I have gained a little weight, about 5 pounds. Food is pretty good. I have tetanus shot weren't bad at all hurt for about 10 min and that was all. No dance tonight on account of the president's death, sure hated to hear about it. Boy they drafted about half of Iowa it seems. Every town here it seems is from the farms.

Hope everything is all right back at home. Has Jim shipped out yet hope he hasn't. Well it is about time to hit the sack so goodby for now. Say hello to Kay for me tell her I miss not being able to talk to her. Well so long for
Your loving son
Bob

letters.summary: Written on April 15, 1945, from the Service Club at Fort Leavenworth, Kansas, Bob writes his father describing daily army life: early reveille, light duties, the Service Club, and a show. He mentions shipping out to Texas on Monday at 12:30, having avoided K.P. duty, gaining five pounds, and receiving tetanus shots. He notes the cancellation of a dance due to President Roosevelt's death and asks after his brother Jim, hoping he hasn't shipped out yet. The letter provides a vivid snapshot of a young soldier's life in the final months of World War II.

topics: brother's friend stationed in Hawaii and Guam
metadata.mood_tone: Upbeat, informative, and casual; Bob is optimistic about his training and upcoming assignments, sharing news about military life with his father.
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but cursive is densely written in places; some words require careful interpretation. 'Keesler field Miss.' is abbreviated. 'bivouac' written as 'bivious' or similar. Page 2 top is partially obscured by bleed-through from page 1.
metadata.historical_context: Written in May 1946, shortly after WWII ended. Bob is in basic training at Sheppard Field, Texas, learning to use the .45 pistol as part of Air Corps crew member training. He is being trained as an AM (Aircraft Mechanic) Gunner. References to Hawaii and Guam reflect the Pacific Theater. The letter mentions tech school options at Amarillo, TX or Keesler Field, MS.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is explicitly dated 'Sun. 20, 6:15 PM' and the content — Sheppard Field, Texas; .45 pistol practice tomorrow; bivouac for 3 days; 18 weeks mechanic school then 6 weeks gunnery; basic over by June 1; Hawaii/Guam reference — all match the spring 1946 Sheppard Field posting. This is confirmed by the metadata normalizing to 1946-05-20. The letter mentions sending $30 home after payday, consistent with Bob's 1946 financial pattern.

pages.transcription:
Sun. 20, 6:15 PM

Dear Dad

Just received your letters, glad everybody liked the flowers. I am going to send home my extra after this pay day it should be around 30 dollars. Got a couple of letters from Jimmy he don't say much. Kept on sending the one he writes to you to me. We are going to fire the 45 pistol tomorrow for practice and Tues we go on bivouac for 3 days. Take a couple of long marches around 10 miles I guess and fire the 45 for record. We learned to use the 45 for all crew members on a plane carries one for his protection in case you are shot down. I am a A.M. Gunner, which mean I will go to a airplane

mechanic school for around 18 weeks and then to gunnery for 6 weeks. Not a bad deal at all. Basic should be over by the first of June I sure hope it is. I will either to Amarillo Texas or Keesler field Miss. for tech school I sure hope it is Miss. I might get a delay in route. Always can hope. It is really hot today was the around 100° in the shade. Don't worry about the swimming suit if you can't find it. I think Johnny might know where it is. I never have had a chance to see the furniture man yet I will one of these days tho. By the way where

do you apply the lacquer on the glasses. It ruins on the eye piece. If you would like a pair tell me. Leather case and all. There are really swell. Best glass in the world so the govt. says. I haven't bought a pair yet. I am still thinking about it. Hope business is still pretty good. I was talking to a kid and his brother stop at Hawaiii too on his way over. He stayed there for about 2 weeks. He is on Guam now. Say hello to everybody for me and goodby for now.

Love
Bob

letters.summary: Written on Sunday, May 20, 1946 from Sheppard Field, Texas, Bob writes to his father about his military training progress. He mentions upcoming .45 pistol practice and a 3-day bivouac, explains his assignment as an AM (Aircraft Mechanic) Gunner with plans for 18 weeks of mechanic school followed by 6 weeks of gunnery school, and notes he hopes to be sent to Keesler Field, Mississippi for tech school. He also discusses sending money home, the extreme heat, government-issued glasses, and a fellow trainee whose brother served in Hawaii and is now stationed on Guam.

The war has ended but Bob remains on active duty.

pages.transcription:
Dear Edith

Sure glad to hear from you. Hope you are enjoying your little vacation. Texas is sure a funny state one day you walk on it and the next you walk thru it. Oklahoma passes about 11:00 and returns about 9:00 the sky looked red at a distance. It was sure hot today and did my face ever get sun burned. We went thru the Gas Chamber and took off our masks so we would be convinced that they worked and did that tear gas Ever work. My eyes voted for remain and did my face burn. Went to show tonight and right now I am at the service Club. That is all there is to do around here, they have two bowling alley but they are alway full up. The show was pretty they have 3 theatres and boy do they ever

packed them full. I have about 4 pound here no wonder I walked on the average of 10 mile a day. boy do my legs get tired at times Especially after P.T. I drew K.P. on Sunday night at 2:50 to 3 in the morning but I get all day mon. off not too bad. Mon. is pay and we get payed. I nie [illegible] so fast around here, haven't but one or two women in texas and they are always with somebody, what a life. I should make the rounds more after when I am home but it always take me a few day to get rolling. With me when I am home next. We should a 18 day furlough around the end of August after we finished Gunnery school. Well sweetheart goodbye for now.

Loving you as allways
Bob

letters.summary: Bob Hanifen writes from Sheppard Field, Texas in May 1946, describing life at the military base to Edith. He recounts a dramatic Oklahoma storm visible from Texas, a tear gas chamber training exercise that left his eyes watering and his face burning, and the limited entertainment options on base (bowling alleys and theaters always packed). He mentions gaining 4 pounds despite walking 10 miles a day, pulling KP duty late Sunday night, and humorously lamenting the scarcity of available women in Texas. He closes by mentioning an anticipated 18-day furlough at the end of August after completing gunnery school.

request to be picked up at Ames Iowa

metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but some words are blurred or faded due to paper aging and ink bleed-through from the other side. Several words required guessing. The envelope annotations are particularly difficult to parse. The illness name on page 1 reads like 'Gastural' or 'Gastro-[enteritis]' — likely gastroenteritis.
metadata.historical_context: Letter appears to be from WWII era military service. Bob is on a military base, references G.I. exams, a Captain, furlough rate tickets, and a large epidemic at the base. The Union Pacific 'Challenger' was a famous train of the era. The mention of furlough travel and military testing suggests wartime military training context.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob is in the infirmary with gastroenteritis, has a furlough rate ticket on the Union Pacific Challenger (a famous train running through Cheyenne), and is traveling to Ames, Iowa at 1:10 AM Sunday. He mentions G.I. and department finals and the risk of flunking out ('if I don't the government pays your way home'). References to a Captain, furlough tickets, and academic finals are consistent with the ASTP program at University of Wyoming, Laramie (near Cheyenne). The annotation 'Oct. 476' is likely a later date notation in an unknown hand, not the letter's date. Given that the University of Wyoming ASTP letters span summer–fall 1944, and finals would occur at end of term, and Letter 18a mentions a leave September 30–October 10, this letter likely predates that leave — placing it in late September 1944. Alternatively, the notation may reference October and the letter could be from early October 1944. Estimated September–October 1944.

pages.transcription:
[Back of envelope, handwritten annotations]
Oct/96. 476
9:57 AM in at 11e
1:30 PM
S.M. 1992

[Address on lower portion:]
1940 Overton
Blvd [?]

Dear Dad

Sorry I haven't wrote but have been in the infirmary since Tues. about 3:00 I got sick and vomited all night darn near dead then had the G.I. till Sat. morn. I still feel pretty low. About 60 of us had it. They called [it] Gastural [Gastroenteritis?] and it goes [?] blue. Maybe you got it they had a big epidemic of it around here. I got real good news for you. A Kid went to Denver and he got me a furlough rate ticket on the U.P. would have

got in Des Moines 1:30 Sun. afternoon, but the Capt. informed us we get at 6:00 Sat morn. instead of 10:00 So I went down to the station and [crossed out word] found out it is good on the Challenger which [leaves] at 8:00 Sat morn. Only had to pay $1.50 extra to go to Cheyenne on the train. Altogether it cost me about $18.30. Get in Omaha at 9:30 and will be in Ames Sun. morn. at 1:10. I was wondering if you could meet me or maybe you could leave one of the cars there.

Have been taking our G.I. final this week and our Department finals and they are really tough baby to handle. I could have flunked out [crossed out word] but I think I will make it. if I don't the government pays your way home. Well I got a lot of studying to do so I will sign off for now.

Love Bob

P.S. Remember 1:10 Sun. morn. at Ames, Iowa

Only had to pay $1.50 extra to go to Cheyenne on the train. Altogether it cost me about $18.30. Get in Omaha at 9:30 and will be in Ames Sun. morn. at 1:10. I was wondering if you could meet me or maybe you could leave one of the cars there.

Have been taking our G.I. final this week and our Department finals and they are really tough baby to handle. I could have flunked out [crossed out word] but I think I will make it. if I don't the government pays your way home. Well I got a lot of studying to do so I will sign off for now.

Love Bob

P.S. Remember 1:10 Sun. morn. at Ames, Iowa
letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from a military base infirmary, where he has been sick since Tuesday with what appears to be a gastrointestinal illness that affected about 60 soldiers in what the letter calls a 'big epidemic.' Despite feeling low, he shares good news: a fellow soldier secured him a furlough rate ticket on the Union Pacific Challenger train, and he provides a detailed travel itinerary arriving in Ames, Iowa on Sunday morning at 1:10 AM, asking his father to meet him or leave a car. He also mentions taking difficult G.I. and department final exams, noting he thinks he will pass but that if he fails, the government pays his way home.

metadata.to: Dad (James Edward Hanifen Sr.)
metadata.location_written_from: Military training camp, likely in Texas (letter references Texas dust and heat)
metadata.mood_tone: Conversational and candid; describes physically demanding and sometimes unpleasant training experiences with a matter-of-fact, slightly humorous tone
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is cursive and occasionally difficult to parse; some words partially obscured by stains or paper damage; 'too' is crossed out in first paragraph; several words require guessing (e.g., 'Allright', 'D.A.' uncertain abbreviation); letter was written over multiple days as noted by the writer
metadata.historical_context: Letter describes WWII-era U.S. Army basic training, including gas mask drills and gas chamber exercises, physical training, and military camp life likely at a Texas base near Wichita Falls.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob describes his second day of training with gas mask drill, Texas dust, and hopes to go swimming at Wichita Falls with a Class A pass — all consistent with basic training at Sheppard Field, Texas. The payday of about $14 and the mention of sending blue swimming trunks are consistent with early basic training (low pay grade). This matches the spring 1945 Sheppard Field period documented in Letters 21 (April 23, 1945) and 52 (June 1, 1945). The gas mask chamber exercise description is very similar to Letter 48 (May 15, 1946, also Sheppard Field) but different enough in detail to suggest a different visit. Given the $14 payday (lowest enlisted pay), this is more likely the 1945 training rather than 1946. Estimated May 1945.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad

Well, one second day of training is finally over. We drilled two hours and had two hours of P.T. It wasn't too bad except for Gas Mask drill we had to practice for an hour putting them on and taking them off and then to wear them for an hour doing double time and running just to get the feel of them. I don't mind being in Texas but when having to eat it, and walk on it it's too much. The dust is so thick the sky looks red. You really get full of it.

This letter is a couple of days old right now, but I am going to continue it anyway. We had P.T. today and walked out to the Gas Chamber which is around a half mile, there we went thru the [Tear?] Gas Chamber. We went

thru first with our mask on and tested for Gas. It was there allright. The next we went in with our masked on but inside we had to take them off and give our City and State. Took a few steps and boy I couldn't see anything. Got outside my face burned and your eyes water and burned for about 5 min. Was it an experience. This afternoon we are marching out to the Camoflage [Camouflage?] area around a mile out of Camp. Had a case of the D.A. but I feel pretty good now. I think we have to work this Sun. We get payed Mon. I should get around 14 bucks.

I sent my blue trunks with you if you haven't because we can go swimming at Wichita Fall. When we get our Class A pass. Well I will write again real soon

Your Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from a military training camp in Texas, describing his second day of basic training which included physical training and an intense gas mask drill. He recounts a gas chamber exercise where recruits had to remove their masks inside and state their city and state, causing burning eyes and faces. He also mentions Texas dust, upcoming pay day (~$14), and hopes to go swimming at Wichita Falls once they receive a Class A pass. The letter was written over multiple days, as Bob notes midway through.

metadata.historical_context: John Hanifen is serving in the Korean War era military, assigned to an Engineer Combat Battalion via APO 301 (which routed through San Francisco, typical of Korea-era mail). The letter is dated March 1952, during the Korean War (1950–1953).

pages.transcription:
March 5, 1952

Dear John,

[Stationery heading reads: 'High Again / Jan' with a cocktail glass illustration]

We took a few pictures a couple of Sundays ago and thought you would like to see them. I have rub[bed] out the front half and you can sure see it in the picture. The lady next to me is Jan's mother. I now work for Texaco gasoline making $206.50 a month, not bad for a nut like me. I hear you are a Cpl and keep up the good work and get a Sgt. stripe so the old man can't say he was the only Sgt. in the family. Haven't heard too much from home. Guess it is because I don't write myself. I don't know what else could be the matter. Hope you are home by Christmas. I get my vacation around the 12th of Dec. and we could have a swell time. Those new Ford and Merc. are really swell looking cars. Sure wish I had one. We have a little cat and he sure has grown a lot. That

little [cat] tears up the house every day. Well John keep up the good work and get those Sgt. stripes for me. I will try and write more often. Write and tell me if you ever got your Christmas box
for Jan & me

Love Your Brother
    Bob

letters.summary: Bob Hanifen writes from Dallas, Texas to his brother John, a Corporal serving in Korea with the 439th Engineer Combat Battalion. Bob shares family news — he has taken some photos, is working at Texaco earning $206.50 a month, and has a new pet cat — and encourages John to earn his Sergeant's stripes so their father won't be the only Sergeant in the family. Bob mentions hoping John will be home by Christmas and asks whether John ever received the Christmas package he and his wife Jan sent.

metadata.mood_tone: Upbeat and chatty; Bob describes a busy, somewhat exhausting military training schedule with humor and good spirits, especially recounting the surprise gas attack on Memorial Day morning.
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but some words are difficult due to cursive style and bleed-through from the other side of the page. A few words on page 3 (e.g., 'Berle fields' and the squadron/classification references) are partially illegible. The postmark year is difficult to read — appears to say 1946 but letter content and context suggest 1945.
metadata.historical_context: Bob is in Army Air Forces basic training at Sheppard Field, Texas, likely mid-1945 (WWII still ongoing or just ended). References to gas mask drills, combat courses, bobby traps [booby traps], and classification/orders reflect wartime military training. Memorial Day gas attack drill reflects realistic wartime training exercises. Other cadets (30 yr. or over) receiving 15-day delay en route likely reflects demobilization or reassignment processing near or after V-E Day (May 8, 1945).

pages.transcription:
Fri. 6:30 PM
June, 1

Dear Dad
    I just received your combination letter from you and Edith. It was a very nice letter. The days are really flying around here. I bet that parade you were in was pretty rough. That a pretty long haul. By the way I am going to tell you what we did Memorial Day. I was rudely awakely [sic] at 6:30 the yelling of a Dozen guys, yelling gas attack, in a flash I was up. Took a quick breath and turn around to get my mask. In a flash I had it on, clear it and starting to breath again I could see the gas coming in through the windows. Every-body in the barracks

acted just as fast. The whole camp was gas at 8:00 with HCL smoke and tear gas. Boy the stuff was everywhere. I finished dressing and by the time chow was rounded the all clear was given. We than went to a couple of classes and than to the Combat Courses, to set and disarmed bobby traps [booby traps]. P.T. and than chow. Drill and B.T. in the afternoon again and a few lectures. I got back to the barracks and somebody said it was Memorial Day. Hell I didn't even know it. Thur. was pay day. The whole got a holiday from near, We whnt [sic] down to the Squadron at 7:30 and finally got pay at 1:30 in the afternoon a gang of us guys than went

swimming had a swell time, eat a good chicken fried steak, seen a show and started back. When we got to the gate. The M.P. said for all Cadet to report immediately to their Squad. for reclassification. It was 11:00 than. at 12:30 they came in and got 12 A.M. gunner and told them they wre [sic] leaving for Berle [Barle? Bartle?] fields, I sues [guess?]. I never got no order yet, so it means I continue on till I do. I should get my orders in a couple of days. Had a rough day today. 2 hours of movie P.T. and another lecture. In the afternoon B.T. again and Drill and a few lectures. You marched about 10 miles a day going to and from lectures and P.T. We really got Drill

to. The Sarge over us really gave us a going over. Tomorrow. We have a parade and big inspection in the afternoon regular basic. I am going to send home 30 dollars as soon as I have time to get to the Post office. It rained for about 10 min. this morning and really helped to keep the dust down. Well Dad goodby for now and I will write again soon. My orders should be here in a couple of days. All the old guys, 30 yr. or over, get a 15 day delay in route. Isn't that a boo loo [boo-hoo?]. Well goodby for now
    Your loving Son
        Bob

letters.summary: Bob Hanifen writes to his father from Sheppard Field, Texas on June 1 (likely 1945), describing his busy and exhausting days of military training including PT, drill, lectures, and combat course exercises. He vividly recounts a surprise gas attack drill on Memorial Day morning that woke the entire barracks, and describes how the holiday was otherwise spent on pay day activities including swimming and a chicken fried steak dinner. Bob notes he is awaiting his orders for reassignment, expects them within a couple of days, and plans to send $30 home as soon as he can get to the post office.

metadata.historical_context: WWII era — Bob is a private stationed at Chanute Field (Army Air Forces technical training base in Rantoul, Illinois). He describes a cross-country flight to the Curtis Wright Airport near St. Louis, mentions the Mississippi River flooding, and flies with a Major and a Captain. The 'Free' frank on the envelope indicates military free mail privilege.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is dated 'Jun. 1900' (military time for 7:00 PM in June) with Chanute Field return address (postmark Rantoul, Ill.). Bob describes flying to St. Louis in a C-47 — the same flight described in Letter 54 (dated Sun. 0130, which by context is the night before the flight) and Letter 78 (dated Mon. 1900, describing KP after the flight). Letter 54 is the Sunday night before; Letter 53 is written the same day as the flight (a Monday), and Letter 78 is the following Monday evening after KP. The C-47 flight to Curtis Wright Airport near St. Louis is the connecting event. Letter 54 mentions school starting July 2, and the flight occurs before that. Estimated June 25, 1945 (Monday).

pages.transcription:
Jun. 1900

Dear Dad

    Just sitting in my bunk now. Had a swell day. Went down to Operations and got our chutes and took off for St. Louis. We took off at 10 till 1:00 and the thrill was really great. The field passed away very quickly and we started climbing for altitude we leveled off a 9000 ft. We were doing around 160 M.P.H. The field looked very pretty up that high and the road and cars so very mall [small?]. The noise was pretty great, but not too bad. The clouds were pretty thick and they just seemed to melt when we went thru them. We up to the [Lt. in] Cockpit

and talked to the Lt. a while. He was a swell Joe. The air wasn't very rough [at?] but each little down draft or up draft your stomach would drop a foot it seemed. The hyd. fluid started leaking out the landing gear and the Ft. Engineering had a time up there fixing it. Sort of scared me a little a bit.
We went over the Miss. and it was flooding all over the country side it seemed. We circled to land and I about [illegible] — because he really whirled the plane around in a beautiful bank and your stomach really fell funny when you come down from that [crossed out words] height. We came in pretty

fast and the tires really squealed when we hit. The time was 10 min. till 2:00. That is really moving along. We spent 30 min. at the Curtis Wright Air Port, 18 miles out of St. Louis. I didn't feel to [too] good so I never eat anything. It was all free too. Seen a lot of swell planes down there. We left at 10 till three and flew at 4,000 ft. back. It was pretty bumpy, brought back a Major and a Capt. I slept most of the way and I felt pretty good when we made our traffic pattern to land. We landed at 10 till 4:00. I felt darns good. I guess I was excited going down there. Eat a good kennen [dinner?] and now I find out

letters.summary: Bob Hanifen writes to his father from Chanute Field, Illinois, describing an exciting cross-country flight to St. Louis. He details the thrill of taking off, leveling at 9,000 feet at 160 MPH, flying through thick clouds, and visiting the Curtis Wright Airport 18 miles outside St. Louis. He mentions a brief scare when hydraulic fluid started leaking from the landing gear, witnessing the Mississippi River flooding, and the stomach-dropping sensation of banking turns; the letter ends mid-sentence on page 3, apparently incomplete.
