metadata.mood_tone: Upbeat, casual, and enthusiastic — Bob is excited about an impromptu flight to St. Louis in a C-47 and marvels at the large aircraft on the base. Lighthearted and conversational.
metadata.legibility_notes: Generally clear cursive throughout. A few words required close reading (e.g., 'monkeying' on page 2). Bleed-through from reverse side is visible but does not significantly impede reading.
metadata.historical_context: Written near the end of WWII (June 1945, after V-E Day but before V-J Day). Bob is stationed at Chanute Field (AAF technical training base in Rantoul, Illinois) awaiting the start of school on July 2. He references large WWII aircraft including B-17, B-24, B-25, B-26, P-61, P-51, P-40, and B-29 bombers visible at the base.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is dated 'Sun. 0130' from Chanute Field. Bob says he is about to fly to St. Louis 'this afternoon' in a C-47. School starts July 2. This Sunday precedes the Letter 53 flight (Monday). The night-before-the-flight context and school starting date place this on approximately June 24, 1945 (Sunday before the June 25 flight). Consistent with the Chanute Field timeline and school start of July 2.

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS:
Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17170600
Sqd. R – 3502 – BKS-604
AAF – Chanute Field, Ill.

POSTMARK: RANTOUL, ILL. / JUN 25 / 10-AM / 1945

STAMP AREA: [torn; 'Free' notation visible]

ADDRESSED TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Leo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

• Service Club • Chanute Field, Illinois •

                                                Sun. 0130
Dear Dad

     Just got done eating chow — chicken it wasn't bad in fact pretty good. I am going to St. Louis this afternoon with a Lt. and John Hover in a C-47 transport. He was down on the line and asked him for a ride and he said okay. He is taking a Colonel's furniture to St. Louis. How do you like that stuff. I will tell you all about it later. We don't start school till July 2, and in the meantime all we do is try and keep out of doing work. Go swimming

all day. Might get a three day if I work hard enough on the Capt. Might fly home so if one of these days I popped into the shop don't be too surprised. It is really a lot of monkeying around the hangar. They have a lot of B-17's, B-24 B-25, B-26, P-61, P-51, P-40, and B-29 and are those 29's ever big. Can't hardly believe. One would have to have a step ladder to get in the darn thing. Well dad goodbye for that Lt. won't wait all day and we have to check out a chute yet

          Your Loving Son  Bob

letters.summary: Written late on a Sunday night (0130) from the Service Club at Chanute Field, Illinois, Bob tells his father he is about to fly to St. Louis in a C-47 transport with a Lieutenant and John Hover, who is transporting a Colonel's furniture. Bob notes that school doesn't start until July 2 and that in the meantime they fill the days swimming and avoiding work detail. He mentions the possibility of a three-day pass and potentially flying home for a surprise visit to the family shop. He marvels at the many WWII aircraft on the field — including B-17s, B-24s, B-25s, B-26s, P-61s, P-51s, P-40s, and especially the enormous B-29s — before signing off quickly because the Lt. is waiting and he still needs to check out a parachute.

metadata.historical_context: John Hanifen is serving in Korea during the Korean War (1950-1953) as a PFC in the 439th Engineer Combat Battalion. Bob references the GI Bill ('G.I. bill of rights') and encourages John to use it for college at Iowa University. Bob mentions a fellow Dallas man finally being drafted and leaving July 16. Letter was forwarded after being returned to the U.S., indicating John may have rotated back stateside.

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS (printed/written in green ink):
After Five Days Return to
R.E. HANIFEN
3614 Noble St.
DALLAS, TEXAS

POSTMARK: DALLAS, TEX. / JUL 16 / 11-AM / 1952 / 8

STAMPS: Two 3-cent United States Postage Thomas Jefferson stamps

VIA AIR MAIL

ADDRESSEE:
P.F.C. John R. Hanifen 55046284
Co. C, 439 Eng. Com. Bn.
APO 301 c/o P.M.
San Francisco, Calif.

FORWARDING ANNOTATION (rubber stamp / handwritten):
Returned to U.S.
Forward to 1115
Park Ave. Des Mo[ines]
Iowa
7/26/52
[signature]

July 15, 1952

Dear John,

     What are you doing in Korea, running the war all by yourself. You have been there long enough. I guess there isn't any one left to draft. But I have good news for you, there is a guy here in Dallas who have been slipping by for a long time and they finally got him, he leaves the 16th of July. I sure they let you go home some time, & figured you should be out for good at least by the 1st of November.

I am still working for the Texas Co. we sell Texaco gas

and Havoline oil. It sure hot here in Dallas hasn't rained for 2 months. I was reading the papers the other day and it says all G.I.s in Korea are eligible for the G.I. bill of rights. You should go to Iowa Uni. for a semester, would do you some good and you could also have a lot of fun. I am still planning on Iowa for Christmas, hope it doesn't snow like it did when you and me came for home in 1948 from Texas. I bought a Des Moines paper, thought this article would interest you a lot more than my writing

sure glad Lew beat Chadwick.
Well John keep your eyes open and maybe the Golden Gate will appear sooner than you think. I hope it does. Drop me a line some time when have the day off.

But so long for now
     Love Your Brother
          Bob

[Photograph enclosed: Black and white photo of a young woman in a dark one-piece swimsuit, seated on what appears to be a wooden diving board or dock, with water and stairs/hillside visible in the background. She is smiling. No writing visible on the photo front.]

LEW JENKINS WINS 75-LAP STOCK CLASSIC BEFORE 3,753

Disregarding the fact that he wasn't one of the leading contenders, Lew Jenkins Friday night roared through 75 laps of blinding dust and spinning cars to win the Pioneer Raceways mid-summer stock car championship before 3,753 fans.

Jenkins, who was all but overlooked in the pre-race forecasts, grabbed the lead from Chris Skadal on the thirty-eighth lap and held it. Fine driving in all of the races made for an unusually small number of collisions.

Skadal, the top driver at Pioneer, led the field through most of the first 38 laps. His stiffest competition came from Wayne Chadwick who held second place until Jenkins made his bid from the No. 3 spot.

From that point on, Jenkins was hounded all the way to the checkered flag by Chadwick and Lefty Robinson, who finished third. At the 50-lap mark Robinson made his big bid, riding Jenkins' rear fender for three laps before falling behind.

Chadwick went back into second place on the sixty-seventh lap and held it to the finish. Skadal had a brush with W. R. Newton on the sixty-third lap and finished sixth.

Main Event—(20 cars, 75 laps) 1. Lew Jenkins. 2. Wayne Chadwick. 3. Lefty Robinson. 4. Jack Thompson. 5. A. DeCarlo. 6. Chris Skadal. Time: 25:51.75.

Semi-Main Event—(20 cars, 30 laps) 1. Keith Lookabaugh. 2. Lee Speck. 3. Al Burherer. 4. John Plum. 5. Bob Huggins. 6. Jim Wilkinson. Time: 11:23.39.

Consolation Race—(13 cars, 20 laps) 1. Bob Bish. 2. Louis Slauson. 3. Clyde Bedford. 4. Newt Bartholmew. 5. Don Burch. 6. Ray Cooper. Time: 7:53.21.

---

BRIGHT WILL PLAY WITH ROAD KINGS

Bobby Grund, Des Moines sports promoter, said Friday Drake grid star Johnny Bright has signed a contract to play pro basketball for him if he plays basketball next season.

Bright played with Grund's Harlem Road Kings briefly last spring.

Bright added, however, "I just received approval of my enrollment in Mount Royal college at Calgary, a branch of the University of Alberta, and I hope to do graduate work this winter."

Bright will play football for a Calgary pro team this fall.

---

LONG SHOTS WIN AT INGLEWOOD

INGLEWOOD, CAL. (AP) — Longshots set off an explosion under the favorites at Hollywood Park Friday, with Admiral Drake making the biggest noise in winning the $50,000 American handicap and sending Intent, the heavy favorite, reeling into third place.

De Anza upset the form [remainder cut off]

letters.full_transcription:
July 15, 1952

Dear John,

     What are you doing in Korea, running the war all by yourself. You have been there long enough. I guess there isn't any one left to draft. But I have good news for you, there is a guy here in Dallas who have been slipping by for a long time and they finally got him, he leaves the 16th of July. I sure they let you go home some time, & figured you should be out for good at least by the 1st of November.

I am still working for the Texas Co. we sell Texaco gas and Havoline oil. It sure hot here in Dallas hasn't rained for 2 months. I was reading the papers the other day and it says all G.I.s in Korea are eligible for the G.I. bill of rights. You should go to Iowa Uni. for a semester, would do you some good and you could also have a lot of fun. I am still planning on Iowa for Christmas, hope it doesn't snow like it did when you and me came for home in 1948 from Texas. I bought a Des Moines paper, thought this article would interest you a lot more than my writing

sure glad Lew beat Chadwick.
Well John keep your eyes open and maybe the Golden Gate will appear sooner than you think. I hope it does. Drop me a line some time when have the day off.

But so long for now
     Love Your Brother
          Bob

[Enclosures: photograph of young woman in swimsuit on dock; newspaper clipping from Des Moines paper about Lew Jenkins winning 75-lap stock car race at Pioneer Raceways, plus secondary articles about Johnny Bright and Inglewood horse racing]
letters.summary: Bob Hanifen writes to his brother John, a PFC serving in Korea with the 439th Engineer Combat Battalion, from Dallas on July 15, 1952. Bob teases John about still being in Korea, shares that he is still working for the Texas Company (Texaco) selling gas and Havoline oil, and notes the severe Dallas drought. He encourages John to use the GI Bill to attend Iowa University when he returns, and mentions his own plans to visit Iowa for Christmas. Bob encloses a Des Moines newspaper clipping about a stock car race won by Lew Jenkins (over Wayne Chadwick) at Pioneer Raceways, as well as what appears to be a photo of a young woman — the letter was later forwarded to 1115 Park Ave., Des Moines, Iowa, suggesting John had rotated back to the United States by late July 1952.

Bob is at Chanute Field, Illinois for technical training in aircraft repair (sheet metal work, welding, radiator repair, self-sealing gas tanks). He mentions B-29 repair men being needed urgently overseas, and references receiving overseas shots, suggesting possible deployment preparation.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob describes his first day of incoming processing at Chanute Field, starting a 15-week (84-day) technical training course in sheet metal work, welding, radiator repair. He has received overseas shots and notes the Army needs B-29 repair men urgently. School was set to start July 2 per Letter 54 (June 24, 1945). This letter describes the first day of processing, which would be around July 2 or shortly after. The description matches the 'Sgt T-BKS-249' squadron designation seen in other Chanute letters from July 1945. Estimated early July 1945.

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS:
Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17170600
Sqd. R - 3502
AAF Chanute Fld. Ill.

SENT TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Keo Way
Des Moines Iowa

POSTAGE: Free (military frank)
POSTMARK: 1945 (partial)

Fri 9:20

Dear Dad

      Another day is done almost time for lights out. Had a swell day got up at 6:00 eat breakfast and then we started incoming processing, got our baggage and all that kind of stuff. We are suppose to start to school Mon. Our course [starts?] last a total of 15 weeks or 84 days total up. It consists of 12 days of basic instruction, a few weeks of welding repairing Radiator and self sealing gas tanks. Cutting and shaping metals for differents parts of planes. And a lot of other stuff the last 30 days consists of actual plane repairs. We are getting our overseas shot next week. They say they need us bad for B-29 repair men. A 15 day delay

in route furlough after school and then P.O.E. I think I am going to like the course fine. I went swimming all afternoon pretty nice pool. Clearest I have ever seen in my life. They have 3 schedules here A, B, C. A schedule start at 0600 and ends at 12 noon. B start at 12 and end at 6:00 and C start at 6:00 and end at midnight not bad at all if you get a good shift. Food is damn good here milk twice a day & I have gained the weight I lost coming up on the train. You can do anything on this field no regulations at all hardly a few but that's all.

Well Dad goodbye for now, will write again. Give my address to everybody for me,

      Your Loving Son
            Bob

letters.summary: Written on a Friday evening at 9:20pm from Chanute Field, Illinois, Bob describes his first day of incoming processing at his new base. He outlines his upcoming 15-week (84-day) technical training course in aircraft sheet metal work, welding, radiator repair, and self-sealing gas tank repair, with the final 30 days devoted to actual plane repairs. Bob mentions receiving overseas shots and the Army's urgent need for B-29 repair men, with a 15-day furlough en route to the Port of Embarkation (P.O.E.) after school. He is in good spirits, praising the food, the swimming pool, and the relaxed base regulations, and notes he has already regained weight lost during his train journey.

pages.transcription:
July 12 - 1952

Dear John

Just a few lines to give you the Weather Data & so forth. This is the Day, The Republicans & Eisenhower are Shouting. I don't No, but what, he might Make it, I supose, all you guys will Vote for him. It is a little quiet here You No a Big Steel Strike is still on.

Do you hear from Bob — I hear Moone often from you. Some one Must have Said Something Jimmie Bought a Nice white Sport Shirt for you on Father day. Better write heem & let him No, it was ok a Box of Cigars from Jim a Shirt from you & Not even a card from Bob doing pretty good don't you think.

Sat morning 7:45 AM

Des Moines 14, Iowa, July 12 - 52

Not the First Page

IN ACCOUNT WITH
HANIFEN BODY & PAINT CO.
J. E. Hanifen
We Specialize in Repairing Wrecked Cars
Phone 4-8223          801 Park Street          Res. Phone 4-9704
All bills due and payable the 10th of the month following date of purchase

Say John, did we ever Send you a card or Picture of your Car. It is worth about 18⁰⁰ and if the Strike Keeps up, it may go to 2000 John Hold on to your dough, it is hard to ge a hold of here. The dif guys stop & holler where you are Keeping your Self. The Taylor Boys & Godard Thought you Would Be here for Sure. By Now Well So long for Now If you got any Bitching to do, Put it in the Letter     Love Dad

letters.summary: Written July 12, 1952 by James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad) to his son Col. John R. Hanifen, who is serving with the 439th Engineer Construction Battalion in Korea (via APO San Francisco). The father shares home-front news including the Republican National Convention and Eisenhower's apparent nomination, the ongoing steel strike, and notes about Father's Day gifts — Jimmie bought John a white sport shirt, but Bob sent nothing, not even a card. Dad advises John to hold onto his money as a car John owns has risen in value due to the strike, and closes warmly telling John to put any complaints in his letters.

pages.transcription:
Nov 5 – 51

Well John,
    Just a little Bull. do you know, you would be ashamed to go away with some of the Kids they take. You make good Water Boys. Ridgeway says you Boys as Stuck for 18 month. Well, it is just as Bad one Place as another they Say. We are Still getting work. But they are only 4 of us Working. down to nite trying to make Mercury Heat. don't No if I can or not. Had a Sign Put on truck the other day

HANIFEN
Body Shop
48223

[written alongside bottom text:] don't look Bad – will check my June in Bad and see if they are any Thermostats in Motors

did you get check I [and?] Jim [Jr.] sent to you, did you get large Box Hell Sent 6 or 8 Weeks ago.
If nothing Happens, will go Pheasant hunting Sunday. I guess Edith & I will go up to Her Sister Alice, to see if they are any Birds. Duck, went thru here like a Bat out of Hell, Cold hit up North, they Moved out of Minsota [Minnesota], then a Cold Blast Sent them on thru Iowa, Just a little Snow flurries here, But So early in the fall. all the Places around Iowa Caught Hell 12 inches in St. Louis today Kansas New Mexico all caught it. Boy are they having it 300 Cars got between St. Louis & K.C.
Nov 11 Bob Birth day didn't mean Say, you got Birth day Card What Happen

Love Dad

Nov 8 – 51

Dear John Jim Just got your letter & Held this up to put check in
June will go down & am Sending you another 60 [illegible word — possibly "00"] You Better Remember Jan 1 – when You Broke It. They are Sure Chomping [Cheating?] the guys as they come Back from [Korea] got a guy for $485 that He Spent in Korea

& one of his good friends took him. They all Say the Same thing didn't No you had any Money at all
Dick & I Brought Back an Elk & Deer So we have a little Meat on Hand. Still have got Work, But other Shops don't So I Guess Were lucky. Going to Edith Sister Alice Sunday & see if any Birds

Love Dad.

letters.summary: This is a two-part letter (begun Nov 5, completed Nov 8, 1951) from James Hanifen Sr. ('Dad') to his son John, who is serving in the military during the Korean War era. Dad updates John on the slow season at the body shop, a major early-season snowstorm hitting Iowa and surrounding states, plans for pheasant hunting with Edith (and later a deer/elk trip with Dick), and reminds John of Bob's November 11th birthday. He also mentions sending checks to John and warns him about soldiers being financially taken advantage of upon returning from Korea. The letter is warm and chatty, signed 'Love Dad' on both sections.

Note: letter is dated 'June 29' but postmark reads 'JUL 29 1945' — likely the letter date is actually July 29, or the postmark month is correct and the written date is a slip.
metadata.mood_tone: Casual, cheerful, and conversational; Bob is in good spirits despite a swollen jaw keeping him close to base, and he is looking forward to coming home.

The word 'Indianapolis' on the envelope is partially obscured. The date '29' could be June or July — postmark says July.
metadata.historical_context: Written during WWII in 1945 (postmark July 29, 1945). Bob is in military training at what appears to be an Army Air Forces technical school in Champaign/Indianapolis area, learning aluminum parts forming and wing tip repairs. Air Forces Day is mentioned. The war in the Pacific was still ongoing at this date (Japan surrendered August 15, 1945).
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Although the letter is dated 'June 29,' the postmark is July 29, 1945 per the metadata. Bob's jaw is still swollen (wisdom tooth extracted in Letter 35, dated July 1945 per Rantoul postmark), he references Air Forces Day on August 30, and plans to come home Sunday August 4 — consistent with Letter 27 (July 20, 1945) also mentioning August 4 as the planned home visit. 'June 29' is almost certainly a writing error for 'July 29.' Confirmed as July 29, 1945.

pages.transcription:
FROM: Cpl. Bob
19170600
BRS 249, Indianapolis [?]
Ill.

[Postmark: CHAMPAIGN, ILL. JUL 29 10-PM 1945]

[Marked: Free]

TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
954 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

June 29

Dear Dad
    Down at the U.S.O. for a couple of hours. Jaw is still swelled up so I never went anywhere this week. Wish you could come over Wed. for Air Forces Day. We get off school so that means a couple days off. Not bad. We start hand forming of aluminum parts next week and wing tip repairs. Haven't had a

letters from Jimmy in a long time. Has he moved out yet? Pay day is [this?] Tues. Glad of that. The [Cubs?] Pirates play here Mon. Should be a good game. I can get a clear thro to Des Moines and back, so if nothing happened I will be home Sun. Aug. 4. Well goodbye for now.

        Your Loving Son
            Bob

letters.summary: Written July 29, 1945 (though dated 'June 29,' likely a slip), Bob writes to his father from the USO in Champaign, Illinois while in military technical training. His jaw is swollen and he has stayed on base all week; he mentions upcoming training in aluminum parts forming and wing tip repairs, an Air Forces Day holiday from school, and a Cubs/Pirates baseball game. He expects to get a pass home to Des Moines around Sunday, August 4, and asks after his brother Jimmy, from whom he hasn't heard in a while.

metadata.envelope_notes: No envelope image provided
metadata.mood_tone: Informative and upbeat despite illness; practical and matter-of-fact about impending overseas deployment
metadata.legibility_notes: Some words are difficult to parse due to cursive style and paper aging; 'initilize' or similar word on page 1 is unclear; 'drizzling' rain reading is a best guess; 'temporary' misspelled as 'temporpary'
metadata.historical_context: Letter references overseas deployment (WWII era), APO number assignment, processing at a large military camp, and crossing on a ship — consistent with WWII deployment staging.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is dated 'Jan. 31' but the content — 2,300 soldiers being processed, heading to New York City, APO number assignment, a large camp with 8 telephone centers of 30 booths each, crossing on a ship — matches exactly the description in Letter 23 (January 29, 1946): '23 hundred of us on the boat. We are going Naples, Italy' departing from Camp Kilmer NJ. Letter 60 says 'We are going to process here today so we will be leaving here pretty soon. Hope we get to see New York City.' This is Camp Kilmer. The date 'Jan. 31' in the letter is close to Letter 23's January 29 date — likely written January 31 at Camp Kilmer before departure, consistent with the 48-hour staging described in Letter 23. Confirmed as approximately January 31, 1946.

pages.transcription:
Jan. 31

Dear Dad

Well everything is jake with me today. I got to bed about 1:00 after talking to you. I will the key to [initilize?] in the letter. We are going to process here today so we will be leaving here pretty. Hope we get to see the New York city. We get paid 25 so the money situation is good. I will write to everybody else as soon as I get my permanent APO number this one is just temporpary [sic] till we get overseas tell everybody that otherwise I might not get all my mail. The food here is good. This camp is really huge, just an example 8 telephone centers with 30 booths apiece. Pretty big size.

There is 2300 of us so we should get a pretty fair size ship I hope won't be to rough that way. Why don't you go to the courthouse and have a duplicate registration made on the 42 chev I don't ever remember seeing it. The weather is still wet here, She a drizzling rain all the time. Have been shining my combat boots all morning not much else to do. My cold is still with me wish I could get rid of it. Make me feel like hell. Well say goodbye for now.

Lots of Love
Bob
[initial/scrawl]

letters.full_transcription:
Jan. 31

Dear Dad

Well everything is jake with me today. I got to bed about 1:00 after talking to you. I will the key to [initilize?] in the letter. We are going to process here today so we will be leaving here pretty. Hope we get to see the New York city. We get paid 25 so the money situation is good. I will write to everybody else as soon as I get my permanent APO number this one is just temporpary [sic] till we get overseas tell everybody that otherwise I might not get all my mail. The food here is good. This camp is really huge, just an example 8 telephone centers with 30 booths apiece. Pretty big size.

There is 2300 of us so we should get a pretty fair size ship I hope won't be to rough that way. Why don't you go to the courthouse and have a duplicate registration made on the 42 chev I don't ever remember seeing it. The weather is still wet here, She a drizzling rain all the time. Have been shining my combat boots all morning not much else to do. My cold is still with me wish I could get rid of it. Make me feel like hell. Well say goodbye for now.

Lots of Love
Bob
letters.summary: Bob writes to his father on January 31st from a large military staging camp, reporting that he and 2,300 fellow soldiers are being processed and will soon be leaving — possibly via New York City — for overseas deployment. He notes the camp's impressive size (including 8 telephone centers with 30 booths each), mentions receiving pay, and asks his dad to get a duplicate registration for the family's 1942 Chevy. He is suffering from a cold and dealing with persistent wet weather, but otherwise remains upbeat and signs off with 'Lots of Love.'

Cpl. B[ob] [H]anifen, 170600

SEP 11
3:30 PM
1945

[Upper right]
Free

[Recipient address, center]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Des [Moines?] Way N.
Des Moines, Iowa

Sept. 1, 45

Dear Dad
  We had a swell time and got back on time. Never had a bit of trouble, those new shoes really helped a lot. We got off okay at Camp no body said a word to us yet, so it looks like we came out ahead I hope. Not a lot to write about around here same old place. Not doing a lot in the shop. I will get Edith a present today hope she will like it. I guess

I will be home again this week as long as everything goes right. to expect me anytime
Well Dad goodbye for now.

Your loving son
  Bob

He notes no one at camp said anything about their absence, so he hopes they came out ahead. He plans to get Edith a present and expects to come home again later that week. The letter is short and upbeat, reflecting the relaxed atmosphere near the end of WWII.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad          Mon.

Well I got back alright to camp. I got back at 7:30. Not bad at all. I rode to Moline with the [Sikon?] and took off from there to Galesburg. I figured it to be 200 miles closer. Got a ride right up to there. At Peoria I met Hank waiting for a ride. Pretty nice to find him along the way. We graduate from school the 22nd Sept. That means no birthday at home. or Thanksgiving or Christmas. Well Dad

Goodbye for now.
Your loving Son
    Bobby

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father on a Monday after returning to Chanute Field, Illinois, describing his hitchhiking journey back to camp through Moline, Galesburg, and Peoria, where he coincidentally met a fellow named Hank also waiting for a ride. He reports that his class graduates September 22nd, which means he will miss his birthday, Thanksgiving, and Christmas at home. The letter is brief and ends affectionately as 'Your loving Son, Bobby.'

Bob mentions men being discharged ('all tech school men must be out by the 16th'), an exhausting 10-12 mile hike, and his buddies Jack Dee Ray and John Hare. This is consistent with the June 1945 Sheppard Field period between Letters 52 (June 1) and 33 (June 18). Confirmed as June 13, 1945.

pages.transcription:
[Return Address]
Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17170600
Sqd. I-3706-ABU
AAF BKS-505
Sheppard Fld, Texas

[Postmark: Wichita Falls, Texas, Jun 13, 11 PM, 1945]

[Upper right: Free]

[Recipient Address]
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Wed. 1750

Dear Dad

     Well the boys are still slipping out but no luck as yet. One remote possibility is they say all tech school men must be out by the 16th, so they really mean business. We went on a 10 miles hike this morning it took us 4 hours to do it. My legs and feet still ached. Boy it seem a long way. When we got back they said it was more like 12 miles. A truck measure it 12 miles. What a life. We got two hours off this afternoon. Boy it seem good to sit down for awhile. My buddies are leaving me all the time. This army sure break up a lot of good buddies. We are going to town tonight, get a couple picture taken in a 10¢ store. I sure hate to leave them. One kid named [continued on page 2]

Jack Dee Ray from Lance Creek Wyo. One swell guy. My other buddies name is John Hare from Hastings Nebr. A great big bruiser 194 pd. of dynamite. The other guy Ray is a small package of dynamite too. A lot of help in a tight place. Sure have a lot of fun with them. They were in a A.S.T.[P.?] at Creighton Nebr. Well so long old boy I will write again real soon.

     Love
          Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from Sheppard Field, Texas, dated Wednesday at 1750 hours (postmarked June 13, 1945). He reports that men are being discharged rapidly — all tech school men must be out by the 16th — but he has not yet received orders. He describes an exhausting 10-12 mile hike that morning and laments that his army buddies are constantly leaving; he fondly describes two friends, Jack Dee Ray from Lance Creek, Wyoming, and John Hare from Hastings, Nebraska, both former A.S.T.P. men from Creighton, Nebraska. The letter captures the bittersweet mood of the post-war demobilization period.

[PAGE 1 — Dad's letter, written upside down, dated Nov 30-51]

Nov 30-51

Dear John,

Been trying to hear from you — what about the box. Customs + [illegible] don't you all do it. [illegible] send some [illegible] the ell [illegible] from [illegible] letter from you. Have one looking for [illegible] from Her — well no one Breaks news from you. I should have gone to Still Beway. Here I looked at a 51 Ford March Creston 3a + looks pick up more bought 4 for you. 4 months [illegible] up more B [illegible] it up. I, you got [illegible] 9 times. To us for. Time let me no we got a Go each time [illegible] Black + white, get boy get Destination [illegible] Hunting is Tough if her hear. I heard Hunting is Tough the of her about 6 all year. 9 am just Come in. Said said Hello to you. He said he just don't seem to get Time to Write

[PAGE 2 — Dad's letter continued]

We Bought a 48 coupe good deals. But Need a Body for it. We locked up 5 Hot last Sat + went Hunting I got 1 pheasant + 3 Rabbits. Dick Brought them Back. Kept 1 Rabbit + I haven't got it yet. We may go Sunday. Leo is here Now + He is Writing a Note — So Now I will Knock it off for You. Bob is not doing so good — I don't think He should Haul His Butt, if here He could at least get By.

Love Dad

[PAGES 3-4 — George & Jan's letter, written in red ink]

Dear John,
      Reed your last letter and was very glad to hear from you. Bet you had a time with that M[?] lewis. I am at your dad's now[?] asked Jim if old [illegible] [illegible] he said "I'll [illegible] going to send him any [illegible] He hasn't wrote me yet.

      Well we sold our house. had have to make $2000.00 and took it + have to [make?] [illegible] apartment house. I bought me an apartment house. Have more money to spend now.
      Jan is pregnant, should have have baby in June. My female dog is also [pregnant?] have pups in February.

Mort and Joyce just got back from California. I guess [his?] is flat broke again. Pat and Colleen's [illegible] baby has been in the Hospital 2 months

the ford is holding up pretty good, but I thinks I get me a new Pontiac before long.
      [Saw?] [illegible] Harold & Shirley awhile back. They are O.K.
      Will be glad to see you when you get back.
      Write soon —

            Your buddies
            George & Jan.

1220 Fremont St.
letters.summary: This letter to P.F.C. John Hanifen (serving overseas in 1951, likely during the Korean War era) contains two separate notes tucked in the same envelope. The first, from his father (Dad/James Hanifen Sr.), dated November 30, 1951, shares family news including a hunting trip, purchasing a '48 coupe that needs body work, concern about Bob's struggling business, and a visit from Leo. The second note, written in red ink by friends George and Jan, shares personal updates including Jan's pregnancy (baby due in June), the purchase of an apartment house after selling their home, news about mutual friends Mort, Joyce, Pat, Colleen, Harold, and Shirley, and warm wishes for John's safe return.

metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob writes from University of Wyoming on 'Cowboy Joe' letterhead about ROTC inspection, basketball, cold weather (-10°F), promotion to Platoon Sergeant, brother Jim at Fort Riley, and anticipation of Air Corps call-up. The mention of 48 hours needed for Sophomore status and currently having 51 hours after one more term suggests he is completing his first or second semester. Given that the ASTP program at Wyoming is documented from summer 1944 (Letter 16, July 1944), and Bob was 'almost a Sophomore' in Letter 66 ('I will be a sophomore when the next term ends'), this letter appears to be from a slightly later term than the summer letters — perhaps late fall 1943 or winter/spring 1944, before the confirmed summer 1944 ASTP period. Jim being at Fort Riley's 'Stables' (cavalry training) and the Air Corps deferment context suggest early 1944. Estimated January–February 1944.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad

Sorry about the last letter. Not much doing here at school. Our govt. inspection came off fine and we got a recommendation from the Colonels. Went to a basketball game last night between the U of Wyoming and Casper Air Base. Casper won by a big margin. Got a letter from Jim from the Stables at Ft. Riley. He says he has it pretty easy. It is really getting cold up here. Sat. night it was about 10 below and the wind is always blowing. I am now a Platoon Sergeant, got a promotion. If I come back for another term I will [be a?] [Freshman?]. It takes 48 hours to be a Sophomore and I will have 51 hours. But another [term?] I will [spend?] [die?] but the Air Corps won't call me until then, so there's not much I can do.

Love Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from the University of Wyoming in Laramie, apologizing for a previous letter and sharing updates from campus life. He mentions a successful ROTC inspection earning a recommendation from the Colonels, a basketball loss by U of Wyoming to Casper Air Base, extremely cold weather (-10°F), and his promotion to Platoon Sergeant. He also notes that his brother Jim is stationed at Fort Riley and doing well, and discusses his academic credit hours and his anticipation of being called up by the Air Corps.

The return date of January 8 is consistent with post-Christmas semester break. The mention of becoming a sophomore 'when the next term ends' suggests he is in an earlier semester than Letter 65 (where he has 51 credit hours toward sophomore). This letter appears to be from just before Christmas break, likely December 1943 or possibly December 1944. Given that the summer 1944 ASTP letters clearly exist and this letter has a different, more collegiate (non-ASTP) tone with no military training references, December 1943 seems more likely — before the ASTP phase. Low confidence due to significant letter damage and illegibility.

pages.transcription:
Dad

Not much going on around here. [Last?] week is over and I never did very [well?]. The A.S.U. [band?] put on a open house for the [seminary?] room and I met a new [girl] — her name is Jigger McClare. She is just a little taller than Kay. I could use a little money. I will have to send some Christmas [cards?] and all that stuff. I come back to school [we are] to be back Jan. 8. But I will be [almost a] Sophomore when the next [term?] ends. The Unit is having a dance this Sat. so I should have a pretty nice time. I got a Christmas present from [a girl?] in Des Moines named Beverly Ratcliffe [I] just barely know her. Sorry to hear about [un]cle [Dan?]. Got [a] letter from Jim [Jr.?] and [he is] having a pretty easy life at [H— ?]
[illegible line(s) at bottom]

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from the University of Wyoming in Laramie, reporting on college life near the holiday break. He mentions meeting a girl named Jigger McClare, asking for money for Christmas expenses, and notes that classes resume January 8th and that he will be a sophomore after the next term. He also mentions receiving an unexpected Christmas present from Beverly Ratcliffe of Des Moines, expresses condolences about a relative ('uncle Dan'?), and references a letter from Jim describing an easy life. The letter is significantly damaged and partially illegible.

Jimmy (brother) is also apparently being deployed soon. References to a 'project' the unit was on having already left suggests troop movements.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is explicitly dated 'Jan. 18, 45' in the text. Content matches perfectly: Bob has completed military processing at Greensboro, NC (same Service Club heading as Letter 13 dated Jan. 21), received five shots and smallpox vaccination, and is awaiting deployment to a POE. Letter 13 (Jan. 21) and Letter 32 (Jan. 14) are from the same Greensboro location, creating a tight date cluster in January 1945. Confirmed as January 18, 1945.

pages.transcription:
Jan. 18, 45

Service Club
AAF ORD AND AAF RS NO. 5
GREENSBORO, N.C.

Dear Dad

How are things at home now, guess I had better call to see what is going on around there. Guess Jimmy will be leaving pretty soon too bad on his account. I sent 10 dollars more. Tell me soon if you receive it all. I will be sending about 40 or 50 in a couple of weeks. We are all done processing now. I caught 5 shots and a smallpox vaccination. And I believe the smallpox is taking. My arms were so damn sore I couldn't hardly carry my tray

to get my food. I am eating at Don Grant [illegible word], Mess hall No. 9 it is about ten times better than ever. I tell you when we get to a P.O.E. I will call home. I will know about when we'll ship out by one. I guess there is nothing as to where we are going. The project we was on has already left. Have only been to town once since my arrival, what a town. Good for — ask Jim he'll tell you. How is Grandma now hope she is a lot better by now. Hope all the automobiles are still running since my departure. Well Dad goodbye for now. Your loving son Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from the AAF Service Club in Greensboro, NC on January 18, 1945. He reports that he has completed military processing, received five shots and a smallpox vaccination that left his arms very sore, and is now awaiting deployment to a Port of Embarkation (POE). He mentions sending money home ($10 now, with $40-50 more to follow), notes that Jimmy will be leaving soon, asks about Grandma's health, and signs off warmly as 'your loving son Bob.'

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS (upper left):
PFC Robert E. Hanifen 17702603
APO Branch 2
GREENSBORO, N.C.

POSTMARK: GREENSBORO / JAN 27 / 2:30 PM / 1946 / N.C.

Upper right: Free [written in ink, military free mail]

ADDRESSEE:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Leo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Murphy
can stop leak
$1.50

[Penciled memo on back flap, likely written by recipient, unrelated to letter content.]

Jan. 25

Dear Dad
     Well I finally got to mail and I got three letters. Good deal. Thurs. we went on detail and Fri. we went on KP at 4:00 and at 6:00 that night. They pulled us and sent us to the warehouse for our clothing issue. We was just ready to leave and a Lt. grab us for more detail. We worked on salvage clothes. we could have anything we could get on. I got a few things and I am sending them home

one each to Johnny and Herby. The [two?] old pants belong to Don Picard. Have Johnny take them over to his when he gets a chance. But my coveralls up on [GI?] if want [use?] [or: if won't use them]. Pay those bills out of my money. We should leave by Tues. They say I sure hope so. What happened to that money order I sent [for?] Registration Pop? I don't [know?] — I put those license on but I think the envelope was already after I [sent?]. But didn't see it. I think. But look on the 41 Chev. Might [have?] got them wiped — could have got them messed up the [note?]. I didn't [mess up?] for.
Deal. So long for
     Love, Bob

letters.full_transcription:
Jan. 25

Dear Dad,
     Well I finally got to mail and I got three letters. Good deal. Thurs. we went on detail and Fri. we went on KP at 4:00 and at 6:00 that night. They pulled us and sent us to the warehouse for our clothing issue. We was just ready to leave and a Lt. grab us for more detail. We worked on salvage clothes. we could have anything we could get on. I got a few things and I am sending them home one each to Johnny and Herby. The [two?] old pants belong to Don Picard. Have Johnny take them over to his when he gets a chance. But my coveralls up on [GI?] if [won't use them?]. Pay those bills out of my money. We should leave by Tues. They say. I sure hope so. What happened to that money order I sent [for?] Registration Pop? I don't [know?] — I put those license on but I think the envelope was already [sealed?] after I [sent?]. But didn't see it. I think. But look on the 41 Chev. Might [have?] got them wiped — could have got them messed up the [note?]. I didn't [mess up?] for.
Deal. So long for

letters.summary: Written January 25, 1946, from Greensboro, N.C., PFC Bob Hanifen writes his father about recent military duties including detail work and KP, and being pulled to work on salvage clothing. He is sending clothing items home — one each for Johnny and Herby — and asks his father to return a pair of pants to Don Picard via Johnny. Bob expects to leave (likely for discharge or transfer) by Tuesday, and asks about a missing money order he sent for car registration, suggesting it may be on or near the '41 Chevy. The letter reflects the winding-down routine of postwar military service and Bob's ongoing involvement in family affairs from a distance.

metadata.jimmy_note: Jimmy/Jim mentioned as receiving more training — context suggests this is Bob's brother Jim (James Jr.), also in the Army.

pages.transcription:
Return address:
Pvt. Bob Hanifen 12110600
Sqd-I-370C-ABO
AAF-BT-C-BKS-605
Sheppard Fld., Texas

[Postmark: Wichita Falls, Texas, Jun 8, 1:30 PM, 1945]

[Upper right: 'Free' (military free postage)]

Addressed to:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Neo Way [street name uncertain]
Des Moines, Iowa

Dear Dad

     Well I am still here sweating it out. Boy was it hot to today must have been around 100°. We had detail the other day. I was a runner for Cap. Jackson's office must have walked 10 miles. But it is pretty nice place to work. An air condition building, ice water to drink and a coke machine and 15 good looking women to work with. A sweet girl came me my order I didn't mind it at all. Today was a rough day we move to another barracks and they broke up our flight we are all over the field now. More guys got orders tonight. But me no luck. Tomorrow we have a 7 miles hike and a couple of hours of P.T. boy will I be glad to get out of here. I suppose you are wondering about that letter from Wyo. It was from Cap.

East. He wanted to know how ADTB benefited us in the Army. It really helped a lot. I drank about 7 bottles of pop today and about 15 glasses of water and salt tablets, and I am still thirsty. Give a 5 cent for a good cold glass of water. Got a letter from Jimmy he didn't a whole lot. Say he is receiving more training. I think that all you do in the Army train and train some more. Well Dad goodby for now. I am so tired I can hardly sit, I hate to think of tomorrow. Goodby for now

Your Loving Son
     Bob

letters.summary: Private Bob Hanifen writes to his father from Sheppard Field, Texas in June 1945, complaining about the extreme heat (around 100°F) and grueling military life. He describes a pleasant detail running messages for Captain Jackson's air-conditioned office, but laments that his flight unit has been broken up and scattered across the field, and that he has yet to receive his orders. He mentions a letter from a Captain in Wyoming asking how ADTB training benefited soldiers, reports on his brother Jimmy also receiving more military training, and closes exhausted, dreading the next day's 7-mile hike and physical training.

metadata.from: Alvin, Carolyn, and girls (last name Zieller or similar, based on return address 'A.J. Zieller' — likely family friends of the Hanifens)
metadata.to: James E. Hanifen (Jim Hanifen Sr. or Jr. — based on address '904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa'; given the adult tone and references to 'Edith,' this is likely James Edward Hanifen Jr. or Sr.)
metadata.location_written_from: Laramie, Wyoming
metadata.location_sent_to: James E. Hanifen, 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.envelope_notes: Return address: A.J. Zieller, RR No. 2, Laramie, Wy. Stamp is a 3-cent purple 'Win the War' stamp. Small decorative ink stamp of what appears to be a boot or shoe on the lower left of envelope.
metadata.mood_tone: Warm, friendly, chatty — family friends catching up, with optimism about the war ending soon
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is generally readable but some words require careful interpretation. 'Zieller' on return address is uncertain. A few words on page 2 are slightly ambiguous.
metadata.historical_context: Written on August 14, 1945 — the very day Japan surrendered (V-J Day). The letter references news that it will 'soon be all over with the Japs,' which is historically significant as this was written on the day of Japan's surrender announcement. References to wartime help shortages and Fort Warren (a WWII military installation in Cheyenne, Wyoming).

pages.transcription:
Return address: A.J. Zieller / RR No. 2 / Laramie, Wy.

Addressed to: James E. Hanifen / 904 Keo Way / Des Moines, Iowa

Postmark: LARAMIE / AUG 14 / 1 30 PM / 1945 / WYO

Stamp: 3-cent purple 'Win the War' United States Postage stamp

Decorative ink stamp (lower left): appears to be a boot or shoe illustration

Dear Jim,

We received your letter saying you wouldn't be able to come out this summer and sure wish you could have come. We realize what the help shortage is like. Alvin didn't get his vacation this summer because they couldn't find anyone to take his place. So he just drew his vacation check and hope we can have some time off next summer. Both the kids and Alvin and I have had the summer flu but all are of [sic] now. Our baby is really growing up fast. She cut her

first tooth now. We hear from Bob & Jim quite often. Sure enjoy their letters. By the looks of the news it will soon be all over with the Japs. It can't be too soon. We went to Fort Collins, Colorado and picked our cherries to can for winter. Canned 52 pints. We're looking forward to meeting your friend from Fort Warren. Know he would enjoy a fishing trip with the gang. They've really had some good trips this summer. Alvin was sick so couldn't go this week end. He sure hated that. We haven't heard from Pauline in quite awhile. Did she come to Des Moines last month. That was her plans the last we heard. We bought the house here so Alvin has been busy doing some re-modeling. Won't have

much time for that now as snow will soon be flying. Hope this finds you well- and our best to Edith. Write us when you can.

Love
Alvin, Carolyn, Girls.

letters.full_transcription:

We received your letter saying you wouldn't be able to come out this summer and sure wish you could have come. We realize what the help shortage is like. Both the kids and Alvin and I have had the summer flu but all are of [sic] now. Our baby is really growing up fast. She cut her first tooth now. We hear from Bob & Jim quite often. Sure enjoy their letters. By the looks of the news it will soon be all over with the Japs. It can't be too soon. We went to Fort Collins, Colorado and picked our cherries to can for winter. Canned 52 pints. We're looking forward to meeting your friend from Fort Warren. Know he would enjoy a fishing trip with the gang. They've really had some good trips this summer. Alvin was sick so couldn't go this week end. He sure hated that. We haven't heard from Pauline in quite awhile. Did she come to Des Moines last month. That was her plans the last we heard. We bought the house here so Alvin has been busy doing some re-modeling. Won't have much time for that now as snow will soon be flying. Hope this finds you well- and our best to Edith. Write us when you can.

Love
Alvin, Carolyn, Girls.
letters.summary: Written on August 14, 1945 — the day of Japan's surrender — this letter from Alvin, Carolyn, and their daughters in Laramie, Wyoming to Jim Hanifen in Des Moines catches up on family news including Alvin missing his vacation due to wartime help shortages, the baby cutting her first tooth, picking and canning 52 pints of cherries in Fort Collins, and the family's recent home purchase and remodeling. The writers mention hearing from both Bob and Jim frequently, express optimism that the war with Japan will soon be over, and inquire about Pauline's possible visit to Des Moines. The letter closes with warm regards to Edith and an invitation to write.

metadata.to: James Edward Hanifen Sr. (Dad) — addressed to Hanifen Body & Paint Co.

metadata.location_sent_to: Hanifen Body & Paint Co., 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa
metadata.envelope_notes: 6-cent air mail stamp affixed. 'POSTAGE DUE, 2 CENTS' stamped in pink/magenta. A 2-cent postage due stamp affixed at lower right. Envelope back has notation: 'Air Mail' on left side, and penciled: 'Major Doc Barnes, c/o General APO 1009' — possibly reused or noted for another purpose. Top flap area has some handwriting partially visible.
metadata.mood_tone: Tired but upbeat; descriptive and chatty about basic training hardships; relieved to be nearly done with basic; hopeful about tech school assignment.
metadata.legibility_notes: Generally readable but some words are difficult due to cursive style and worn paper. A few words on page 2 (crossed-out scribble) and parts of page 4 are unclear. 'May' on page 4 could be a person's name or part of a phrase — context suggests it's a name (possibly a girlfriend or family member). The back of the envelope has additional writing that may be unrelated to this letter's mailing.
metadata.historical_context: WWII-era Army Air Forces basic training at Sheppard Field, Texas. Letter references firearms qualification, bivouac exercises, gas mask drills — standard WWII Army basic training activities. Bob anticipates assignment to Keesler Field for technical school, consistent with AAF training pipeline of the era.

pages.transcription:
RETURN ADDRESS (upper left):
Pvt. Bob Hanifen 17170600
Sqd I-3206-ABU
AAF-B-T-C-BKS-302
Sheppard Field, Texas

POSTMARK: Wichita Falls, TX, May 25 [year illegible], 1 PM

STAMPS: 6-cent Air Mail stamp (US, airplane design)
Postage Due 2-cent stamp affixed at lower right

PINK STAMP: POSTAGE DUE, 2 CENTS

ADDRESSED TO:
Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
904 Keo Way
Des Moines, Iowa

Air Mail [written across center]

Air Mail [written on left side]

[Penciled, right side]:
Major Doc Barnes
c/o General APO 1009

[Top flap, partially visible, largely illegible — appears to be bleed-through or additional notation]

Friday May 25
7:00 P.M.

Dear Dad,

  Sorry I haven't written sooner but we just got back from bivouac and out there we never had any time to write. We fired the 45 cal. pistol on Mon. for practice and Tues. we fired for scores. I made Marksman and it wasn't bad considering about 30 never qualified and only [about?] a few made Expert.

Tues. night we went on bivouac carrying all our equipment with us that we needed. That night I liked to froze to death. We drew guard that night and was pretty lucky had it from 10 to 11 we got up at 5:00 and had to be dressed and outside in formation in 15 min. Just try and put a pair of leggings on in the dark Air Corps style. About half the guys never took off there [their] clothes.

We had so little time we never washed in three days. Boy what a life. We walked constantly from difference [different] lectures on all kinds of warfare. Our last night we marched to a campsite and pitched pup tent and slept there for the night. It wasn't bad at all, only the ground was slightly rough. We was slightly late in fact 1 min. for a formation so we [got] two hours of extra drill that night from 7:00 to 9:00. We went on a 8 mile hike that morning and it was around 100° in the shade and no shade for miles. Only the [crossed-out word — illegible] catch was we had to wear gas mask. God did we ever get tired. In the afternoon we had C.T. and boy it about killed me. I got the watch band all right. I like it fine. I received my trunks and the

candy from B.A. and John in fine shape. Say today is my 31 day of basic and I only have 4 more days to go. I don't know when I will leave for tech school. Might be tomorrow or two months from now. Everybody has been leaving lately around on there [their] 33 day of basic. I hope I get a break and leave. I will undoubtedly go to Keesler Field. I would like to tell you so very much but I don't know how I can right now. I [am] listening for every development so I will as soon as I find them out. On bivouac we had to wear all of our equipment — cartridge belt and canteen and gas mask. God they got heavy. I received a letter from Kay and it was very nice.

Tom was up to see me but I was out and he left a note saying he [would] call again. Gee, wish I would [have] loved to have seen him. Tell May [or 'Mary'?] he called. Boy I am really getting tanned. I took a good shower and put on some clean clothes and I feel pretty good tonight. A good night sleep, I think, and I will be roaring to go. We might get R.P. Sun. I am praying we don't. Would liked to go swimming. Say hello to everybody for me. It looks like the Air Corps is going to the infantry that [the] way they anyway around here. Good night for now it [is] 9:30 and lights are going out. I write again and soon.

Your Loving Son,

Bobby

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from Sheppard Field, Texas on May 25 (likely 1944), just returned from a three-day bivouac. He describes qualifying as Marksman with the .45 pistol, enduring cold nights, guard duty, an 8-mile hike in 100-degree heat while wearing gas masks, and going three days without washing. He notes he is on his 31st day of basic training with only 4 more days left, and expects to be assigned to Keesler Field for tech school, though the timing is uncertain. He also mentions receiving packages from family, a letter from Kay, a missed visit from Tom, and closes just before lights-out at 9:30 PM.

metadata.to: Dad (James Edward Hanifen Sr.)

This fits the Greensboro timeline: Letter 32 (Jan. 14), Letter 72 (Jan. 23), Letter 68 (Jan. 25), Letter 23 (Jan. 29). pages.transcription:
[Back of envelope, handwritten in teal ink:]
Sunday Jan
Sand [|||] 2222

[Note: Text visible through envelope paper is bleed-through from letter contents, not additional writing on the envelope itself.]

[Army Air Forces letterhead with winged propeller insignia]

Jan. 23

Dear Dad
     You can forget about the pen I bought here at camp for 3 bucks at $4 pen if it is a Eversharp lifetime. Not a bad deal.
We are still lying around doing nothing at all. They say we should ship out by the 25 but I guess it was just another rumor. Went evening at YMCA last night with Picart and a couple buddies not much else to do. How we [doing?] around the shop now. Going good I hope. Well Dad goodby for now          over
          Love Bob

[Continuation, written on back/inside of folded letter — top of page:]
say have they ever found that stationery folder of mine if they do send the pictures in it.

[Remainder of page is bleed-through text from other pages, not additional content.]

[Bottom of page repeats envelope annotation in teal ink:]
Sunday Jan
Sand [|||] 2222

letters.full_transcription:
[Army Air Forces letterhead with winged propeller insignia]

Jan. 23

Dear Dad,
     You can forget about the pen I bought here at camp for 3 bucks at $4 pen if it is a Eversharp lifetime. Not a bad deal.
We are still lying around doing nothing at all. They say we should ship out by the 25 but I guess it was just another rumor. Went evening at YMCA last night with Picart and a couple buddies not much else to do. How we [doing?] around the shop now. Going good I hope. Well Dad goodby for now

[over]

say have they ever found that stationery folder of mine if they do send the pictures in it.

          Love Bob
letters.summary: Written on January 23rd on Army Air Forces stationery, Bob writes his father a brief, casual letter from camp. He mentions buying an Eversharp lifetime pen for 3 dollars (a good deal), notes that he and his fellow soldiers are idle waiting to ship out — a rumored departure date of the 25th apparently just another false rumor. He also asks whether the family has found his stationery folder and requests they send the pictures inside it.

5 April 1946

Dear Dad
        Just got to work so I thought I had better write a few lines before the Lt. got here. We don't have much work to do this month, but they are having a annual inspection by the Colonel of the big office. So we will be on the ball for a couple of days. The Lt. just got back yesterday from Switzerland. He said he had a sweet time up there. It costs something like 80 dollars for 2 weeks. I am sure going if I'd ever get the chance. We are off all day [today?] — some kind of a holiday for the Army I guess. I am going to Foggia Sunday to see Jack Buckley about my camera. I have sold my cigarette rations now for a [couple of?] weeks, and I have enough to [last?]

beside send home all of my base pay. I will enclose a check for 59 dollars. And in a couple of days another one 40 dollars. You see I have been across the pay table 99.43 and that is all the money I can send home till I am payed [sic] again. I want to know about those war bonds as soon as possible, because I think I am getting screwed. Have May take an inventory on the bonds. They should start in May 1945, and I think I should have 2 bonds for Nov. 1945 and 2 bonds for Dec. 1945. I would like to know. [Sending?] word. Hope everything is going good at the shop. I have gained a little weight. 153.7 lbs. getting large ain't I. Well Dad say hello to everyone for me.
        Lots of Love Your Son
                Bobby

[Photo/novelty image: A carnival-style cutout photograph showing two people — a man and a woman — with their real faces inserted into a painted illustration of a couple riding a tandem bicycle. The man is on the rear seat and the woman on the front. Both are dressed in formal/dress attire as painted. This appears to be a souvenir photo taken at a fairground or amusement attraction.]

[Black and white photograph: Three men standing in front of a large dark-colored automobile, outdoors. The man on the left is a young boy/teenager wearing a plaid shirt. The man in the center is wearing a U.S. Army uniform with garrison cap — likely Bob Hanifen. The man on the right is older, wearing a suit and fedora hat — likely the father, James Edward Hanifen Sr. Trees and a building are visible in the background. This appears to be a family photo, possibly taken during a furlough or visit home.]

letters.summary: Written on April 5, 1946, from PFC Bob Hanifen to his father in Des Moines, Iowa, while still stationed near Foggia, Italy in post-war occupation duty. Bob describes the slow work pace, an upcoming Colonel's inspection, his Lieutenant's recent trip to Switzerland, and his own plans to visit Jack Buckley in Foggia about his camera. He encloses checks totaling $99 from his base pay, explains the limits of what he can send home, and requests an inventory of his war bonds, suspecting he is owed bonds for November and December 1945. The letter also includes two photographs enclosed — a novelty tandem bicycle cutout photo and a family snapshot of Bob in uniform with what appear to be his father and younger brother.

Bob mentions getting out Friday night and being home for Easter, shipping equipment home, and possibly going to Kessler Field or Sheppard Field, Texas. Easter 1946 fell on April 21. Getting out on a Friday before Easter and being home in time is consistent with mid-to-late March 1946. The mention of a basketball tournament in the letter and the weather 'fine today' fits late March in the South/Midwest. Confirmed as March 20, 1946.

pages.transcription:
March 20, 1946
June: 1045

Dear Dad

I received the money but I haven't spent any of it yet. We got in to most of the games free except the semifinal and final and I had enough money to go to them. I will use the money to get my train ticket with it cost about 16 bucks. I think I will ride the train right into Des Moines that way you won't have to go all the way to Ames. The tournament was pretty nice seen a lot of good basketball especially the Championship game. Their baby is really nice. I will have to get up around 1:00 in the morning to see Jim so I don't I will go to bed that night till after the train comes goes. The weather is fine today don't even have to wear a field jacket. Last week snow was two left[?] deep. D.D.

test start [this?] so that it's almost all over. I have all my equipment ready to send home. If I am missing in a couple of handkerchiefs not bad. I am going to ship them [home?] to Des Moines so I won't be bother with them. I will insure them for 50 bucks apiece in case they lost. That what the Captain said to do. Don't take any chances. We are getting out Friday night so that means I will be home in time for Easter. You be home and we will go to Church together. Hope it is nice weather at home. I don't like rainy weather. We are either going to Kessler Field or Sheppard Field, Texas we don't for sure yet.

Well good by for now and don't work to hard.

Love,
Bobby

letters.summary: Written March 20, 1946, Bob (Bobby) Hanifen writes to his father from what appears to be a military base, reporting that he received money sent by his dad but hasn't spent it yet as they got into most of the basketball tournament games for free. He plans to use the money for his train ticket home to Des Moines (~$16), so his father won't need to travel to Ames to pick him up. Bob is excited to be getting out on Friday night and expects to be home in time for Easter, hoping they can go to church together. He is shipping his equipment home to Des Moines, insured for $50 per item on the Captain's advice, and notes they may be reassigned to either Kessler Field or Sheppard Field in Texas, though details are not yet confirmed.

Estimated September–October 1944.

pages.transcription:
Dear Dad

I got your letter and the money. I haven't been down to Alvin lately because I have restricted but I am going down this week end if they we get off in time. Got another letter from Jimmy Saturday and he says he hasn't been warm for two days. It was warm here yesterday and today. We haven't wore nothing but shirt sleeve for a couple of days. All men in the Air Corps saw the Captain Sat. and he explains to us what was going on. All 18 yr. before Dec. 1 will receive a 14 day furlough if they wish to after this term is finished. You can go to basic right from here if you want to, but everybody wants a furlough. The govt. pays our way from our home to Leavenworth, but we have to pay our way home from here. If we leave for basic training from here they will pay our way

there. I will make enough from Des Moines to Leavenworth to make up for my fare from Laramie to Des Moines. I called up Sunday about 7:15 and it took a few min to get thru and I am sorry I missed getting to talk to you. I hope Dean had a nice birthday. We have been playing basketball in P.T. lately and between platoon. Yesterday was the first day I seem to get the feel of the ball and I am made a few points. I have been feeling pretty good lately but the last few days my stomach has been about halfway upset but I still am walking. My roommate has been in the infirmary for about 3 days now and he just now got out. Tonsellites [tonsillitis] he says was the cause. If there is a raincoat at home with my serial No. in it H-0600 write and tell me or I will be charged for a new one. Well so long for now and find out about the raincoat if you can
Your Loving Son, Bob.

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from military training, likely at an Air Corps base near Alvin, Texas. He reports on furlough policies — soldiers under 18 before December 1 will receive a 14-day furlough after their current term — and discusses the travel pay arrangements between home, Des Moines, and Leavenworth. He mentions playing basketball in PT, a mild stomach upset, his roommate recovering from tonsillitis, and asks his father to check if a raincoat with his serial number H-0600 is at home, otherwise he'll be charged for a replacement.

pages.transcription:
[Return Address]
Mrs. Jan Hanifen
3614 Noble
Dallas, Texas

[Recipient Address]
P.-F.-C. J. R. Hanifen-55046284
Co. C439-Eng. Com. Bn.
A.P.O. 301 % P. M.
San Francisco, California

[Postmark] DALLAS, TEX. / NOV 8 / 6 PM / 1951
[Meter stamp] AIR MAIL 6¢
[Slogan] GIVE ENOUGH THROUGH YOUR COMMUNITY CHEST
[Red printed label on envelope face] AIR MAIL

[Header in red, printed/typed]
PLEASE ADDRESS YOUR NEXT LETTER JUST TO ME,
THEN I CAN FUSS AT BOB FOR OPENING MY MAIL.

November 8, 1951

P. F. C. J. R. Hanifen 55046284
Co C439 Eng Co. Bn
APO 301 % P. M.
San Francisco, California

Dear Johnnie,

      I am so sorry I havn't written you before, but Bob never tells me when he is writing you a letter, so I don't have a chance to write you anything.

      You better hurry up and come home, so I can get you a date with some of my girl-friends. I've told a lot of them about you, and they say they would like to go with you.

      Your brother, Jim, just wrote us a letter yesterday, and told us to come to Des Moines, so he could manage your dad's shop at a buck 78 an hour, but you know Jimmy, your dad's in Colorado elk hunting, and I'm sure he doesn't know a thing about it.

      I'm working at an insurance company for a bunch of claim adjusters, and half of them are from Des Moines. The name of it is United States Fidelity and Guaranty Co. The claims superintendent, Mr. Chittenden, said he sent a lot of business to the shop when he was in Des Moines, but Bob said he had never heard of them. I'm trying to get a new job, though, cause I'm not making enough money.

      Bob heard about a good deal. If some general motors dealer will recommend you, you can go to school in Michigan, and, then they will send you out to some plant, anywhere in the U. S. Bob is going to try to get the guy he is working for to recommend him. It would really be a good deal if he could get on.

      If you want me to get one of my girl-friends to write you, I will, cause I know a slew of them that would like to, (write, that is)

      Did that deal about getting your high school diploma work out all right? I sure hope it did, cause you can get a lot better job, if you have it.

      Well, I guess I better close for now, some queer is fixing to dictate me some letters.

                              Be good and take care of yourself,

                                          Love, Your sister, ( almost)

                                          Jan Hanifen
                                          Jan

P. S.  I will write you lots more in the future.

[Printed header]
DEBIT OR CREDIT
MEMORANDUM

[Handwritten date] April 3     195[?]

[Printed form fields: oval blank box, 'We to-day _____ your account as follows:', columns for EXPLANATION and AMOUNT]

Dear John

      Pardon the paper I am at work and decided to drop you a line. By the way you never did tell me whether you got your Christmas package or that recording of my voice to you. What a good deal the old man and Jim got for you the 51 [Ford?] they was [were] sure looking cars. I wish I had one myself the Chrysler is okay but it is not worth a thing. I sure hope you get home soon. Tom [?] you can home [come home?] this summer. Jan and I went last Sunday to see some modified stock cars here in Dallas at [Love?] Bowl and they weren't worth a damn. I don't think I saw a fender finished in the whole afternoon. They tell me Jim it is beginning to talk and Rosemary is trying to walk a little bit. I bet those kids are plenty cute. I'll [I'm?] in Des Moines around Christmas I figure to hoping you will be there too.

Love Your Brother [Bob?]

[Printed footer]
THE TEXAS COMPANY
Approved _____ By _____

letters.summary: This envelope contains two letters to John (Johnnie) Hanifen, who is serving in the U.S. Army overseas during the Korean War era. The first letter, dated November 8, 1951, is a warm and humorous typed letter from Jan Hanifen (Bob's wife or fiancée), who playfully asks John to address future letters to her alone so Bob will stop opening her mail; she shares family news including Jim's scheme to manage the family shop, her job at an insurance company, Bob's General Motors school opportunity, and offers to set John up with girlfriends. The second letter, written by Bob on Texas Company office memorandum paper dated April 3 (year partially illegible), mentions a Christmas package and voice recording sent to John, a '51 Ford the family bought, attending a stock car race in Dallas, and news that young children (likely Jim Jr.'s children) are beginning to talk and walk. Together the letters paint a lively picture of the Dallas branch of the Hanifen family during the early 1950s while John was stationed abroad.

• Recreation Center No. 1 • Chanute Field, Illinois •

Tues 24.

Dear Dad

    Had a swell weekend, went to Chic. with my Buddie Hanken and we had a fine time. Hitch hiked up and got there at 9:00 AM. Went over to Cary [Gary] and went swimming there. Beverly Fenton never knew we coming so she went to Chic. Hank and me couldn't find a locker to put our clothes in so we had to find somebody to watch them. They were pretty cute too. Had to give them a few swimming lessons but it wasn't hard either. Went back to Chic. about

about 6:15 eat supper and then Hank and I went down town, on the way down we ask two girls on the train if they were Cadet nurses and they said no, they were Spars. Had a nice time with them. Every there nit [?] cases to the station, but they had to catch a train to Cleveland in about 10 min. so we left them. Got in town and had a swell time. Seen [saw] two cute numbers but a couple of sailors were on the beam but they couldn't seem to land them. We walked up and said, what does a guy do in Chic. and they didn't know

[Duplicate of page 2 — same content, likely a scan of the reverse side or duplicate scan]

fell flat on his rump. Scared me half to death. They wasn't too bad. I was supposed to see the Dentist today but couldn't make it. I guess tomorrow I can though. Want to be in good shape when I come home. I can get a ride [clear?] thru I think so it won't be too bad. I am inclosing [enclosing] the picture you sent me. Well goodby for now.

Love

letters.full_transcription:
• Recreation Center No. 1 • Chanute Field, Illinois •

Tues 24.

Dear Dad

    Had a swell weekend, went to Chic. with my Buddie Hanken and we had a fine time. Hitch hiked up and got there at 9:00 AM. Went over to Cary [Gary] and went swimming there. Beverly Fenton never knew we coming so she went to Chic. Hank and me couldn't find a locker to put our clothes in so we had to find somebody to watch them. They were pretty cute too. Had to give them a few swimming lessons but it wasn't hard either. Went back to Chic. about 6:15 eat supper and then Hank and I went down town, on the way down we ask two girls on the train if they were Cadet nurses and they said no, they were Spars. Had a nice time with them. Every there nit [?] cases to the station, but they had to catch a train to Cleveland in about 10 min. so we left them. Got in town and had a swell time. Seen two cute numbers but a couple of sailors were on the beam but they couldn't seem to land them. We walked up and said, what does a guy do in Chic. and they didn't know — fell flat on his rump. Scared me half to death. They wasn't too bad. I was supposed to see the Dentist today but couldn't make it. I guess tomorrow I can though. Want to be in good shape when I come home. I can get a ride [clear?] thru I think so it won't be too bad. I am inclosing the picture you sent me. Well goodby for now.

Love
    Bob
letters.summary: Written Tuesday July 24, 1945, from Chanute Field, Illinois, Bob Hanifen recounts a lively weekend pass to Chicago with his buddy Hank (Hanken). They hitchhiked to Chicago, went swimming at Gary (Indiana), chatted up a pair of SPARS (Coast Guard women's reservists) on the train, and had an entertaining evening on the town where they apparently outcompeted some sailors in meeting girls. Bob closes by mentioning a missed dentist appointment and his plans to come home soon, and he returns a picture his father had sent him.

metadata.legibility_notes: Heavy bleed-through from the reverse side of the pages makes several words difficult to read. Some street address on envelope unclear — 'Heo Way' likely 'Leo Way.' The date written as 'Mon. 1900' appears to be a shorthand notation; the postmark confirms June 25, 1945.
metadata.historical_context: Written near the end of WWII (June 1945). Bob mentions training at Chanute Field (an Army Air Forces technical training base), flying in a C-47 transport, the Mississippi River flooding near St. Louis, and sheet metal work needed to repair shot-up B-29s — all consistent with late-war AAF technical training activities.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Letter is dated 'Mon. 1900' from Chanute Field Service Club, addressed to Aunt Pauline and Gran. Bob mentions having KP from 2:30 AM to 1:00 PM today (Monday), the C-47 flight to St. Louis 'yesterday' (Sunday), and anticipating KP again tomorrow (Tuesday). The C-47 flight is the same event described in Letters 53 (Monday flight account) and 54 (Sunday night before). Therefore this Monday is the day of the flight return, approximately June 25, 1945. Confirmed as approximately June 25, 1945.

pages.transcription:
• Service Club • Chanute Field, Illinois •

                                                          Mon. 1900
Dear Aunt Pauline & Gran

        Just got back from chow after sleeping all afternoon I won't be too tired so I stay a little later tonight in order so that I get to sleep. Had K.P. from 2:30 in the morn. till 1:00 this afternoon so at that I worked a little bit today. I was pretty sick this morn. Eat a couple of lead pancakes and 3 peaches and a couple bottles of milk. I have K.P. again tomorrow but it from 9:00 to 7:00 not too bad. Might get a three day and if I do I am going to go Chicago and see Aunt Bonnie I think. It has been hot all week but today it rained and it is rather chilly out. Ran into a kid in my Graduation class

a Lincoln High. He is here taking electronics. Had a swell airplane ride yesterday in a C-47 transport flew down to St. Louis and back only took two hrs. and a half both way. It sure seemed funny flying around at 9000 ft. the Miss. is flooding around St. Louis. Been in a big river. I am going to be here at least 16 weeks so that means sometime around the first of Nov. I will get a 15 day delay in route and P.O.E. They need Sheet Metal Workers bad to fix up those shot up B-29s.

Well goodby for now and I will write again soon.
        Your Loving Nephew
                Bob

letters.summary: Bob Hanifen writes to his Aunt Pauline and grandmother from the Service Club at Chanute Field, Illinois on June 25, 1945. He describes exhausting KP duty, a C-47 airplane ride down to St. Louis and back where he observed the flooding Mississippi River, and his training as a sheet metal worker needed to repair battle-damaged B-29s. He anticipates being at Chanute Field for at least 16 more weeks before receiving a 15-day delay en route to a port of embarkation, and mentions possibly visiting Aunt Bonnie in Chicago if he gets a three-day pass.

metadata.historical_context: References to a flight physical, gunnery training, and a furlough strongly suggest a WWII-era military context, consistent with Bob's service during World War II. The mention of a 'Class A pass' is military terminology for an authorized pass off-base.
metadata.date_estimation_reasoning: Bob mentions taking a flight physical, a Class A pass, 20 days remaining before 7 weeks of gunnery training, and a furlough after gunnery. He is sunbathing and received a letter from Aunt Bonnie and Kay. The mention of gunnery training and a furlough matches Bob's projected training timeline described in Letter 47 (May 20, 1946 from Sheppard Field: '18 weeks mechanic school then 6 weeks gunnery') — but since that is 1946 and this letter's tone and context (class A pass into 'the Big City') matches early training, this could be either the 1945 or 1946 Sheppard Field period. Given the flight physical (Letter 43, May 1945, also mentions upcoming flight physical) and the 20-days-before-gunnery timeline, this may be from Sheppard Field, spring 1945. Estimated May 1945, possibly spring 1946.

letters.summary: In this letter, Bob writes to his father (Dad) from what appears to be a military base during WWII. He mentions taking his flight physical, enjoying a sunbath, and looking forward to a Class A pass into the city with a companion (Ollert). He reports receiving a letter from Aunt Bonnie and Kay, asks if there is any news from Jimmy (his brother), and requests that his father send one of his photos left at home to Aunt Bonnie and Kay, as he had promised them one. He closes by noting he has about 20 days remaining before seven weeks of gunnery training, after which he anticipates a furlough.

Just a few lines to let you know I am all right. The work is fine and I am doing all right so far. The Lt. got no [jeep?] to [use?] so [everything is?] fine [so?] far. The times seemed to be going [awfully?] [so though?], but it always does [at first?].

We got a new shipment of men here. 60 of them. We didn't [exactly?] appreciate them as we [don't?] have [enough?] work for the [guys here?] [already?]. But we probably appreciate them later on. Our trip to [MacFarland?] was cancelled [to?] [due to?] conditions which arose, so [we'll?] probably [go?] [next?] week, the Lt. says [we'll?]

[be?] gone for 3 days or more. Well that's all [I'll?] write again tomorrow, only a longer letter. But a [bunch?] of guys are waiting [for me?] to go back to camp. This [illegible] [has?] [me?] [gonna?] put down. It's only 9:30 [&?] [I?] am sleepy already.

[My love to?] everybody.

[Lots?] of love Your Son
    Bobby

letters.summary: This is a brief letter from Bob (Robert C. Hanifen), written on American Red Cross stationery from his overseas military posting (APO 528), to his father in Des Moines. Bob reassures his father he is well, mentions a new shipment of 60 men to his unit, and notes that a planned trip was cancelled due to unspecified conditions. He keeps the letter short because he is tired (it is only 9:30 but he is already sleepy) and his fellow soldiers are waiting to return to camp. Enclosed with the letter are a Western Union advertisement and a receipt dated March 28, 1945, showing that his father (J.E. Hanifen of Des Moines) sent Bob $10 by telegraphic money order to Laramie, Wyoming.

Free
