Hanifen Body & Paint Co.
984 Keo Way

Des Moines, Iowa

U. S. ARMY AIR FORCES

Dear Dad

  I am back at Camp again had a swell weekin. Hitch Hiked up to Chicago. The right rear tire on the Chev. is junk and the spare isn't a lot better. All it does is hold up rear end. I am going down to the ration board now to see about a new ones. they are pretty good about it on the field, so that the only I can figure out how to get

the car home unless you send a tire by mail. Write and tell me what to do. only took 16 gal. of gas and two gts [qts?] of oil. Very good mirage [mileage?]. Have you heard from Jimmy yet, sure hope so. Well Dad goodby for now. Everything is going well
          Your Loving Son
               Bob

letters.summary: Bob writes to his father from Chanute Field, Illinois in August 1945, reporting that he had a good weekend and hitchhiked up to Chicago. He explains that the rear tire on the family Chevrolet is worn out and the spare is barely usable, and that he is going to the ration board to try to get new tires; he asks his father to write and advise whether he should send a tire by mail. Bob also notes the car got good gas mileage (16 gallons and 2 quarts of oil for the trip) and inquires whether his father has heard from his brother Jimmy.

metadata.envelope_notes: This is a Gasoline Ration Book Envelope (not a mailed letter). Front cover reads 'Gasoline Ration Book Envelope' with handwritten annotation '41 Ply' (1941 Plymouth). Back reads: 'Conserve Your Car... have dented, scratched or rusted areas refinished—damaged sections repaired. For this service, bring your car to— HANIFEN BODY & PAINT CO. 904 Keo Way, Des Moines, Iowa. Compliments of MINNESOTA MINING & MFG. CO., St. Paul, Minn.' Handwritten on back: '41 Ply' and signature 'James E. Hanifen 4/8/22-3'. Inside pocket has handwritten notation '41 Ply - 37'. The ration book inside is OPA Form R-525C, 'A Basic Mileage Ration', United States of America, Office of Price Administration. Registered owner: J. E. Hanifen, 424 Virginia, Des Moines, Iowa. License No. 77-33990 (Iowa). Year Model and Make: Ply[mouth] 41 [Iowa?]. Serial number on coupons: 584736. Several 'A17' mileage coupons visible; some coupons appear to have been used/torn. Back of ration book has 'Certification of Bookholder' signed by James E. Hanifen, address 424, Virginia Ave. Notice instructs holder to write car license number and state on each coupon.
metadata.mood_tone: Administrative/official — a wartime rationing document, not a personal letter
metadata.legibility_notes: Handwriting is fairly clear. The date on back '4/8/22-3' is somewhat ambiguous — may refer to a date (April 8) combined with a model year notation, or another reference.
metadata.historical_context: WWII-era gasoline rationing. The Office of Price Administration (OPA) issued 'A' Basic Mileage Ration books to civilian car owners, limiting gasoline consumption as part of the wartime effort. The 'A' card was the lowest-priority ration, allotting approximately 3-4 gallons per week for non-essential driving. The envelope was produced and distributed by Hanifen Body & Paint Co. as a promotional item, compliments of Minnesota Mining & Manufacturing Co. (3M).

pages.transcription:
Gasoline Ration Book Envelope
[handwritten:] 41 Ply

DIRECTIONS: Open book and place back cover and pages of coupons in pocket of envelope, except the top cover and page in current use. See cut. This prevents tearing off coupons from more than one page at a time.

[Inside of folded envelope showing two pockets. Handwritten notation in center fold:]
41 Ply - 37

[Bottom printed text:]
PATENT PENDING
U. S. ENVELOPE CO.

IMPORTANT INSTRUCTIONS
1. Coupons can be used only in connection with the vehicle described on the front cover. Detached coupons are VOID.
2. If you stop using your car, this book and all unused coupons must be surrendered to your Board within 5 days.
3. If you sell your car, this book and all unused coupons must be surrendered to your Board. The purchaser will not be issued a gasoline ration unless he presents the receipt which you receive at the time of such surrender.
☆ OPO

[Right side:]
A  BASIC MILEAGE RATION
UNITED STATES OF AMERICA
OFFICE OF PRICE ADMINISTRATION

NAME OF REGISTERED OWNER
J. E. Hanifen

ADDRESS—NUMBER AND STREET

Gasoline Ration Book Envelope
[handwritten:] 41 Ply
DIRECTIONS: Open book and place back cover and pages of coupons in pocket of envelope, except the top cover and page in current use. See cut. This prevents tearing off coupons from more than one page at a time.

INSIDE:
[Handwritten:] 41 Ply - 37
PATENT PENDING / U. S. ENVELOPE CO.

RATION BOOK (FRONT):
IMPORTANT INSTRUCTIONS
1. Coupons can be used only in connection with the vehicle described on the front cover. Detached coupons are VOID.
2. If you stop using your car, this book and all unused coupons must be surrendered to your Board within 5 days.
3. If you sell your car, this book and all unused coupons must be surrendered to your Board. The purchaser will not be issued a gasoline ration unless he presents the receipt which you receive at the time of such surrender.

A BASIC MILEAGE RATION
UNITED STATES OF AMERICA / OFFICE OF PRICE ADMINISTRATION

letters.summary: This item is not a personal letter but a WWII-era gasoline ration book and its envelope, belonging to James E. Hanifen Sr. of 424 Virginia Ave., Des Moines, Iowa. The 'A Basic Mileage Ration' book (OPA Form R-525C) was issued for his 1941 Plymouth (Iowa license 77-33990) and contains several unused 'A17' mileage coupons with serial number 584736. The envelope was a promotional item from Hanifen Body & Paint Co. (904 Keo Way, Des Moines), distributed compliments of Minnesota Mining & Manufacturing Co. (3M), suggesting James Sr. owned or operated an auto body shop — consistent with the family business context. This document provides important evidence of the Hanifen family's wartime home-front experience and their connection to the automobile repair trade in Des Moines.

pages.transcription:
OPERATOR'S LICENSE  No.
State of Iowa, Dept. of Public Safety

NOTICE. This stub to be torn off by the court and mailed to Operator's License Div., Dept. of Public Safety, if convicted of a motor vehicle law violation, as provided in Sec. 238, Chapter 134.

FIRST OFFENSE

--- FOLD HERE ---

STATE OF IOWA
1939-1941
OPERATOR'S No.  4455

Applicant's picture will appear on his [license]

---

The Department of Public Safety of the State of Iowa hereby acknowledges receipt from  James Edward Hanifen
of application and Fifty Cents (50c) fee for renewal of 1939-41 Iowa Operator's License Number  4455  which expires July 5, 1941, and hereby extends to said applicant permission to operate a motor vehicle while the department is completing its investigation and determination of all facts relative to such applicant's right to receive an operator's license, subject however to any restrictions imposed on the above numbered 1939-41 license. For the purpose of restrictions and identification said 1939-41 license shall be considered a part hereof and at all times must be kept herewith.

This temporary permit is issued under authority of Section 5013.07, Code of Iowa, 1939, and must be in the immediate possession of said applicant while operating a motor vehicle after July 5, 1941, until receipt of new license. It shall be invalid when such applicant's renewal license has been issued or for good cause has been refused, and in any event shall be invalid after September 15, 1941.

KARL W. FISCHER
COMMISSIONER OF PUBLIC SAFETY

Date  6-20-41

County  Polk        By  [illegible signature]
                         Department Representative

(Over)

letters.summary: This document (letter ID 83) is not a personal letter but rather the 1939-1941 Iowa Operator's License (driver's license) belonging to James E. Hanifen Sr., father of Bob, Jim, and John Hanifen. The license shows he lived at 424 Virginia, Des Moines, Iowa, and was a 'Shop Proprietier' [sic], approximately 5'9", 175 lbs, with light hair and gray eyes. Accompanying the license is a temporary renewal permit dated June 20, 1941, signed by Commissioner of Public Safety Karl W. Fischer, extending his driving privileges through September 15, 1941, while his new license was being processed. A partially legible note fragment overlaps the inside image, apparently discussing the Temporary Permit's validity for Iowa residents.

metadata.from: Bob Hanifen (signed 'Bobby') — the sender signs off 'Keep this for me. Bobby' at the bottom of the voyage log page
metadata.to: Family (likely parents/siblings back home)
metadata.date: February 1946 (based on voyage dates: departed New York 8 February, arrived Naples 19 February)
metadata.location_written_from: Aboard USNS/SS NYU Victory (troopship), en route New York to Naples, Italy via Casablanca
metadata.envelope_notes: No envelope visible; this appears to be a printed/mimeographed ship's newspaper ('The Victory NYUS') and a voyage log document sent home
topics: troop transport voyage from New York to Naples
metadata.mood_tone: Humorous and lighthearted; the ship's newspaper column is breezy and comedic, poking fun at lost items and shipboard life; the voyage log is matter-of-fact and informational; Bob's handwritten note is brief but affectionate
metadata.legibility_notes: Pages are aged with brown staining/foxing and some burn/water damage at edges obscuring text, particularly in the right column of page 1 and portions of the voyage log. The mimeographed typeface is somewhat faded and uneven. Several words required guessing.
metadata.historical_context: This document dates from early 1946, shortly after WWII ended. Bob appears to be returning to or traveling to Europe (Naples) as part of post-war military operations or repatriation. The ship 'NYU Victory' (SS William S. Victory class) was a troop transport. The stop at Casablanca references the famous Moroccan port. The voyage log records a 4,331-mile journey from New York to Naples taking 11 days, 5 hours, 35 minutes.

pages.transcription:
Page 4                    VICTORY    NYUS                    Februa[ry 1946]

TOPSIDE                    WITH CRAIG

    Well, by now the punch marks in our mess cards are well up in their thirties. Beginning to look as though there were more hole-space than paper. That is, if we have been present at every meal the regulation number of times. Some cards are minus punch-marks for one reason or another, most of which happened a week ago, when the owners were heavily burdened with 'heave-y' burdens. However, having finally gotten our sea-legs --- (pardon me, while I seek the lee rail), it is now not a question of how [long we] can hold what we eat as how long until the next time we eat.

    Have been astonished, surprised and appalled at the number of articles reported missing by their individual owners. Seems as though Separation were not confined to all the Joes going west. A guy would need better than 20-20 vision to keep track of his worldly goods. From what we've heard noised aboard by the complaining parties and soon advertised in the NYUSpaper of which we are a proud as well as humble member, if all the goods that have been lost, neglected, forgotten, left behind, dropped out of pockets during various maneuvers, such as when the seat was trying to sit on the head, were collected under the hand of one person, he could do a nice business, either in the curio or second-hand line. And that, mind you, is without mentioning several other ways that a guy be separated from his goods. And the rewards that are offered! Compartment 30 is away out in front there!

    Let us continue, getting personal. I had a pen-knife. Not much as pen knives go; rather an ordinary, usual, nonplace and unspectacular item, or [min-?] inal cost, if I remember correctly. But

[RIGHT COLUMN:]

of Walker's wallet! What a guy could do in Naples with that. Think of all the conversation he could effect! M'm, m'm!!

    Speaking of Ali Baba, how did you make out at Casablanca? As well as at the spot by the same name Stateside? We'd give anything to see the financial page of the Casablanca Courier, Monday's edition. But there was a sudden collar in trading prices. Hope that all you fellows got what you thought you were getting. Itchy-koo to you! Old Ali on the starboard side had only one of the famous forty with him, but he did bett[er] than he ever did with the thirty nine [counterparts?]! See you in Naples, [thanks?]!

    - NYUS - - -

[CARTOON by Sanson: illustration of figures in a scene, with caption:]
"... and this one can cook!"

[Bottom right:]
THE VICTORY NYUS
Published daily aboard ssNYU Victo[ry]

[LEFT COLUMN — continuation of 'Topside with Craig':]

inal cost, if I remember correctly. But if I had the juice from all the oranges it has pooled, I could put this ship in[to] — and have plenty left over for the life boats. And as for the peanut cans I've opened with it— alas and alack, if all that tin were gold I could retire on the interest of what I owe. Oh where, oh where has my penknife gone? . . See what I mean? It's loss has demented me. I did depend on that implement. I've got some leads, as Perry Mason might say: either (a) it committed a puncture of the eighth commandment or (b) there is a hole in the bottom of the ship that looks only out, or (c) our favorite nocturnal haunter, erstwhile reported in these columns, thought it was a bone — from the handle, you know. By the way, if the present possessor of a brown handled pen knife happens to cut off his thumb, then gets rooked trading it to one of Ali Baba's forty thieves, please don't hesitate to think he's been cursed. Oh, for the fifty buck reward offered for the return

[RIGHT COLUMN — masthead/staff listing:]

THE VICTORY NYUS
Published daily aboard ssNYU Victory as an official news service for free distribution among United States Army personnel.

- - - IMPRINT - - -

Pfc John Benson                Editor
Pfc Stanley M. Cohen           Sports
Pvt William L. Cowley          Writer
Pfc Omer Syr                   Writer
Pfc Rob Endicott               Writer
Pfc Joseph Craig               Writer
Pfc Lloyd George               Mimeograph
Pfc Philip Wahl                Writer
Cpl Richard Brahm              Cartoonist
Pfc Jay Sellick                Cartoonist
Pfc Roy B. Lipe Jr.            Typist
Cpl Donald A. Lindor           Mimeograph
Pfc George Uselman             Mimeograph
Pfc Apollinar M. Gonzalez      Mimeograph
Cpl Daniel Johnson             Mimeograph
Cpl Don Blake                  Mimeograph
Pfc Carl O. Blumhagen          Mimeograph
Pvt Jerry Barth                Mimeograph

[Voyage log entries, with map overlay:]

5.  13 February, noon: Lat. 40.49N, Long. 54.38W. Distance, 1731 miles. Time at sea, 4-07-35. Speed, 17.17 knots. Partly cloudy, Northwesterly sea and swell changing to southeasterly.

6.  14 February, noon: Lat. 39.37N, Long. 25.65W. Distance, 2187 miles. Time at sea, 5-07-35. Speed, 16.9 knots. Scattered clouds, visibility good, Southeasterly sea and swells. Ship easy.

7.  15 February, noon: Lat. 37.16[N], Long. 17.56W. Distance, 2587 miles. Time at sea, 6-06-35. Speed, 17.4 knots. Scattered clouds, visibility good. East by Northerly sea and swells. Ship easy.

8.  16 February, noon: Lat. 34.37N, Long. 10.28W. Distance, 2981 miles. Time at sea, 7-05-35. Speed, 17.1 knots. Clear, scattered clouds. Northeasterly sea and swells.

ARRIVED CASABLANCA, 16 February, 2115 hours. Distance, dock to dock, 3[1]48 miles. Average speed, 17.[?]

DEPARTED CASABLANCA 1[7 February, at] [?] hours. Clear, visibility good, slight[ly] North-northeasterly sea and swells.

[10.]  18 February, noon: Lat. 36.24N, Long. [0?]. Distance, via Casablanca, 3521 mil[es]. Time at sea, 0-13-15. Speed, 17.8 knots. Clear, scattered clouds, smooth Easterly sea.

11.  19 February, noon: Lat. 38.10N, Long. 06.30E. Distance, 3951 miles. Time at sea, 1-19-15. Speed, 17.9 knots. Clear, scattered clouds, moderate Easterly sea and swells. Ship rolling slightly.

ARRIVAL, NAPLES, ITALY: 19 February, 1300 hours.
DISTANCE FROM NEW YORK: 4331 MILES. TOTAL LENGTH OF VOYAGE, 11 days, 5 hours, 35 minutes. TOTAL ACTUAL TIME AT SEA: 10 days, 10 hours, 5 minutes.

[Handwritten note:]
Keep this for me.

Bobby

SENTIMENTAL JOURNEY

GI ARRANGEMENT, #6, PART I

Departure from New York [8] February, at 0125 hours.

1.  9 February, noon: Lat. 40.07N, 69.54W. Distance, 181 miles. Time [at sea,] [?] hrs, 35 m[in]. Speed, 17 knots. Visibility good, cloudy, slight southerly sea.

2.  10 February, noon: Lat. 40.49N, Long. 6[3]15[W]. Distance from New York, 576 miles. Time at sea, 1-09-35. Speed, 17.17 knots. Cloudy, visibility good, Northwesterly sea, ship easy.

3.  11 February, noon: Lat. 41.41N, Long. 52.[6?]6[W]. Distance, 976 miles. Time at sea: 2-06-35. Speed, 17.4 knots. Cloudy, visibility good, Northerly sea and swell. Ship easy.

4.  12 February, noon: Lat. 42.01N, Long. 43.16W. Distance, 1300 miles. Time at sea, 3-08-35. Speed, 17.1 knots. Partly cloudy, visibility good. North[w]erly to northwesterly sea and [swells?]. Ship easy.

5.  13 February, noon: Lat. 40.49N, Long. 54.38W. Distance, 1731 miles. Time at sea, 4-07-35. Speed, 17.17 knots. Partly cloudy, Northwesterly sea and swell changing to southeasterly.

[Partial entry visible for item 6 (cut off at bottom):]
6.  14 Februar[y...]

[RIGHT SIDE — partial entries:]
7.  15 February, noon: Lat. 37.16[N], Long. 17.56W. Distance, 2587 miles. Time at sea, 6-06-35[.]

6.  14 February, noon: Lat. 39.37N, Long. 25.65W. Distance, 2187 miles. Time at sea, 5-07-35. Speed, 16.9 knots. Scattered clouds, visibility good, Southeasterly sea and swells. Ship easy.

7.  15 February, noon: Lat. 37.16[N], Long. 17.56W. Distance, 2587 miles. Time at sea, 6-06-35. Speed, 17.4 knots. Scattered clouds, visibility good. East by Northerly sea and swells. Ship easy.

8.  16 February, noon: Lat. 34.37N, Long. 10.28W. Distance, 2981 miles. Time at sea, 7-05-35. Speed, 17.1 knots. Clear, scattered clouds. Northeasterly sea and swells.

ARRIVED CASABLANCA, 16 February, 2115 hours. Distance, dock to dock, 3[1]48 miles. Average speed, 17.[?]

DEPARTED CASABLANCA 1[7 February, at] [?] hours. Clear, visibility good, slight[ly] North-northeasterly sea and swells.

[10.]  18 February, noon: Lat. 36.24N, Long. [0?]. Distance, via Casablanca, 3521 mil[es]. Time at sea, 0-13-15. Speed, 17.8 knots. Clear, scattered clouds, smooth Easterly sea.

11.  19 February, noon: Lat. 38.10N, Long. 06.30E. Distance, 3951 miles. Time at sea, 1-19-15. Speed, 17.9 knots. Clear, scattered clouds, moderate Easterly sea and swells. Ship rolling slightly.

ARRIVAL, NAPLES, ITALY: 19 February, 1300 hours.
DISTANCE FROM NEW YORK: 4331 MILES. TOTAL LENGTH OF VOYAGE, 11 days, 5 hours, 35 minutes. TOTAL ACTUAL TIME AT SEA: 10 days, 10 hours, 5 minutes.

Keep this for me.

Bobby
letters.summary: This letter (item 84) consists of pages from 'The Victory NYUS,' the mimeographed daily newspaper published aboard the troopship SS NYU Victory, along with a detailed voyage log titled 'Sentimental Journey — GI Arrangement #6, Part I.' The newspaper includes a humorous column 'Topside with Craig' about lost personal items (especially a penknife), the Casablanca stopover, and shipboard life, along with a cartoon and the full staff masthead. The voyage log meticulously records daily noon positions, distances, speeds, and weather from the ship's departure from New York on 8 February 1946 through its arrival in Naples, Italy on 19 February 1946 — a journey of 4,331 miles in just over 11 days. Bob Hanifen (signing as 'Bobby') added a handwritten note asking his family to 'Keep this for me,' indicating he mailed these printed materials home as a keepsake of his wartime/post-war voyage to Europe.
